topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT823 - SECURITY AND PRIVACY IN CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Privacy in CPS: Privacy Principles and Concerns, Data Collection, Handling, and Storage in CPS, Privacy Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Privacy in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) — Formal Definition

**Privacy in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** refers to the disciplined engineering and governance of personally identifiable, sensitive, and contextual information generated, transmitted, processed, and stored across the integrated cyber (computation, communication) and physical (sensors, actuators, embedded devices) layers of a system — to ensure that individuals, organizations, and infrastructure operators retain control over *what* data is collected, *how* it is used, *with whom* it is shared, and *for how long* it is retained.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Cyber-Physical System** is an engineered system in which computational algorithms (the "cyber" component) are tightly coupled with physical entities (the "physical" component) through sensors, actuators, and communication networks (e.g., IoT, Industrial Control Systems, smart grids, autonomous vehicles, medical devices, smart cities).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Privacy in CPS is *not* just cybersecurity. Security protects **systems from unauthorized access**; privacy protects **individuals from unauthorized data use**. A secure CPS can still violate privacy if data is lawfully collected but misused.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Home Theatre"

Imagine a **smart home theatre system** with:
- **Physical layer:** Smart TV, smart speakers, ambient light sensors, cameras, microphones, motion detectors, smart curtains, smart AC.
- **Cyber layer:** Cloud AI that recommends shows, voice assistants, mobile app, cloud-stored viewing history.
- **Privacy layer:** The system knows **who is in the room, what they say, when they sleep, what they watch, their emotional reactions, and even their heart rate** (from a smartwatch).

Now, if the cloud AI:
- Sells your viewing data to advertisers → **privacy violation**.
- Stores voice recordings indefinitely → **privacy violation**.
- Shares room-occupancy patterns with insurance companies → **privacy violation**.
- Uses children's voice data for behavioural profiling → **privacy & legal violation**.

> The *physical world* (your living room) is now a **data source**, and *privacy engineering* is the discipline that decides **which data crosses which boundary, in what form, and for what purpose**.

This is the heart of **Privacy in CPS**.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Privacy in CPS (KTU Conceptual Model)

| Pillar | Meaning in CPS Context | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Subject Privacy** | Protecting the individual whose data is captured by sensors | Driver of a connected car, patient wearing a medical IoT device |
| **Operational Privacy** | Protecting sensitive system-level telemetry from inference | Power consumption patterns revealing factory production schedules |
| **Location & Contextual Privacy** | Protecting physical presence, trajectory, and environmental context | Smart-city camera tracking pedestrian movement |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** CPS privacy is fundamentally harder than IT privacy because **the physical sensor cannot be "logged out"**. A CCTV camera still records when the subject has not given consent.

---

## 1.4 Core Privacy Principles in CPS (FIPPs Foundation)

The **Fair Information Practice Principles (FIPPs)** are the globally accepted baseline for privacy engineering. They are the foundation of GDPR, India's DPDP Act, and US privacy law.

### The 8 FIPPs Applied to CPS:

1. **Notice / Transparency** — Subjects must be informed *what* sensors collect and *why*.
2. **Choice / Consent** — Opt-in/opt-out for data collection beyond system necessity.
3. **Purpose Limitation** — Data collected for one purpose (e.g., traffic flow) cannot be reused for another (e.g., speeding tickets) without consent.
4. **Data Minimization** — Collect only the minimum data required for the CPS function.
5. **Use, Retention, and Disclosure Limitation** — Data must be deleted after its purpose is fulfilled.
6. **Access and Participation** — Subjects can view, correct, and delete their data.
7. **Integrity and Security** — Protect data from tampering and unauthorized access.
8. **Accountability** — The data controller is legally and ethically responsible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Must Memorize:** When asked to "list privacy principles," examiners expect the **8 FIPPs (or the 7 OECD principles)**. Memorize the names *and* one CPS example for each.

---

## 1.5 Privacy Concerns Specific to CPS

CPS introduces **privacy risks that classical IT systems do not have** because of the physical-sensing layer.

### A. The Five CPS-Specific Privacy Concerns:

#### 1. **Side-Channel Inference**
Even when a sensor does not directly collect personal data, the *physical signal* can leak information. Example: Smart-meter electricity readings can infer when a person is home, their sleep schedule, and even the TV channel they watch.

#### 2. **Re-Identification via Sensor Fusion**
Multiple non-personal data streams, when combined, can re-identify an individual. Example: Anonymized GPS pings + smart-watch heart rate + facial-recognition camera feed → fully identified individual.

#### 3. **Continuous / Pervasive Surveillance**
CPS sensors operate **24×7** (wearables, smart cities), unlike a laptop that you can close.

#### 4. **Secondary Use / Function Creep**
A CPS deployed for one purpose (e.g., smart-card fare collection) starts being used for another (e.g., location-based advertising) — without user consent.

#### 5. **Physical-World Linkage**
A data breach in CPS can lead to **physical-world harm** — a hacked insulin pump, a compromised smart lock, a leaked patient location.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often answer "privacy concerns" with generic IT concerns (hacking, phishing). You must specifically mention **CPS-specific** concerns: side-channel inference, sensor fusion re-identification, and physical-world linkage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The CPS Privacy Engineering Stack (Layered Model)

Privacy in CPS is not a single mechanism — it is a **stack of coordinated controls** spanning device, network, cloud, and policy layers.

### Layer 1 — Device / Sensor Layer
- **Hardware-level privacy** (e.g., camera lens covers, microphone kill switches).
- **On-device anonymization** (edge computing, federated learning).
- **Sensor access control** (Android/iOS permission model applied to IoT).

### Layer 2 — Communication Layer
- **Encryption in transit** (TLS 1.3, DTLS for constrained devices).
- **Anonymous routing** (Tor, mixnets for CPS telemetry).
- **Network-level privacy** (MAC randomization, traffic padding).

### Layer 3 — Computation / Cloud Layer
- **Differential privacy** (add calibrated noise before aggregation).
- **Homomorphic encryption** (compute on encrypted CPS data).
- **Secure multi-party computation (SMPC)**.
- **Trusted Execution Environments (TEE)** like ARM TrustZone, Intel SGX.

### Layer 4 — Storage Layer
- **Encryption at rest** (AES-256, format-preserving encryption).
- **Tokenization** (replace PII with opaque tokens).
- **Data sharding & distributed storage**.
- **Right-to-be-forgotten** compliance (GDPR Art. 17).

### Layer 5 — Policy / Governance Layer
- **Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA)**.
- **Data Protection Officer (DPO)** role.
- **Consent management platforms (CMP)**.
- **Regulatory compliance** (GDPR, HIPAA, DPDP Act 2023, CCPA).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "D-C-C-S-P":** **D**evice, **C**ommunication, **C**omputation, **S**torage, **P**olicy. Use this to structure any "explain CPS privacy architecture" answer.

---

## 2.2 Data Collection in CPS — The Lifecycle

### 2.2.1 The Three Classes of Data Collected by CPS

| Class | Description | CPS Example | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Identity Data** | Direct identifiers | Driver license RFID tag, biometric fingerprint | **High** |
| **Behavioural Data** | Activity patterns | Smart-home routine, driving style | **Medium–High** |
| **Contextual / Environmental Data** | Surroundings, location, ambient state | Temperature, air quality, room occupancy | **Medium** (but inferable to high) |

### 2.2.2 The CPS Data Collection Pipeline (4 Stages)

1. **Sensing** — Physical-to-digital conversion (camera, microphone, accelerometer, LiDAR, ECG).
2. **Pre-processing** — Filtering, denoising, feature extraction (often on-edge).
3. **Transmission** — To gateway, fog node, or cloud (via Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa, NB-IoT).
4. **Storage** — Cloud DB, data lake, time-series DB (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privacy must be embedded at EVERY stage** — it cannot be retrofitted. This is the **Privacy by Design (PbD)** principle, mandated by GDPR Art. 25.

---

## 2.3 Data Handling in CPS

Data handling covers everything between collection and storage: processing, analysis, sharing, and disposal.

### 2.3.1 The 7 Principles of Privacy-Preserving Data Handling

| # | Principle | Mechanism in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Anonymization** | Strip PII, use k-anonymity, l-diversity |
| 2 | **Pseudonymization** | Replace identifiers with random tokens (GDPR-compliant) |
| 3 | **Aggregation** | Only release statistical aggregates (city-wide pollution, not per-house) |
| 4 | **Differential Privacy** | Add Laplace/Gaussian noise to query results |
| 5 | **Federated Learning** | Train ML models on-device; share only gradients |
| 6 | **Access Control** | Role-based (RBAC), attribute-based (ABAC) |
| 7 | **Audit Logging** | Immutable log of every data access (forensic accountability) |

### 2.3.2 K-Anonymity — A Key CPS Privacy Metric

A dataset is **k-anonymous** if every record is indistinguishable from at least *k−1* other records with respect to quasi-identifiers (e.g., age, ZIP, gender).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CPS cares:** Smart-grid usage data, even without names, can re-identate a household via (ZIP + 3-digit consumption signature). k-anonymity prevents this.

---

## 2.4 Data Storage in CPS — Privacy-Preserving Patterns

### 2.4.1 Storage Architecture Options

| Architecture | Privacy Strength | CPS Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Centralized cloud** | Weak (single point of leakage) | Legacy enterprise CPS |
| **Edge / on-device** | Strong (data never leaves) | Wearables, smart cameras |
| **Federated / distributed** | Very strong | Smart-grid, federated health AI |
| **Blockchain-anchored** | Strong audit, weak confidentiality | Supply chain CPS, sensor provenance |
| **Hybrid (edge + cloud)** | Balanced | Autonomous vehicles |

### 2.4.2 Storage-Level Privacy Controls

- **AES-256 encryption at rest** (NIST standard).
- **Key management** via HSM (Hardware Security Module) or cloud KMS.
- **Format-Preserving Encryption (FPE)** for legacy CPS databases.
- **Tokenization** for payment/identity CPS data (PCI-DSS).
- **Geofenced storage** (data must remain in a specific jurisdiction — GDPR data residency).
- **Time-bound storage** (auto-deletion after purpose expiry).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Privacy Frameworks Cheat Sheet

| Framework / Law | Year | Region | Core Principle | CPS Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OECD Privacy Guidelines** | 1980/2013 | Global | 7 principles: collection limitation, purpose specification, use limitation, security, openness, individual participation, accountability | Baseline for all CPS privacy |
| **FIPPs** | 1980 | US/global | 8 fair information practices | Foundational framework |
| **GDPR** | 2018 | EU | Lawful, fair, transparent processing; data minimization; purpose limitation; right to be forgotten | Applies to any CPS handling EU resident data |
| **HIPAA** | 1996 | US | Protects health information (PHI) | Medical CPS, wearables, telehealth |
| **CCPA / CPRA** | 2020/2023 | California | Consumer right to know, delete, opt-out of sale | Smart consumer CPS |
| **India DPDP Act** | 2023 | India | Consent-based, purpose limitation, data principal rights | Indian smart-city, Aadhaar-linked CPS |
| **China PIPL** | 2021 | China | Localization + consent + cross-border restrictions | CPS manufacturers in China |
| **NIST Privacy Framework** | 2020 | US/global | Identify-P, Govern-P, Control-P, Communicate-P, Protect-P | Engineering-aligned privacy |
| **ISO/IEC 27701** | 2019 | Global | Extension of ISO 27001 for privacy | CPS vendor certification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High Weightage:** GDPR + India DPDP Act + FIPPs are the most frequently asked. Know the **specific articles** (e.g., GDPR Art. 25 = Privacy by Design, Art. 17 = Right to Erasure).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility & Engineering Relevance

| Industry | CPS Privacy Application |
|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | HIPAA-compliant wearable ECG monitors; federated learning across hospitals |
| **Smart Grid** | Differential privacy on smart-meter aggregates to prevent household re-identification |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | On-board anonymization of dashcam data before cloud upload |
| **Smart Cities** | k-anonymized traffic flow data; no per-individual tracking |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Trade-secret protection of production telemetry; TEE-based computation |
| **Consumer IoT** | GDPR consent banners; data minimization in smart speakers |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** In 14-mark questions, always tie the privacy principle to a **specific CPS domain** (e.g., "GDPR Art. 25 applies to a smart-home camera by requiring data minimization at the firmware level"). Generic answers lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Conceptual Derivations

> This section is structured as **analytical walkthroughs** because privacy in CPS is a conceptual + framework-based topic. Numerical derivations are replaced by **principle-by-principle decomposition** and **privacy-metric derivations** (e.g., k-anonymity, differential privacy budget ε).

---

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1: From FIPPs to GDPR — Deriving the Modern CPS Privacy Stack

### Step 1 — The FIPPs Foundation (1980)
The OECD issued 7 privacy principles in 1980. These were the **axioms** from which all modern privacy law is derived.

$$
\text{FIPPs} = \{\text{Notice, Consent, Purpose, Minimization, Security, Disclosure, Accountability}\}
$$

### Step 2 — Translation into EU Law (1995 → 2018)
The EU's 1995 Data Protection Directive (Directive 95/46/EC) was the first legal codification of FIPPs.

$$
\text{Directive 95/46/EC} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{GDPR (Regulation 2016/679, enforced 2018)}
$$

### Step 3 — GDPR's 7 Principles (Art. 5)
GDPR Art. 5(1) lists **7 binding principles**:

1. **Lawfulness, fairness, transparency**
2. **Purpose limitation**
3. **Data minimization**
4. **Accuracy**
5. **Storage limitation**
6. **Integrity and confidentiality**
7. **Accountability**

### Step 4 — Mapping GDPR Principles to CPS Engineering Controls

| GDPR Principle | CPS Engineering Control |
|---|---|
| Lawfulness, fairness, transparency | Sensor notice (e.g., "this camera records for safety") |
| Purpose limitation | Firmware lock: GPS used only for navigation, not advertising |
| Data minimization | On-device face-blurring; only upload metadata |
| Accuracy | Periodic re-calibration; user-facing data dashboards |
| Storage limitation | Auto-delete raw video after 30 days |
| Integrity and confidentiality | AES-256 + TLS 1.3 |
| Accountability | DPO + DPIA + audit logs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For a 14-mark "Explain GDPR in CPS" question, present this **4-step derivation** (FIPPs → Directive → GDPR → CPS mapping). It demonstrates depth.

---

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2: Deriving the k-Anonymity Bound for CPS Datasets

### Step 1 — Define Quasi-Identifiers (QI)
A quasi-identifier is a set of attributes that, *in combination*, can re-identify an individual.

$$
\text{QI} = \{\text{Age}, \text{ZIP}, \text{Gender}, \text{Smart-meter consumption signature}\}
$$

### Step 2 — The k-Anonymity Condition
A dataset $D$ is **k-anonymous** if, for every record $r \in D$, the equivalence class (set of records with identical QI values) has size $\geq k$:

$$
\forall\, r \in D, \quad \vert\,\{r' \in D : \text{QI}(r) = \text{QI}(r')\}\,\vert \;\geq\; k
$$

### Step 3 — Implication for CPS
If a smart-grid dataset has $k = 5$ for the QI = (ZIP, hourly consumption, appliance count), then every household is hidden among at least 4 others with the same QI. Re-identification probability $\leq 1/k = 20\%$.

### Step 4 — Limitation → l-Diversity and t-Closeness
k-anonymity fails if the sensitive attribute (e.g., "has solar panels") is uniform within a class. Extensions:

- **l-Diversity** — each equivalence class has $\geq l$ "well-represented" values of the sensitive attribute.
- **t-Closeness** — the distribution of the sensitive attribute in each class is within distance $t$ of the global distribution.

### Step 5 — CPS Numerical Example
A smart-city releases $(k=10, l=3, t=0.2)$ anonymized mobility data. This means every pedestrian trajectory is indistinguishable from 9 others, with at least 3 distinct destination types, and the destination distribution is within 20% of the city-wide average. Re-identification risk is **< 10%** even with auxiliary data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "k hides you, l diversifies you, t normalizes you."

---

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3: Differential Privacy — The ε-Budget Derivation

### Step 1 — The Core Definition
A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ is **ε-differentially private** if, for any two datasets $D$ and $D'$ that differ in exactly one record:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \;\leq\; e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

where $S$ is any output subset and $\varepsilon$ is the **privacy budget** (smaller ε = stronger privacy).

### Step 2 — The Laplace Mechanism
For a numeric query $f(D)$, the **Laplace mechanism** adds noise drawn from a Laplace distribution:

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)
$$

where $\Delta f$ is the **sensitivity** (max change in $f$ when one record is added/removed).

### Step 3 — CPS Application: Smart-Meter Aggregation
- True query: average household consumption in a neighbourhood.
- Sensitivity $\Delta f = $ max one-household consumption = 10 kWh.
- Privacy budget $\varepsilon = 0.5$ (moderate).
- Noise scale: $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon = 10 / 0.5 = 20$.

So the released average has noise $\pm 20$ kWh — this is the **privacy–utility trade-off**.

### Step 4 — Composition Theorem
If we make $k$ queries on the same dataset, the total privacy budget degrades:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i
$$

For a CPS releasing hourly aggregates for 24 hours:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{day}} = 24 \times 0.5 = 12
$$

This is **too weak** — privacy degrades. The solution: $\varepsilon_{\text{day}} = \sqrt{24} \times 0.5 \approx 2.45$ using **advanced composition** (Gaussian mechanism).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Differential privacy is the **gold standard** for CPS aggregate release. Apple and Google use it on-device.

---

## 3.4 Walkthrough 4: Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) — Step-by-Step Methodology

A **Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA)** is a mandatory process under GDPR Art. 35 for high-risk CPS deployments.

### Step 1 — Identify the Need
- "We are deploying a smart-camera system in a residential society."

### Step 2 — Describe the Information Flows
- Camera → NVR → Cloud AI → Resident app.
- Data types: video, audio, facial features, timestamps.

### Step 3 — Identify Privacy Risks
| Risk | Likelihood | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Unauthorized access to video | Medium | High |
| Function creep (used for marketing) | High | High |
| Side-channel inference (lifestyle) | Medium | Medium |

### Step 4 — Apply Mitigation Controls
- End-to-end encryption.
- On-device face blurring.
- Purpose-binding via firmware.
- Data retention policy: 7 days only.
- Consent banner on resident app.

### Step 5 — Residual Risk & Sign-off
- Residual risk = Likelihood × Impact after controls.
- Documented and approved by DPO.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Template:** For "Explain PIA in CPS," use the above 5-step structure. Examiners reward methodical answers.

---

## 3.5 Walkthrough 5: Mapping the India DPDP Act 2023 to CPS

The **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** is India's first comprehensive privacy law. Key CPS mappings:

| DPDP Section | CPS Implementation |
|---|---|
| §4 — Grounds for processing | CPS must have consent OR legitimate use |
| §6 — Consent | Must be free, specific, informed, unconditional |
| §7 — Certain legitimate uses | Medical emergency, employment, public interest |
| §8 — General obligations of Data Fiduciary | Implement security safeguards, breach notification |
| §10 — Right of Data Principal to access information | CPS dashboard for user data |
| §11 — Right to correction and erasure | "Right to be forgotten" in CPS |
| §12 — Right of grievance redressal | CPS must have a grievance officer |
| §33 — Exemptions | National security, research (with conditions) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Indian Context:** Since this is a Kerala university, expect at least one question on DPDP Act 2023 vs. GDPR. Memorize the section numbers above.

---

## 3.6 The CPS Privacy Threat Model Summary Table

| Threat | Description | CPS Example | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Eavesdropping** | Intercept sensor data on network | Unencrypted smart-meter traffic | TLS 1.3, VPN |
| **Inference attack** | Deduce private info from aggregates | Household routine from power data | Differential privacy |
| **Re-identification** | Link anonymized data to identity | Anonymized GPS + face = identified | k-anonymity, l-diversity |
| **Function creep** | Re-purpose data without consent | Traffic cams used for surveillance | Purpose limitation, audit |
| **Insider threat** | Employee abuses data access | Hospital admin leaks patient CPS data | RBAC, audit logging |
| **Physical tampering** | Sensor hardware compromised | Smart-lock firmware extracted | Secure boot, TPM |
| **Data breach** | Bulk CPS data exfiltration | Cloud IoT database hacked | Encryption at rest, tokenization |
| **Secondary use** | Selling CPS data to third parties | Smart-speaker recordings sold | Contractual controls, GDPR Art. 28 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often list *security* threats here (DDoS, malware). Privacy threats are distinct: they assume **authorized access** but **improper use**. Always emphasize this distinction.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: The Privacy-Utility Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["LOW PRIVACY<br/>High utility<br/>Full data, no noise"] --> Y["MEDIUM PRIVACY<br/>Balanced<br/>k-anonymity, basic DP"]
    Y --> Z["HIGH PRIVACY<br/>Low utility<br/>Strong DP, heavy noise"]

    X -.Add noise.-> Y
    Y -.Add more noise.-> Z
```

> The **privacy budget ε** is the dial that moves a CPS left/right on this curve.

```mermaid

    A2["Smartwatch heart rate"] --> R
    A3["CCTV facial features"] --> R

    R --> O["FULLY IDENTIFIED<br/>INDIVIDUAL"]

    style O fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This is the **sensor-fusion re-identification** problem unique to CPS. Even *individually anonymous* CPS data streams become identifying when combined.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate between data privacy and data security in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems.**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Data Privacy | Data Security |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Protect individuals from improper *use* of their data | Protect data from *unauthorized access* |
| **Concern** | Consent, purpose, minimization | Confidentiality, integrity, availability |
| **CPS Example** | Smart camera uses video only for safety, not marketing | Smart camera's video feed is encrypted against hacking |
| **Failure mode** | Lawful but improper use | Unauthorized breach |

A CPS can be **secure but not private** (e.g., encrypted camera whose footage the vendor sells to advertisers) — both are necessary but distinct.

*(1 mark for each correct row + 1 mark for the concluding statement = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. List any three CPS-specific privacy concerns with a one-line example each.**  `[KTU Model Question Bank - 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Side-channel inference** — Smart-meter electricity data revealing when a person is home. *(1 mark)*
2. **Re-identification via sensor fusion** — Anonymized GPS + smartwatch + CCTV identifying an individual. *(1 mark)*
3. **Function creep** — A traffic camera deployed for safety being repurposed for surveillance. *(1 mark)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**  `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the **8 Fair Information Practice Principles (FIPPs)** in detail, with a CPS example for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss **GDPR's 7 principles** under Article 5, and explain how each is implemented as an engineering control in a smart-home CPS. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

#### **Part (a) — FIPPs in CPS (7 marks)**

| # | FIPPs Principle | Definition (1 mark each row) | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Notice / Transparency** | Subject must be informed about data collection | A sign near a smart CCTV: "This area is recorded for safety" |
| 2 | **Choice / Consent** | Opt-in/opt-out for data use | Smart-speaker asking permission before saving voice to cloud |
| 3 | **Purpose Limitation** | Data used only for the stated purpose | Smart meter data for billing, not behavioural profiling |
| 4 | **Data Minimization** | Collect only the minimum required | Smart doorbell blurs neighbours' faces by default |
| 5 | **Use, Retention, Disclosure Limitation** | Don't share; delete when done | Cloud video auto-deleted after 7 days |
| 6 | **Access and Participation** | User can view, correct, delete | Mobile app showing all data a smart thermostat has collected |
| 7 | **Integrity and Security** | Protect from tampering and breach | Encrypted sensor transmission with TLS 1.3 |
| 8 | **Accountability** | Data controller is responsible | Vendor appoints a DPO; performs PIA |

*(1 mark per row × 7 = 7 marks — give partial credit for understanding even if naming is partial)*

#### **Part (b) — GDPR Art. 5 in Smart-Home CPS (7 marks)**

| GDPR Art. 5 Principle | Engineering Control in Smart-Home CPS |
|---|---|
| 1. Lawfulness, fairness, transparency | On-screen notice when camera activates; no hidden sensors |
| 2. Purpose limitation | Firmware lock: microphone cannot be activated remotely for marketing |
| 3. Data minimization | Edge processing — only metadata uploaded, not raw audio |
| 4. Accuracy | Periodic sensor re-calibration; user dashboard to correct wrong data |
| 5. Storage limitation | Raw video purged after 30 days; only events retained |
| 6. Integrity and confidentiality | AES-256 at rest + TLS 1.3 in transit + secure boot |
| 7. Accountability | DPO appointed; PIA conducted annually; breach reported within 72 hrs (Art. 33) |

*(1 mark for identifying each principle, 0.5 for engineering control, total 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Describe the **CPS data lifecycle** in detail. For each stage, identify the privacy risk and the corresponding engineering control. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **Differential Privacy** with the Laplace mechanism. Derive the noise scale for a smart-meter aggregation query and discuss the privacy-utility trade-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

#### **Part (a) — CPS Data Lifecycle (7 marks)**

| Stage | Activity | Privacy Risk | Engineering Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Sensing** | Cameras, mics, GPS, biometrics capture raw data | Covert collection; over-collection | Sensor notice; on-device consent check; physical kill switch |
| **2. Pre-processing** | Filtering, feature extraction at the edge | Inference from features (e.g., gait) | On-device anonymization; face blurring |
| **3. Transmission** | Send to gateway or cloud | Eavesdropping, MITM | TLS 1.3; DTLS for constrained devices; VPN |
| **4. Storage** | Persist in DB, data lake, blockchain | Breach; unauthorized access | AES-256 at rest; tokenization; HSM-backed key management |
| **5. Analysis** | ML training, dashboards | Re-identification; function creep | Differential privacy; federated learning; purpose-binding |
| **6. Sharing** | Release to third parties | Sale of data; secondary use | k-anonymity; contractually enforced purpose limitation (GDPR Art. 28) |
| **7. Disposal** | Delete data after retention | Retention beyond purpose | Right-to-be-forgotten pipeline; cryptographic shredding |

*(1 mark per stage explained = 7 marks)*

#### **Part (b) — Differential Privacy with Laplace Mechanism (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ is ε-differentially private if, for neighbouring datasets $D$ and $D'$ differing in one record:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \;\leq\; e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

**Step 2 — Laplace Mechanism (1.5 marks):**
For a numeric query $f$ with sensitivity $\Delta f$:

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — CPS Smart-Meter Example (2 marks):**
- True average consumption: $f(D) = 4.2$ kWh.
- Max one-household sensitivity: $\Delta f = 10$ kWh.
- Privacy budget: $\varepsilon = 0.5$.
- Noise scale: $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon = 10 / 0.5 = 20$ kWh.
- Released value: $4.2 + \text{Lap}(20)$ — typically $\pm 20$ kWh noise.

**Step 4 — Privacy-Utility Trade-off (1.5 marks):**
- Smaller $\varepsilon$ → stronger privacy → larger noise → lower utility.
- Larger $\varepsilon$ → weaker privacy → smaller noise → higher utility.
- Composition: releasing 24 hourly aggregates with $\varepsilon = 0.5$ each → $\varepsilon_{\text{day}} = 12$ (weak). Solution: advanced composition $\sqrt{k} \cdot \varepsilon$ or use **Gaussian mechanism** for tight composition.

*(Marks distributed: 2 + 1.5 + 2 + 1.5 = 7)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing **privacy** with **security** — they are distinct.
> 2. Listing **IT privacy concerns** (phishing, malware) instead of **CPS-specific concerns** (side-channel inference, sensor fusion, function creep).
> 3. Not naming **specific articles** of GDPR/DPDP (e.g., "GDPR Art. 25 — Privacy by Design") — vague answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. Failing to **map each principle to a concrete CPS example** — abstract answers cap at 60% marks.
> 5. Forgetting **Privacy by Design** is a *mandatory* GDPR requirement for CPS engineering, not an option.
> 6. In differential-privacy questions, not defining **sensitivity $\Delta f$** — examiners specifically look for it.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **CPS** = Cyber (computation) + Physical (sensors/actuators) + Network.
- **Privacy in CPS** ≠ Security in CPS. Security protects *systems*; privacy protects *individuals*.
- **FIPPs (8 principles)** = Notice, Choice, Purpose Limitation, Minimization, Retention Limitation, Access, Integrity, Accountability.
- **Privacy by Design (PbD)** = Embed privacy at every layer of CPS — device, network, compute, storage, policy.
- **Differential Privacy** = Add calibrated noise; ε is the privacy budget.
- **k-Anonymity** = Each record indistinguishable from k−1 others.
- **GDPR Art. 25** = Privacy by Design; **Art. 17** = Right to Erasure; **Art. 35** = PIA for high-risk CPS.
- **DPDP Act 2023** = India's consent-based privacy law; **DPO** mandatory for significant data fiduciaries.

### 🛡️ CPS-Specific Privacy Concerns (Must Memorize)
- Side-channel inference
- Sensor-fusion re-identification
- Continuous pervasive surveillance
- Function creep / secondary use
- Physical-world linkage harm

### 🏗️ The 5-Layer CPS Privacy Stack
**D**evice → **C**ommunication → **C**ompute → **S**torage → **P**olicy
(Mnemonic: **D-C-C-S-P**)

### 🔐 Engineering Controls (7 Categories)
Anonymization, Pseudonymization, Aggregation, Differential Privacy, Federated Learning, Access Control (RBAC/ABAC), Audit Logging.

### 📜 Key Regulations
- **EU:** GDPR (2018)
- **USA:** HIPAA (health), CCPA/CPRA (California)
- **India:** DPDP Act (2023)
- **China:** PIPL (2021)
- **Frameworks:** NIST Privacy Framework (2020), ISO/IEC 27701 (2019), OECD Guidelines (1980/2013)

### 🧠 Mnemonics
- **D-C-C-S-P** — 5-layer privacy stack
- **"k hides, l diversifies, t normalizes"** — anonymization hierarchy
- **"Privacy cannot be retrofitted"** — PbD mantra

### ⚠️ Common Exam Mistakes
- Treating privacy as a *legal* problem only (it is also an *engineering* problem).
- Forgetting that **sensors cannot be "logged out"** — physical-layer privacy needs hardware controls.
- Confusing **anonymization** (irreversible) with **pseudonymization** (reversible with a key).
- Missing **breach notification timelines**: GDPR = 72 hours; DPDP = 72 hours.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT823 - SECURITY AND PRIVACY IN CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Protection Techniques: Data Encryption and Decryption Methods, Authentication and Authorization Mechanisms, Access Control Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is Data Protection in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Data Protection in Cyber-Physical Systems refers to the comprehensive set of cryptographic, authentication, authorization, and access control methodologies engineered to preserve the **Confidentiality**, **Integrity**, **Availability** (the classic **CIA Triad**, alongside **Authenticity** and **Non-Repudiation**) of data exchanged between computational algorithms and physical processes within an interconnected CPS architecture (e.g., IoT-enabled smart grids, autonomous vehicles, industrial SCADA, medical cybernetics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept — The CIA Triad Plus Two:**
> In CPS, data protection is NOT just about secrecy. It guarantees:
> 1. **Confidentiality** – No unauthorized disclosure.
> 2. **Integrity** – No unauthorized modification.
> 3. **Availability** – Systems accessible when needed.
> 4. **Authenticity** – Proof of identity of the sender.
> 5. **Non-Repudiation** – Sender cannot deny sending a message.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The High-Security Bank Vault"

Imagine a **Swiss bank vault** built inside a smart factory (the CPS):

- **The Vault Door (Encryption):** The vault door is a mathematical lock. You scramble your gold (data) using a key so that even if thieves break in, they cannot read or use it.
- **The Biometric Scanner + ID Card (Authentication):** Before the door even opens, the system must verify *who you are* (fingerprint = something you are; PIN = something you know; security token = something you have).
- **The Internal Access List (Authorization):** Once inside, a junior clerk can only access the safe deposit boxes on the ground floor, while the manager can enter the bullion room. The *role* and *policy* dictate what actions are permitted.
- **The 24/7 Armed Guard (Access Control Model):** A continuous policy engine enforces these rules in real-time — this is the **Access Control Model**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in CPS:** A compromised authentication credential in a smart grid could let an attacker issue false control commands, causing physical blackouts. Hence, CPS data protection is simultaneously a *digital* and a *physical safety* problem.

---

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Data Protection Techniques

Data protection techniques are typically organized into three functional pillars, which together form the **AAA Framework (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting)**:

| Pillar | Function | Example Mechanisms |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality Layer** | Hides data from eavesdroppers | AES, RSA, ECC, ChaCha20 |
| **Authentication Layer** | Verifies identity of entities | Passwords, Biometrics, OTP, Digital Certificates |
| **Authorization / Access Control Layer** | Defines what authenticated entities can do | DAC, MAC, RBAC, ABAC, ACLs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked "Explain data protection techniques," ALWAYS start with the **CIA Triad**, then map each technique to the specific CIA property it protects. Examiners reward a structured narrative that links *threat → property → countermeasure*.

---

## 1.4 Formal Mathematical Setup

Let $M$ denote the original **plaintext message** (the raw data from a CPS sensor or actuator), $C$ denote the resulting **ciphertext**, and $K$ denote the **key**.

The fundamental cryptographic relationship is defined by a pair of functions:

$$E(M, K) \longrightarrow C$$

$$D(C, K) \longrightarrow M$$

Where:
- $E(\cdot)$ is the **Encryption** function.
- $D(\cdot)$ is the **Decryption** function.
- $K$ belongs to a key space $\mathcal{K}$.

The **correctness property** mandates that for any valid $M$ and $K$:

$$D(E(M, K), K) = M$$

This is the cornerstone equation for every cipher students must memorize for KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Data Encryption & Decryption Methods

Encryption is broadly classified by **key architecture**. The two foundational paradigms are **Symmetric-Key** and **Asymmetric-Key** cryptography, supplemented by **Hash Functions** and **Hybrid Schemes**.

### 2.1.1 Symmetric-Key Encryption (Secret-Key Cryptography)

**Concept:** The *same* secret key $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption. Both sender and receiver must possess a shared secret prior to communication.

$$C = E(M, K)$$

$$M = D(C, K)$$

**Sub-Categories:**

| Type | Mode of Operation | KTU-Relevant Examples | Block Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Block Ciphers** | Encrypt fixed-size blocks | **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)**, DES, 3DES, Blowfish | 64 / 128 bits |
| **Stream Ciphers** | Encrypt bit-by-bit/byte-by-byte | **ChaCha20**, RC4, Salsa20 | N/A (continuous) |

**AES (Advanced Encryption Standard — Rijndael):**
- Standardized by **NIST in 2001 (FIPS PUB 197)**.
- Block size: **128 bits**.
- Key sizes: **128, 192, or 256 bits** (AES-128, AES-192, AES-256).
- Rounds: **10, 12, or 14** corresponding to key size.
- Operates on a 4×4 byte matrix called the **State Array**.

**Operational Steps of AES (Per Round):**
1. **SubBytes** — Non-linear substitution via S-Box.
2. **ShiftRows** — Cyclic shift of state rows.
3. **MixColumns** — Linear mixing over $\text{GF}(2^8)$.
4. **AddRoundKey** — XOR with the round key derived from key schedule.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Memory Aid:** **"S-S-M-A = SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey"**. The *last round* skips MixColumns.

---

### 2.1.2 Asymmetric-Key Encryption (Public-Key Cryptography)

**Concept:** A mathematically related **key pair** $(K_{pub}, K_{priv})$ is used. The public key encrypts; the private key decrypts. Solves the *key distribution problem* inherent in symmetric schemes.

**Foundational Algorithms:**

| Algorithm | Based On | Key Size (Modern) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RSA** | Integer Factorization | 2048 / 4096 bits | Digital signatures, TLS handshakes |
| **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)** | Discrete Log on elliptic curves | 256 / 384 bits | Mobile, IoT, CPS edge nodes |
| **Diffie-Hellman (DH)** | Discrete Logarithm Problem | 2048+ bits | Key exchange |
| **ElGamal** | Discrete Logarithm Problem | 1024+ bits | Encryption, signatures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trap Question:** "Which algorithm is used for digital signatures — RSA or AES?"
> **Answer:** **RSA** (asymmetric). AES is symmetric and cannot be used directly for non-repudiation because the *same* key is shared.

---

### 2.1.3 Cryptographic Hash Functions

A hash function $H(\cdot)$ maps arbitrary-length input $M$ to a **fixed-length** digest $h$:

$$h = H(M), \quad \text{where} \quad \vert h \vert = n \text{ bits (fixed)}$$

**Five Security Properties (KTU Exam Favorite):**
1. **Determinism** — Same input $\Rightarrow$ same output.
2. **Pre-image Resistance** — Given $h$, infeasible to find $M$.
3. **Second Pre-image Resistance** — Given $M_1$, infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
4. **Collision Resistance** — Infeasible to find any $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
5. **Avalanche Effect** — Changing 1 bit in $M$ changes $\geq 50\%$ of bits in $h$.

**Industry Standards:**
- **SHA-256 / SHA-3 (Keccak)** — 256-bit digest, used in blockchain, TLS 1.3, CPS firmware integrity.
- **MD5** — Broken (collision attacks exist). **DO NOT** recommend in 2024.
- **HMAC** — Keyed hash for message authentication codes.

---

### 2.1.4 Hybrid Encryption (Real-World CPS Standard)

In practice, CPS systems (e.g., TLS 1.3, MQTT over TLS, OPC UA) use **hybrid cryptography**:
- **Asymmetric** (e.g., RSA/ECC) securely exchanges a temporary symmetric key.
- **Symmetric** (e.g., AES-256-GCM) encrypts the bulk data stream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reason:** Asymmetric is *expensive* (1000× slower), symmetric is *fast* but has a key-exchange problem. Hybrid = best of both worlds.

---

## 2.2 Authentication Mechanisms

**Authentication** answers the question: **"Are you really who you claim to be?"** It is the verification of an entity's claimed identity.

### 2.2.1 Authentication Factors (Something you...)

| Factor | Category | Examples | Vulnerability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge** | Something you *know* | Passwords, PINs, Security questions | Phishing, Brute-force |
| **Possession** | Something you *have* | Smart card, OTP token, Mobile authenticator | Theft, Cloning |
| **Biometric** | Something you *are* | Fingerprint, Iris, Face, Voice | Spoofing, FAR/FRR errors |
| **Location** | Somewhere you *are* | GPS, IP geolocation | GPS spoofing |
| **Behavioral** | Something you *do* | Keystroke dynamics, Gait analysis | ML adversarial attacks |

**MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication):** Combines $\geq 2$ factors. Standard for CPS operator consoles.

### 2.2.2 Authentication Protocols

1. **Password-Based (PAP):** Plaintext/Hashed password over channel. *Weak.*
2. **Challenge-Response:** Server sends random *nonce* $N$, client computes $f(N, K)$ using shared secret. Defeats replay.
3. **One-Time Password (OTP):**
   - **TOTP (RFC 6238):** Time-based, e.g., Google Authenticator (30-second window).
   - **HOTP (RFC 4226):** Counter-based.
4. **Kerberos (Used in ICS/SCADA networks):** Ticket-based, uses symmetric key cryptography, relies on a Key Distribution Center (KDC) and Time-Stamped tickets to prevent replay.
5. **Public-Key Authentication (TLS Handshake):** Digital certificates (X.509) issued by a Certificate Authority (CA).

### 2.2.3 Digital Signatures (Non-Repudiation)

A digital signature provides **authenticity + integrity + non-repudiation**. For message $M$ and signer with private key $K_{priv}$:

$$\text{Signature } \sigma = \text{Sign}(M, K_{priv})$$

$$\text{Verification: Valid} \iff \text{Verify}(M, \sigma, K_{pub}) = \text{True}$$

Used in CPS for: firmware update authentication, signed sensor telemetry, blockchain-anchored audit trails.

---

## 2.3 Authorization & Access Control Models

**Authorization** answers: **"Once I know who you are, what are you allowed to do?"** It governs **access rights** to resources.

### 2.3.1 Core Components of an Access Control System

- **Subject** — The entity requesting access (user, process, device).
- **Object** — The resource being accessed (file, sensor, actuator, API endpoint).
- **Access Right / Permission** — The action allowed (read, write, execute, control).
- **Policy** — The rule set determining permission grants.

### 2.3.2 Classical Access Control Models (KTU High-Yield)

| Model | Full Name | Authority Locus | Granularity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control | Resource *owner* decides | Per-user | File systems (Windows NTFS, UNIX) |
| **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control | Central *system policy* (admin) | Per-label | Military, High-security CPS (e.g., nuclear SCADA) |
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | *Roles* assigned to users | Per-role | Enterprise IT, Hospital systems |
| **ABAC** | Attribute-Based Access Control | *Attributes* of subject/object/env | Fine-grained | Cloud, IoT, Zero-Trust CPS |
| **RuBAC** | Rule-Based Access Control | Predefined *if-then* rules | Conditional | Firewalls, IDS |
| **OrBAC** | Organization-Based Access Control | Organizational context | Multi-tenant | Healthcare CPS |

**The BLP Model (Bell-LaPadula) — A KTU Mandatory Reference:**
- Foundation of **MAC**. Used in classified government / defense CPS.
- **No Read Up (NRU)** — *Simple Security Property*: Subject of clearance $\text{level}(S)$ cannot read object of higher classification.
- **No Write Down (NWD)** — *Star Property (⋆)*: Subject cannot write to a lower classification (prevents data leakage).
- **Tranquility Principle:** Security labels do not change dynamically during a session.

**The Biba Model — Integrity Counterpart:**
- **No Write Up:** Prevents low-integrity subjects from corrupting high-integrity data.
- **No Read Down:** Prevents high-integrity subjects from being "contaminated" by low-integrity data.
- Critical in **CPS where actuator commands must not be corrupted.**

### 2.3.3 Modern Models (Industry 4.0 / Smart CPS)

- **Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207):** "Never trust, always verify." Continuous authentication + least privilege + micro-segmentation.
- **Capability-Based Access Control (CapBAC):** Tokens carry permissions — ideal for IoT/CPS edge devices.
- **Trust-Based Access Control (TrustBAC):** Trust scores derived from behavior, used in federated CPS.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is gold for KTU 2024 valuation. Memorize all rows.**

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Key Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encryption/Decryption Correctness** | $D(E(M, K), K) = M$ | Cipher $E$, Key $K$ | — |
| **RSA Key Generation** | $n = p \cdot q$ | $p, q$ large primes | $p, q \geq 1024$ bits each |
| **RSA Modulus Property** | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Euler's Totient | — |
| **RSA Public Exponent** | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | $e$ usually 65537 | $e = 2^{16}+1$ |
| **RSA Private Exponent** | $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | $d$ | — |
| **RSA Encrypt** | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Mod $n$ | — |
| **RSA Decrypt** | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Mod $n$ | — |
| **Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret** | $K_{AB} = (g^{b})^{a} \pmod{p} = (g^{a})^{b} \pmod{p}$ | $g, p$ public | $p \geq 2048$ bits |
| **Hash Digest Length (SHA-256)** | $n = 256$ bits | — | $2^{256}$ search space |
| **AES Block Size** | $b = 128$ bits | — | Fixed |
| **AES Round Count** | $R \in \{10, 12, 14\}$ | Depends on key | For 128/192/256-bit key |
| **HMAC Construction** | $HMAC(K, M) = H((K \oplus opad) \vert\vert H((K \oplus ipad) \vert\vert M))$ | $opad, ipad$ constants | — |
| **Kerberos Ticket Lifetime** | $T_{ticket} = T_{start} + \Delta t$ | Typically 8–24 hours | Renewable |
| **Shannon Entropy (Password Strength)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Bits of entropy | $> 80$ bits = strong |
| **Birthday Attack on Hash** | $O(2^{n/2})$ to find collision | $n$ = hash length | SHA-256 ⇒ $2^{128}$ ops |
| **Bell-LaPadula (No Read Up)** | $\text{level}(S) \geq \text{level}(O)$ for read | Clearance | — |
| **Bell-LaPadula (No Write Down)** | $\text{level}(S) \leq \text{level}(O)$ for write | Classification | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule (for KTU handwritten/typed answers):** When writing inline math, use **single dollar signs** `$ ... $`. When writing display equations, use **double dollar signs** `$$ ... $$` on a *separate line*. This matches KTU's official answer script notation expectations and avoids valuation ambiguity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grid (IEC 61850):** Uses **MAC with BLP-style labels** for substation automation, plus **digital signatures** for GOOSE message authentication.
- **Connected Vehicles (V2X):** **ECC-based digital signatures** (ECDSA, Ed25519) authenticate messages within milliseconds.
- **Industrial IoT (OPC UA):** Combines **X.509 certificates** (asymmetric) with **AES-256** (symmetric) and **RBAC** for device access.
- **Medical CPS (Pacemakers, Insulin Pumps):** **Biometric + PIN** for clinician access, **HMAC** for telemetry integrity, **ABAC** for emergency override.
- **Drones & Robotics (ROS 2):** **SROS2** (Secure ROS) wraps DDS with **DTLS** and uses **CapBAC** for node-to-node permissions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 RSA Key Generation — Exhaustive Derivation

**Problem Setup:** Alice wants to generate an RSA key pair so Bob can send her confidential messages.

### Step 1 — Choose Two Large Primes
Select two random, distinct, large primes $p$ and $q$.

For the sake of KTU exam demonstration, let us take the **smallest valid example** (educational only — real systems use $\geq 1024$ bits):

$$p = 61, \quad q = 53$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Modulus $n$

$$n = p \cdot q$$

$$n = 61 \times 53$$

Let us break this down:
$$61 \times 53 = 61 \times 50 + 61 \times 3$$

$$= 3050 + 183$$

$$= 3233$$

$$\therefore n = 3233$$

### Step 3 — Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$

For a product of two distinct primes, Euler's totient has the elegant closed form:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$$

Substituting:

$$\phi(n) = (61 - 1)(53 - 1)$$

$$= 60 \times 52$$

$$= 3120$$

$$\therefore \phi(n) = 3120$$

### Step 4 — Choose the Public Exponent $e$

Choose $e$ such that:
$$1 < e < \phi(n) \quad \text{and} \quad \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$$

A common choice is $e = 17$. Let us verify $\gcd(17, 3120)$:

$$3120 = 183 \times 17 + 9$$

$$17 = 1 \times 9 + 8$$

$$9 = 1 \times 8 + 1$$

$$8 = 8 \times 1 + 0$$

The last non-zero remainder is $1$, confirming:

$$\gcd(17, 3120) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

Therefore, $e = 17$ is a valid public exponent.

### Step 5 — Compute the Private Exponent $d$ via Modular Inverse

We need $d$ such that:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

$$17d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

We solve using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:

From Step 4:
$$3120 = 183 \times 17 + 9 \implies 9 = 3120 - 183 \times 17$$

$$17 = 1 \times 9 + 8 \implies 8 = 17 - 1 \times 9$$

$$9 = 1 \times 8 + 1 \implies 1 = 9 - 1 \times 8$$

Back-substitute:
$$1 = 9 - 1 \times 8$$

$$= 9 - 1 \times (17 - 1 \times 9) = 2 \times 9 - 1 \times 17$$

$$= 2 \times (3120 - 183 \times 17) - 1 \times 17$$

$$= 2 \times 3120 - 366 \times 17 - 1 \times 17$$

$$= 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17$$

Thus:
$$-367 \times 17 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

$$d \equiv -367 \pmod{3120}$$

$$d = 3120 - 367 = 2753$$

$$\therefore d = 2753$$

**Public Key:** $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$
**Private Key:** $(d, n) = (2753, 3233)$

### Step 6 — Verify Correctness (Sanity Check)

To prove correctness, we must show $D(E(M, K)) = M$. Per Euler's theorem:

$$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

For RSA:
$$C^{d} \equiv (M^{e})^{d} \equiv M^{e \cdot d} \pmod{n}$$

Since $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k$:

$$M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore (M^{e})^{d} \equiv M \pmod{n}}$$

This is the **mathematical proof of RSA correctness** — guaranteed by Euler's theorem.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — RSA Encrypt/Decrypt a Single Character

Let $M = 65$ (ASCII for 'A'). Encrypt with $e = 17, n = 3233$:

### Encryption

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

$$C \equiv 65^{17} \pmod{3233}$$

We compute step by step using **modular exponentiation (square-and-multiply)**:

$$65^{1} \equiv 65 \pmod{3233}$$

$$65^{2} = 4225 = 1 \times 3233 + 992 \implies 65^{2} \equiv 992 \pmod{3233}$$

$$65^{4} \equiv 992^{2} = 984064$$

$$984064 \div 3233 \approx 304.3$$

$$984064 - 304 \times 3233 = 984064 - 982832 = 1232$$

$$65^{4} \equiv 1232 \pmod{3233}$$

$$65^{8} \equiv 1232^{2} = 1517824$$

$$1517824 - 469 \times 3233 = 1517824 - 1516277 = 1547$$

$$65^{8} \equiv 1547 \pmod{3233}$$

$$65^{16} \equiv 1547^{2} = 2393209$$

$$2393209 - 740 \times 3233 = 2393209 - 2392420 = 789$$

$$65^{16} \equiv 789 \pmod{3233}$$

Now, $17 = 16 + 1$:

$$65^{17} \equiv 65^{16} \times 65^{1} \equiv 789 \times 65 \pmod{3233}$$

$$789 \times 65 = 789 \times 60 + 789 \times 5$$

$$= 47340 + 3945 = 51285$$

$$51285 - 15 \times 3233 = 51285 - 48495 = 2790$$

$$\therefore C = 2790$$

### Decryption

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$$

$$M \equiv 2790^{2753} \pmod{3233}$$

In a real KTU exam, you will not be asked to compute this manually. Instead, the verifier will **apply the correctness theorem** we just proved and state:

$$\therefore (2790)^{2753} \equiv 65 \pmod{3233}$$

This confirms the message 'A' was successfully recovered.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** For 14-mark questions, the *derivation of RSA* and the *statement of Euler's theorem* is what earns full marks. Manual decryption of large numbers is never required.

---

## 3.3 Hash Avalanche Effect — Quantitative Analysis

**Property Definition:** A *single-bit change* in the input must cause a *massive, statistically uniform* change in the output.

**Mathematical Statement:** For a hash function $H(\cdot)$ of output size $n$ bits, the probability of any individual bit $i$ flipping when the input changes by 1 bit should be:

$$P(\text{bit } i \text{ flips}) = \frac{1}{2}, \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$$

**Consequence for SHA-256 (n = 256):**
- Expected number of bits flipped: $256 \times 0.5 = 128$ bits.
- This is a **50% avalanche threshold** — a hash function is considered cryptographically strong if empirical avalanche tests confirm this.

**Why It Matters in CPS:** Sensor data from a smart grid meter that differs by **one cent** in value should produce a *completely different* hash, enabling tamper detection on continuous data streams.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption

| Parameter | Symmetric (AES) | Asymmetric (RSA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Count** | 1 shared key | 2 keys (public + private) |
| **Speed** | $\sim 1$ GB/s (hardware-accelerated) | $\sim 1$ MB/s (1000× slower) |
| **Key Length for Equiv. Security** | AES-128 ≈ RSA-3072 | RSA-3072 |
| **Key Distribution** | Hard (requires secure channel) | Easy (public key published) |
| **Scalability** | $O(n^2)$ keys for $n$ users | $O(n)$ keys |
| **Provides Non-Repudiation** | No | Yes (via digital signature) |
| **Computation Type** | Substitution + Permutation (SPN) | Modular exponentiation |
| **CPS Use Case** | Real-time sensor data encryption | Key exchange, firmware signing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalence Table for KTU 2024:**
> | Security Bits | Symmetric | RSA / DH | ECC |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 80 | AES-128 (legacy) | 1024 | 160 |
> | 112 | AES-128 | 2048 | 224 |
> | 128 | AES-128 | 3072 | 256 |
> | 192 | AES-192 | 7680 | 384 |
> | 256 | AES-256 | 15360 | 512 |

---

## 3.5 Kerberos Authentication — Step-by-Step Ticket Exchange

**Actors:** Client (C), Authentication Server (AS), Ticket Granting Server (TGS), Service Server (SS).

| Step | Message | Cryptographic Operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C \rightarrow AS: ID_C, ID_{TGS}$ | Plaintext | Request TGT |
| 2 | $AS \rightarrow C: E(K_C, K_{TGS} \vert\vert TGT)$ | Symmetric (DES/AES) | Deliver TGT encrypted with C's key |
| 3 | $C \rightarrow TGS: TGT, Authenticator_1$ | TGT forwarded | Request service ticket |
| 4 | $TGS \rightarrow C: E(K_{TGS}, K_{S} \vert\vert S_{ticket})$ | Symmetric | Deliver service session key |
| 5 | $C \rightarrow SS: S_{ticket}, Authenticator_2$ | Symmetric | Authenticate to service |
| 6 | $SS \rightarrow C: E(K_S, Timestamp+1)$ | Symmetric | Mutual auth confirmed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Kerberos is critical in CPS:** SCADA/ICS networks frequently use Kerberos-authenticated Active Directory domains. Compromise of the KDC is total compromise — hence the "KDC must be hardened" rule.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 High-Level Data Protection Pipeline in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA[Physical Sensor] -->|Raw Data M| encryptA[Encryption Module E]
    encryptA -->|Ciphertext C| channelA[Insecure Channel]
    channelA -->|C| decryptA[Decryption Module D]
    decryptA -->|Recovered M| actuatorA[Physical Actuator]
    authA[Authentication Server] -.->|Verifies Identity| encryptA
    authA -.->|Verifies Identity| decryptA
    acA[Access Control Engine] -.->|Policy Check| actuatorA
```

## 4.2 Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption — Key Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SymA[Symmetric Encryption]
        sM[Plaintext M] --> sE[Encryption E with K]
        sE --> sC[Ciphertext C]
        sC --> sD[Decryption D with K]
        sD --> sM2[Plaintext M]
    end
    subgraph AsymA[Asymmetric Encryption]
        aM[Plaintext M] --> aE[Encryption E with K_pub of Bob]
        aE --> aC[Ciphertext C]
        aC --> aD[Decryption D with K_priv of Bob]
        aD --> aM2[Plaintext M]
    end
```

## 4.3 Authentication, Authorization, Accounting (AAA) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User/Device] --> reqA[1. Access Request]
    reqA --> authA[2. Authentication Module]
    authA -->|Valid Identity| authzA[3. Authorization Module]
    authA -->|Invalid| denyA[Access Denied]
    authzA -->|Policy Check| permitA{Action Allowed?}
    permitA -->|Yes| grantA[4. Grant Access]
    permitA -->|No| denyA
    grantA --> acctA[5. Accounting - Log Action]
    acctA --> resourceA[Resource Accessed]
```

## 4.4 Access Control Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA[Access Control Models]
    rootA --> classicalA[Classical Models]
    rootA --> modernA[Modern Models]
    classicalA --> daca[DAC - Discretionary]
    classicalA --> maca[MAC - Mandatory with BLP and Biba]
    classicalA --> rbaca[RBAC - Role Based]
    classicalA --> rulea[RuBAC - Rule Based]
    modernA --> abaca[ABAC - Attribute Based]
    modernA --> capa[CapBAC - Capability Based]
    modernA --> zta[Zero Trust Architecture]
    modernA --> trusta[TrustBAC - Trust Based]
```

## 4.5 RSA Key Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1A[Step 1 - Select Primes p and q] --> step2A[Step 2 - Compute n = p x q]
    step2A --> step3A[Step 3 - Compute phi n]
    step3A --> step4A[Step 4 - Choose e coprime to phi n]
    step4A --> step5A[Step 5 - Compute d as inverse of e mod phi n]
    step5A --> step6A[Output Public Key e n and Private Key d n]
```

## 4.6 Digital Signature Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender
    participant Receiver
    Sender->>Sender: Compute hash h = H of M
    Sender->>Sender: Sign sigma = Sign with K_priv
    Sender->>Receiver: Send M and sigma
    Receiver->>Receiver: Compute h_prime = H of M
    Receiver->>Receiver: Verify sigma with K_pub of Sender
    alt Valid
        Receiver->>Receiver: Integrity and Authenticity Confirmed
    else Invalid
        Receiver->>Receiver: Reject Message
    end
```

## 4.7 Hybrid Cryptosystem (TLS 1.3 in CPS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cli[Client e.g. SCADA HMI] -->|1. ClientHello| srv[Server e.g. PLC]
    srv -->|2. ServerHello plus Certificate| cli
    cli -->|3. Verify Cert with CA| ca[Certificate Authority]
    ca -->|4. Valid| cli
    cli -->|5. ECDHE Key Exchange| srv
    srv -->|6. Derive Shared Session Key K| srv
    cli -->|6. Derive Shared Session Key K| cli
    cli -->|7. Encrypt Bulk Data with AES-256-GCM using K| srv
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the three fundamental security properties known as the CIA Triad and how each is realized in a Cyber-Physical System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **CIA Triad** comprises:
1. **Confidentiality** — Ensures that sensitive data (e.g., patient vitals from a medical CPS, or control commands to a substation) is accessible only to authorized parties. Realized through **encryption** (AES, RSA, ECC).
2. **Integrity** — Ensures that data has not been tampered with during transmission. Realized through **hash functions** (SHA-256) and **digital signatures** (RSA, ECDSA).
3. **Availability** — Ensures that the CPS remains operational and accessible when needed. Realized through **redundancy, fault tolerance, and DoS-resistant protocols** (e.g., rate limiting, load balancing, intrusion detection).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking:** 1 mark per property + 0.5 mark for each CPS example + 0.5 mark for the realization technique. Total = **3 marks**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric key encryption with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
|---|---|---|
| **Keys Used** | Single shared secret key | Public-private key pair |
| **Speed** | Fast (suitable for bulk data) | Slow (suitable for small payloads) |
| **Examples** | AES, DES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC, Diffie-Hellman |
| **Key Distribution** | Difficult (must share secretly) | Easy (publish public key) |
| **CPS Use** | Encrypting sensor streams | Authenticating firmware updates |

**Example:** AES-256 is used to encrypt real-time telemetry from a wind turbine, while RSA-2048 is used by the central SCADA to sign firmware updates pushed to the turbine's controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking:** 1 mark for table content, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for CPS context. Total = **3 marks**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:**
**With neat diagrams, explain the AES encryption algorithm. State the block size, key sizes, and number of rounds.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** is a symmetric block cipher standardized by NIST in 2001 (FIPS PUB 197). It is based on the Rijndael algorithm designed by Joan Daemen and Vincent Rijmen. AES operates on a **128-bit block** organized as a **4×4 byte matrix** called the *State*.

**Parameters (1 mark):**

| Key Size (bits) | Rounds (Nr) | Round Key Size |
|---|---|---|
| 128 (AES-128) | 10 | 176 bytes |
| 192 (AES-192) | 12 | 208 bytes |
| 256 (AES-256) | 14 | 240 bytes |

**The Four AES Round Transformations (4 marks):**

1. **SubBytes** — Each byte in the State is replaced using a non-linear **Substitution Box (S-Box)** which is the multiplicative inverse in the Galois Field $\text{GF}(2^8)$ followed by an affine transformation. This provides *confusion*.

2. **ShiftRows** — The State rows are cyclically shifted left: Row 0 unchanged, Row 1 shifted by 1 byte, Row 2 by 2 bytes, Row 3 by 3 bytes. This provides *diffusion*.

3. **MixColumns** — Each column of the State is multiplied by a fixed polynomial $a(x) = \{03\}x^{3} + \{01\}x^{2} + \{01\}x + \{02\}$ over $\text{GF}(2^8)$. This provides inter-column diffusion.

4. **AddRoundKey** — Each byte of the State is XORed with the corresponding byte of the round key derived from the key schedule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In the final round, MixColumns is omitted** (only SubBytes, ShiftRows, AddRoundKey are performed).

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**
Plaintext → AddRoundKey (initial) → [SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey] repeated $N_r - 1$ times → [SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey] (final) → Ciphertext

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:**
**Perform RSA encryption and decryption for $M = 7$, given $p = 11$, $q = 13$, and $e = 7$. Show all steps.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute $n$:**
$$n = p \cdot q = 11 \times 13 = 143$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\phi(n)$:**
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = (11-1)(13-1) = 10 \times 12 = 120$$

**Step 3 — Verify $e$ is valid:**
$$\gcd(7, 120) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Compute private exponent $d$:**
We need $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$.

Using Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
$$120 = 17 \times 7 + 1$$

$$1 = 120 - 17 \times 7$$

$$-17 \times 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$$

$$d \equiv -17 \equiv 103 \pmod{120}$$

$$\therefore d = 103$$

**Step 5 — Encryption of $M = 7$:**
$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

$$C \equiv 7^{7} \pmod{143}$$

Computing stepwise:
$$7^{2} = 49$$

$$7^{4} = 49^{2} = 2401 = 16 \times 143 + 113 \implies 7^{4} \equiv 113 \pmod{143}$$

$$7^{7} = 7^{4} \times 7^{2} \times 7^{1}$$

$$\equiv 113 \times 49 \times 7 \pmod{143}$$

$$113 \times 49 = 5537$$

$$5537 = 38 \times 143 + 103 \implies 113 \times 49 \equiv 103 \pmod{143}$$

$$103 \times 7 = 721 = 5 \times 143 + 6 \implies 103 \times 7 \equiv 6 \pmod{143}$$

$$\therefore C = 6$$

**Step 6 — Decryption of $C = 6$:**
$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$$

$$M \equiv 6^{103} \pmod{143}$$

By Euler's theorem (or modular exponentiation tool), this recovers:

$$M \equiv 7 \pmod{143}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{Decrypted message} = 7 \quad \checkmark}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — RSA Errors:**
> - **Forgetting to verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$** — costs 0.5 mark.
> - **Computing $d$ as $\phi(n) - 17$ instead of $\phi(n) \mod$ inverse** — major error, -1 mark.
> - **Not stating Euler's theorem** when justifying correctness — -1 mark.
> - **Confusing $n$ with $\phi(n)$** in the modular inverse step — fatal error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:**
**Explain the Bell-LaPadula (BLP) model with its properties. How does it enforce confidentiality in CPS?**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Bell-LaPadula (BLP) model**, developed in 1973 by David Bell and Leonard LaPadula, is a formal state-machine model that captures the **confidentiality** requirements of access control in military and high-security systems. It is the foundational model for **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)** and is used in CPS contexts like nuclear plant SCADA, defense command systems, and classified data networks.

**Components (1 mark):**
- **Subjects** — Active entities (users, processes, IoT controllers).
- **Objects** — Passive entities (files, databases, sensor data).
- **Security Levels** — Hierarchical labels: e.g., *Unclassified < Confidential < Secret < Top Secret*.
- **Clearance vs Classification** — Subject's clearance vs Object's classification.

**The Three BLP Properties (4 marks):**

1. **Simple Security Property (No Read Up — NRU):**
   A subject $S$ can read object $O$ *only if*:
   $$\text{level}(S) \geq \text{level}(O)$$
   - *Meaning:* A Secret-cleared user CANNOT read Top Secret files. Prevents unauthorized information flow upward.

2. **Star Property / ⋆-Property (No Write Down — NWD):**
   A subject $S$ can write to object $O$ *only if*:
   $$\text{level}(S) \leq \text{level}(O)$$
   - *Meaning:* A Top Secret-cleared user CANNOT write down to a Secret file. Prevents *covert channel* leakage of high-classified data to lower classifications.

3. **Discretionary Security Property (ds-property):**
   Access must also be permitted by the access control matrix (per-object permission list). BLP layers this *on top of* MAC.

**CPS Application (1 mark):**
In a **nuclear power plant CPS**, telemetry from reactor sensors is labeled *Top Secret*; operator commands are labeled *Secret*. An operator's HMI workstation has *Secret* clearance, so it can:
- Read *Secret* logs (NRU satisfied).
- Send control commands to the reactor (NWD: Secret ≤ Top Secret ✓).

A malicious script attempting to exfiltrate *Top Secret* sensor data to a *Confidential* audit log would be blocked by the NWD property.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**
Subject (Clearance L_S) → [BLP Policy Engine] → Object (Classification L_O) → Decision: Grant/Deny

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:**
**Describe the ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) model. Compare it with RBAC. Show how ABAC supports Zero Trust Architecture in CPS.**

**Model Answer:**

**ABAC Definition (1.5 marks):**
**Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)** is a fine-grained, policy-driven access control model where authorization decisions are dynamically computed based on the evaluation of **attributes** associated with the subject, object, action, and environment.

Formal policy rule:
$$\text{Grant}(s, o, a) \iff \text{Policy} \big( A_{sub}, A_{obj}, A_{act}, A_{env} \big) = \text{True}$$

Where $A_{sub}$ = subject attributes, $A_{obj}$ = object attributes, $A_{act}$ = action attributes, $A_{env}$ = environment attributes (time, location, device trust score, etc.).

**ABAC Components (1.5 marks):**
- **Policy Decision Point (PDP)** — Evaluates the policy.
- **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** — Enforces the PDP's decision.
- **Policy Information Point (PIP)** — Retrieves attribute values.
- **Policy Administration Point (PAP)** — Manages policy creation.

**ABAC vs RBAC Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | RBAC | ABAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Basis** | Static role assignment | Dynamic attribute evaluation |
| **Granularity** | Coarse (per-role) | Fine-grained (per-context) |
| **Scalability of Permissions** | Role explosion problem | Solved by attributes |
| **Context-Awareness** | Limited | Native (time, location, device) |
| **Policy Expression** | Implicit (role-permission map) | Explicit (XACML, OPA) |
| **CPS Use Case** | Hospital staff roles | Smart building access based on shift, badge, and posture |

**ABAC in Zero Trust CPS (2 marks):**
The **Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207)** operates on the principle: *"Never trust, always verify."* ABAC is the *enforcement engine* of Zero Trust.

In a **smart factory CPS**:
- A maintenance technician requests access to a robotic arm controller.
- The PEP captures request context: technician ID, current shift (3 PM), IP range (plant VLAN), device posture (managed tablet, OS patched), behavior history.
- The PDP evaluates an ABAC policy: *"Maintenance role AND (shift between 6 AM–6 PM) AND (device is managed) AND (location inside plant) → Permit."*
- Access is granted *per session*, *per resource*, with continuous re-evaluation.

This dynamic, attribute-driven approach defeats **lateral movement**, **insider threats**, and **stolen-credential attacks** — all common CPS attack vectors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Access Control Models:**
> - **Writing "RBAC is better than ABAC" or vice-versa** — examiners want you to highlight *trade-offs*, not declare a winner.
> - **Confusing BLP (Confidentiality) with Biba (Integrity)** — Biba is NOT for confidentiality; it protects *integrity*. This is a classic 1.5-mark deduction.
> - **Omitting the BLP star property** when asked about MAC — costs 1 full mark.
> - **Forgetting environment attributes ($A_{env}$)** in ABAC — costs 1 mark. Many students only list subject/object attributes.
> - **Drawing BLP without security labels** — diagram carries 1 mark; must show clear level hierarchy.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1: Data Protection Techniques**

### Encryption Fundamentals
- **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (+ Authenticity + Non-Repudiation).
- **Symmetric:** Same key for $E$ and $D$. Fast, but key distribution problem. Examples: **AES, DES, ChaCha20**.
- **Asymmetric:** Public + Private key pair. Slow but solves key distribution. Examples: **RSA, ECC, DH**.
- **Hash:** One-way, fixed-length output. Properties: pre-image resistance, collision resistance, avalanche effect. Examples: **SHA-256, SHA-3, MD5 (broken)**.
- **Hybrid** = Asymmetric for key exchange + Symmetric for bulk data (used in **TLS 1.3, OPC UA**).

### RSA Key Generation (Always in this order!)
- Pick primes $p, q$ → Compute $n = p \cdot q$ → Compute $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ → Pick $e$ with $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ → Compute $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
- **Correctness:** $M \equiv C^{d} \equiv (M^{e})^{d} \pmod{n}$. Justified by **Euler's Theorem**.

### AES in a Nutshell
- 128-bit block, 128/192/256-bit keys, 10/12/14 rounds.
- Four round ops: **SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey** (final round skips MixColumns).
- Designed by **Daemen & Rijmen (Rijndael)**, adopted **NIST 2001**.

### Authentication
- **Factors:** Knowledge, Possession, Biometric, Location, Behavioral.
- **Protocols:** PAP, Challenge-Response, OTP (TOTP/HOTP), Kerberos (KDC + tickets), Public-Key (X.509).
- **Digital Signatures** = Hash + Asymmetric Encryption → provide **Authenticity + Integrity + Non-Repudiation**.

### Authorization & Access Control Models
- **DAC** = Owner decides (per-user).
- **MAC = BLP** for *confidentiality* (No Read Up, No Write Down); **Biba** for *integrity* (No Write Up, No Read Down).
- **RBAC** = Role → Permissions. Faces "role explosion" in large CPS.
- **ABAC** = Attribute-driven, dynamic, fine-grained. Foundation of **Zero Trust**.
- **CapBAC** = Capability tokens, ideal for IoT/edge CPS.
- **Zero Trust (NIST SP 800-207):** *Never trust, always verify.* Continuous authentication + least privilege + micro-segmentation.

### CPS-Specific High-Yield Phrases (Use in answers!)
- Smart Grid → IEC 61850 + GOOSE message authentication.
- Industrial IoT → OPC UA + X.509 + AES-256-GCM.
- Medical CPS → Biometric + HMAC + Emergency Override ABAC.
- Autonomous Vehicles → ECDSA / Ed25519 for V2X message signing.
- ROS 2 Robotics → SROS2 + DTLS + CapBAC.

### Top 5 High-Yield Mnemonics
- **AES Rounds:** **"S-S-M-A"** (SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey).
- **BLP:** **"No Read Up, No Write Down"** (confidentiality focus).
- **Biba:** **"No Write Up, No Read Down"** (integrity focus).
- **AAA:** **"AuthN → AuthZ → Acct"** (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting).
- **RSA:** **"p, q, n, φ, e, d"** (prime-prime-modulus-totient-pub-priv).
