# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) — The Foundation

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system in which computational algorithms (the *cyber* component) are tightly integrated with physical processes (the *physical* component). Sensors, actuators, and embedded controllers bridge the digital and physical worlds, forming a continuous feedback loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A CPS is a networked system of physical and computational components that sense, monitor, and control the physical world through tightly coupled, real-time feedback loops. The *cyber* domain handles computation, communication, and control logic; the *physical* domain handles actuation, sensing, and mechanical/electrical processes.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart House" View
Think of a modern **smart home** as a mini CPS. A temperature sensor (physical) reads the room temperature, sends the data to a microcontroller (cyber), which decides to switch on the air conditioner (physical actuator). The cyber and physical layers communicate continuously, just like the brain and body of a human.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CPS Security Matters:** Unlike traditional IT systems, a security failure in a CPS can cause *physical harm* — power grid blackouts, industrial equipment damage, water contamination, or even loss of human life. Hence, CPS security is fundamentally different and more critical than conventional cybersecurity.

---

## 1.2 Basic Concepts of Cybersecurity in the CPS Context

**Cybersecurity** refers to the practice of protecting systems, networks, programs, devices, and data from digital attacks, unauthorized access, damage, or disruption. In the CPS context, cybersecurity extends beyond the digital realm to ensure the **safety, reliability, and integrity of physical operations**.

### Core Terminology (Syllabus-Aligned Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | CPS-Specific Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Asset** | Anything of value to the system (data, hardware, software, processes) | Includes physical actuators, control software, sensor data |
| **Threat** | Any potential event that can cause harm to a system | A hacker injecting false sensor readings |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in the system that can be exploited | Unpatched firmware in a PLC |
| **Risk** | The likelihood and impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability | Equation: **Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact** |
| **Attack** | An intentional attempt to bypass security controls | A Stuxnet-style worm targeting industrial controllers |
| **Defense-in-Depth** | Layered security approach using multiple controls | Network segmentation + encryption + access control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of CPS Security Vocabulary — Memorize for KTU:**
> 1. **Asset** → What we protect
> 2. **Threat** → What can harm it
> 3. **Vulnerability** → The weakness that lets harm occur

---

## 1.3 The Security Goals: The CIA Triad

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model for information security, and it underpins ALL CPS security discussions. It consists of three interdependent goals:

### 🔒 **C — Confidentiality**
*Ensuring that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it.*

In CPS, confidentiality protects:
- Proprietary control algorithms
- Sensor data streams
- Network topology and device identifiers
- User authentication credentials

> **Example:** A SCADA system password transmitted in plaintext over an industrial network violates confidentiality.

### 🛡️ **I — Integrity**
*Ensuring that data and system configurations are accurate, consistent, and unaltered by unauthorized parties.*

In CPS, integrity protects:
- Sensor measurements (a tampered temperature reading can cause a furnace to overheat)
- Control commands sent to actuators
- Firmware and software on embedded devices
- Calibration parameters

> **Example:** Stuxnet modified the centrifuge spin frequency values while reporting normal readings to operators — a classic **integrity attack**.

### ⚙️ **A — Availability**
*Ensuring that systems, data, and services are accessible to authorized users whenever needed.*

In CPS, availability protects:
- Real-time control loops (latency can be catastrophic)
- Emergency shutdown systems
- Communication channels between sensors and controllers
- Power supply to critical control nodes

> **Example:** A **Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack** on a water treatment plant's control network prevents operators from adjusting chemical levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **CIA Triad Memory Trick for KTU:** "**C**an't **I**dle my **A**ctuators" — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. CPS without any one of these fails catastrophically.

---

## 1.4 Beyond CIA: Extended Security Properties for CPS

While CIA is the classic foundation, modern CPS security literature expands it. KTU expects students to recognize these additional properties:

| Property | Meaning | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Authenticity** | Verifying the identity of a sender | Ensuring a control command truly came from the authorized controller |
| **Non-repudiation** | Sender cannot deny having sent a message | Audit logs of actuator commands |
| **Accountability** | Actions are traceable to a specific entity | Logging who changed a PLC setpoint |
| **Privacy** | Protection of personally identifiable information | Smart meter consumption data revealing household behavior |
| **Safety** | System does not endanger human life or the environment | A medical infusion pump delivering correct dosage |
| **Real-time Guarantee** | Responses occur within bounded time | Anti-lock braking system reacting within milliseconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often treat safety and security as the same concept. **Security** is a precondition for **safety**, but they are distinct. A system can be secure but unsafe (e.g., a bug causing incorrect readings) or safe but insecure (e.g., a physically isolated legacy system).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a CPS — Where Security Must Be Applied

A typical CPS architecture contains **three layers**, and each layer has unique security requirements:

### Layer 1: Physical / Perception Layer
- **Components:** Sensors, actuators, RFID tags, embedded devices
- **Security Concerns:** Physical tampering, side-channel attacks, sensor spoofing, battery drain attacks
- **Example Attack:** Cloning an RFID tag used in access control

### Layer 2: Network / Communication Layer
- **Components:** Wired (Fieldbus, Ethernet) and wireless (ZigBee, LoRa, 5G, Wi-Fi) links
- **Security Concerns:** Eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle, jamming, replay attacks
- **Example Attack:** Jamming wireless signals to a fleet of autonomous vehicles

### Layer 3: Application / Control Layer
- **Components:** SCADA systems, HMIs (Human-Machine Interfaces), cloud analytics, control algorithms
- **Security Concerns:** Malware injection, unauthorized access, SQL injection, insider threats
- **Example Attack:** Stuxnet injecting malicious code into Siemens S7-300 PLCs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3-Layer Model is a KTU High-Yield Topic.** Examiners frequently test whether students can map a given attack to the correct layer.

---

## 2.2 CPS-Specific Threat Taxonomy

Threats in CPS differ from traditional IT threats because of their **safety-critical nature**, **real-time constraints**, and **legacy device ecosystem**. The following taxonomy is commonly referenced in KTU-aligned literature:

### 2.2.1 By Attack Surface
1. **Physical-layer Threats:** Hardware tampering, sensor/actuator manipulation
2. **Cyber-layer Threats:** Malware, unauthorized access, software exploits
3. **Network-layer Threats:** Eavesdropping, injection, replay, DoS
4. **Human-layer Threats:** Insider attacks, social engineering, operator error

### 2.2.2 By Attacker Capability
| Attacker Type | Resources | Typical Capability |
|---|---|---|
| **Script Kiddie** | Low | Run existing exploits |
| **Hacktivist** | Medium | DoS, defacement, data leaks |
| **Organized Crime** | High | Ransomware, financial fraud |
| **Nation-State** | Very High | Stuxnet-class, supply chain attacks |
| **Insider** | Variable | Knowledge of internal systems |

### 2.2.3 By Attack Strategy (STRIDE Model — KTU Favorite)
The **STRIDE** model, developed by Microsoft, classifies threats into six categories. This is **high-yield for KTU exams**:

| Letter | Threat | Property Violated |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | Authenticity |
| **T** | **Tampering** | Integrity |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | Non-repudiation |
| **I** | **Information Disclosure** | Confidentiality |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | Availability |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | Authorization |

> [!NOTE]
> **STRIDE ↔ CIA Mapping:** Each STRIDE threat maps to a violation of one CIA property or its extensions. KTU often asks students to identify which CIA property a given STRIDE threat violates.

---

## 2.3 CPS-Specific Vulnerabilities

CPS inherit all IT vulnerabilities but also have **unique** weaknesses:

### 2.3.1 Architectural Vulnerabilities
- **Legacy Systems:** Many industrial controllers run for 20–30 years without patches (e.g., Windows XP embedded)
- **Air-Gap Myth:** Believing physical isolation guarantees security (Stuxnet crossed the air gap via USB)
- **Resource Constraints:** Many IoT/CPS devices cannot run heavy encryption (CPU, memory, battery limits)
- **Heterogeneity:** Mixing protocols (Modbus, DNP3, Profinet, OPC-UA) creates integration vulnerabilities

### 2.3.2 Protocol Vulnerabilities
- **Modbus/DNP3:** No authentication, no encryption (designed in 1970s for closed networks)
- **ZigBee/Z-Wave:** Known key-management weaknesses
- **CAN bus (automotive):** No message authentication

### 2.3.3 Human & Operational Vulnerabilities
- Weak passwords on industrial HMIs
- Shared credentials among operators
- Lack of security training for plant operators
- Inadequate patch management

---

## 2.4 The Risk Equation — KTU Formula Sheet

The fundamental risk model used in CPS security analysis is:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk** (a measure of expected harm)
- $T$ = **Threat** (probability of a threat occurring, $0 \le T \le 1$)
- $V$ = **Vulnerability** (probability the vulnerability is exploitable, $0 \le V \le 1$)
- $I$ = **Impact** (severity of consequence, often measured in monetary loss or safety cost)

### 2.4.1 Risk Matrix (Qualitative)

| Threat Probability ↓ \ Impact → | Low (1) | Medium (2) | High (3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low (1)** | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| **Medium (2)** | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| **High (3)** | 3 | 6 | 9 |

> Risk score $= T \times I$, where values $1, 2, 3$ represent Low, Medium, High.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Treatment Options (after computing R):**
> 1. **Mitigation** — Reduce $T$, $V$, or $I$ through controls
> 2. **Transfer** — Shift risk to a third party (e.g., insurance)
> 3. **Acceptance** — Acknowledge and proceed (low-risk items)
> 4. **Avoidance** — Eliminate the risk by removing the asset or activity

### 2.4.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Table

| Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $R = T \times V \times I$ | Basic risk equation | Dimensionless score |
| $\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | System availability metric | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures; MTTR = Mean Time To Repair; result is a ratio (0 to 1) |
| $\text{SLA} \ge 1 - \text{downtime fraction}$ | Service Level Agreement | E.g., 99.999% ("five nines") = 5.26 min/year downtime |
| $\text{Attack Surface} = \sum \text{entry points}$ | Sum of all exploitable interfaces | Larger surface = higher risk |
| $\text{Defense in Depth} = \text{multiple independent layers}$ | Layered security principle | No single point of failure |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** For absolute value notation in equations, use $\lvert x \rvert$ — never $\vert x \vert$ inside markdown tables, as the pipe symbol breaks table rendering.

---

## 2.5 Mapping CIA to CPS: Engineering Trade-offs

In CPS design, the three CIA goals often **conflict**, creating engineering trade-offs. KTU expects awareness of these tensions:

| Trade-off | Description |
|---|---|
| **Confidentiality vs. Availability** | Heavy encryption increases latency, reducing real-time availability |
| **Integrity vs. Performance** | Cryptographic checksums on every sensor packet consume bandwidth |
| **Security vs. Safety** | Authentication timeouts can block legitimate emergency shutdowns |
| **Privacy vs. Functionality** | Smart meters reveal lifestyle; anonymization reduces utility for grid optimization |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not assume that achieving all three CIA goals is always possible simultaneously. CPS designers must prioritize based on use-case risk analysis. For example, in a life-critical medical device, **availability and integrity** typically outrank **confidentiality**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Risk Score Calculation (Quantitative)

> **Problem:** A water treatment plant CPS has:
> - Threat probability $T = 0.4$ (a known ransomware campaign targets the sector)
> - Vulnerability score $V = 0.6$ (the SCADA software is 2 years out of date)
> - Impact $I = \$1{,}000{,}000$ (estimated cost of a successful ransomware attack)
>
> **Find:** The annual risk exposure and the residual risk after applying a patch that reduces $V$ to $0.1$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Write down the risk equation.**

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

**Step 2: Substitute the original values to find the baseline risk.**

$$R_{\text{original}} = 0.4 \times 0.6 \times 1{,}000{,}000$$

**Step 3: Compute the product step by step.**

$$R_{\text{original}} = 0.24 \times 1{,}000{,}000$$

**Step 4: State the result with proper units.**

$$R_{\text{original}} = \$240{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 5: Compute the residual risk after patching ($V_{\text{new}} = 0.1$).**

$$R_{\text{residual}} = 0.4 \times 0.1 \times 1{,}000{,}000$$

**Step 6: Compute the product.**

$$R_{\text{residual}} = 0.04 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = \$40{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 7: Compute the risk reduction (delta) achieved by patching.**

$$\Delta R = R_{\text{original}} - R_{\text{residual}}$$

$$\Delta R = 240{,}000 - 40{,}000 = \$200{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This $\$200{,}000$ annual risk reduction justifies the patching cost, providing a clear ROI argument for security investment — exactly the kind of justification CPS security officers must present to plant managers.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Availability Computation

> **Problem:** A critical CPS server has:
> - $\text{MTBF} = 500$ hours
> - $\text{MTTR} = 5$ hours
>
> **Find:** The system availability as a ratio, as a percentage, and the annual downtime.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: State the availability formula.**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

$$A = \frac{500}{500 + 5}$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator.**

$$A = \frac{500}{505}$$

**Step 4: Compute the ratio.**

$$A \approx 0.990099$$

**Step 5: Convert to percentage.**

$$A_{\%} = 0.990099 \times 100 \approx 99.01\%$$

**Step 6: Compute the annual downtime fraction.**

$$D = 1 - A = 1 - 0.990099 = 0.009901$$

**Step 7: Convert to hours per year (8760 hours in a non-leap year).**

$$D_{\text{hours}} = 0.009901 \times 8760 \approx 86.7 \text{ hours per year}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A 99.01% availability ("two nines") translates to over **3.6 days of downtime per year** — unacceptable for a safety-critical CPS. Industrial systems often target **99.999% ("five nines")** = ~5.26 minutes/year downtime.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — STRIDE → CIA Mapping

> **Problem:** A KTU exam question gives four scenarios. Map each to the violated CIA property and the corresponding STRIDE threat.

### Analytical Matrix

| Scenario | Description | Violated Property | STRIDE Letter |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | An attacker copies an authorized user's RFID badge to access a restricted plant area | Authenticity → falls under **Confidentiality** of identity | **S** (Spoofing) |
| 2 | An attacker modifies the chemical dosing setpoint in a water treatment PLC | **Integrity** | **T** (Tampering) |
| 3 | A botnet floods the SCADA network, preventing operators from sending commands | **Availability** | **D** (DoS) |
| 4 | An operator denies having pressed the emergency stop button | **Non-repudiation** | **R** (Repudiation) |

> **KTU Tip:** Memorize the STRIDE → CIA mapping. The six STRIDE letters are almost always testable in either Part A (short answer) or Part B (full descriptive).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Threat vs. CPS Domain Mapping

> This matrix is **examiner-favorite** content. It tests whether a student can identify *where* a given attack occurs and *what* it affects.

| Attack Vector | CPS Layer Affected | CIA Property Compromised | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Replay of sensor data | Communication | Integrity | Adversary resends old "normal" temperature data |
| GPS spoofing | Perception | Integrity + Availability | Drone hijacked by fake GPS coordinates |
| Firmware modification | Application | Integrity | Stuxnet altering centrifuge code |
| Network jamming | Communication | Availability | Wireless disruption of smart grid sensors |
| Physical sensor destruction | Perception | Availability | Attacker cuts fiber optic cable |
| Insider credential theft | Application | Confidentiality + Authenticity | Snowden-style data exfiltration |
| Ransomware on HMIs | Application | Availability | Colonial Pipeline 2021 attack |
| Side-channel analysis | Physical | Confidentiality | Power-analysis to extract encryption keys |
| Supply chain implant | All layers | Integrity + Authenticity | Malicious chip in network hardware |

---

## 3.5 Threat Modeling Procedure (Step-by-Step)

KTU often asks for the **process** of threat modeling, not just definitions. The following 6-step procedure is the standard:

**Step 1 — Identify Assets**
List all critical CPS components: PLCs, sensors, actuators, network links, control algorithms, and data.

**Step 2 — Construct Architecture Diagram**
Document how data flows between sensors → controllers → actuators and external systems (cloud, operators).

**Step 3 — Identify Threats**
Apply the STRIDE model to each component and data flow.

**Step 4 — Identify Vulnerabilities**
For each threat, list the corresponding weakness (e.g., unpatched firmware, weak authentication).

**Step 5 — Assess Risk**
Use $R = T \times V \times I$ to quantify each threat-vulnerability pair.

**Step 6 — Recommend Countermeasures**
Propose controls: encryption, authentication, segmentation, intrusion detection, patching, etc.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the CIA Triad. Explain how each property applies uniquely to Cyber-Physical Systems with one example each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security comprising three goals:

1. **Confidentiality (1 mark):** Ensures that data is accessible only to authorized entities. *CPS Example:* Protecting proprietary control algorithms and sensor data from industrial espionage.

2. **Integrity (1 mark):** Ensures that data and configurations remain unaltered and accurate. *CPS Example:* Tamper-proofing the temperature sensor readings sent to a chemical reactor controller to prevent catastrophic reactions.

3. **Availability (1 mark):** Ensures that systems and data are accessible when needed. *CPS Example:* Maintaining real-time control of a power substation despite network latency or DoS attacks.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any three CPS-specific vulnerabilities and briefly explain why they differ from traditional IT vulnerabilities.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

1. **Legacy Systems (1 mark):** Industrial controllers often run for decades without patches. Unlike IT systems, replacing them is costly and disruptive to operations.

2. **Resource Constraints (1 mark):** CPS devices (sensors, embedded controllers) have limited CPU, memory, and battery, making standard encryption and authentication infeasible. IT systems have abundant resources.

3. **Real-Time Requirements (1 mark):** CPS demands bounded latency for control loops. IT security measures like heavy encryption introduce unacceptable delays.

> **Distinction:** Traditional IT vulnerabilities primarily affect *data and money*; CPS vulnerabilities can cause *physical damage and loss of life*.

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**A CPS is deployed in a smart power grid for managing electricity distribution. The system includes:**
- Smart meters at customer premises
- A SCADA control center
- Wireless communication links (ZigBee) between meters and concentrators
- Cloud-based analytics for demand forecasting

**(a) Identify and classify the major threats to this system using the STRIDE model. List at least 4 threats with their corresponding CPS-layer and violated security property. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss in detail the application of the CIA Triad to this smart grid CPS. For each property, propose two specific countermeasures. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

| STRIDE Threat | CPS Layer | Violated Property | Concrete Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S — Spoofing** | Communication (ZigBee) | Authenticity | Attacker spoofs a smart meter's identity to send false consumption data |
| **T — Tampering** | Application (SCADA) | Integrity | Modification of demand-response setpoints leading to grid imbalance |
| **R — Repudiation** | Application | Non-repudiation | Operator denies issuing a load-shedding command due to absent audit logs |
| **I — Information Disclosure** | Communication | Confidentiality | Eavesdropping on ZigBee traffic to learn household consumption patterns (privacy breach) |
| **D — Denial of Service** | Communication | Availability | Jamming ZigBee channels prevents meter data from reaching concentrators |
| **E — Elevation of Privilege** | Application | Authorization | Exploiting a vulnerability to gain SCADA administrator access |

**Explanation (1 mark for introduction, 1 mark per well-explained threat × 4 threats = 4 marks, 2 marks for table clarity and classification accuracy):**

The smart grid is exposed to threats across all three CPS layers. The wireless ZigBee link is particularly vulnerable due to its broadcast nature. The SCADA control center, often legacy, is a high-value target for nation-state attackers. Smart meters are physically accessible and can be tampered with to manipulate billing or grid forecasts.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

### Confidentiality in Smart Grid (2 marks)
- **Property Definition:** Protecting customer consumption data, grid topology, and control algorithms from unauthorized disclosure.
- **Countermeasure 1:** End-to-end encryption of ZigBee communication using AES-128 with rotating session keys.
- **Countermeasure 2:** Data anonymization and aggregation at the concentrator level before forwarding to cloud analytics, so individual household patterns are not revealed.

### Integrity in Smart Grid (2 marks)
- **Property Definition:** Ensuring that meter readings, control commands, and firmware are accurate and unaltered.
- **Countermeasure 1:** Use of Message Authentication Codes (MACs) on every meter packet so that tampered data is rejected.
- **Countermeasure 2:** Digital signatures on all SCADA control commands and periodic firmware integrity verification using cryptographic hashes.

### Availability in Smart Grid (2 marks)
- **Property Definition:** Ensuring that real-time grid control and customer service are accessible at all times.
- **Countermeasure 1:** Redundant communication paths (e.g., ZigBee + cellular fallback) and replicated SCADA servers.
- **Countermeasure 2:** Anti-jamming techniques such as frequency hopping spread spectrum (FHSS) and quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization for critical grid traffic.

### Conclusion (1 mark)
The CIA Triad must be applied holistically. A breach in any single property can cascade — e.g., a confidentiality breach exposing grid topology can enable a targeted DoS attack, demonstrating the interdependence of CIA properties.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Explain the concept of a Cyber-Physical System. With the help of a neat block diagram, describe its three-layer architecture and identify two security threats specific to each layer. (7 marks)**

**(b) Define Risk in CPS security. Compute the annual risk exposure for the following scenario and recommend two countermeasures: A hospital's infusion pump system has T = 0.5, V = 0.8, and I = ₹50,00,000. After applying recommended controls, V reduces to 0.2. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

### Definition of CPS (1 mark)
A Cyber-Physical System is an integration of computation, networking, and physical processes. Embedded computers and networks monitor and control the physical processes, usually with feedback loops where physical processes affect computations and vice versa.

### Three-Layer Architecture (3 marks for diagram + explanation)

```
[ Layer 3: Application Layer ]
       (SCADA, HMI, Cloud, Control Logic)
                    ↕
[ Layer 2: Network Layer ]
       (Wired/Wireless, Fieldbus, ZigBee, 5G)
                    ↕
[ Layer 1: Perception/Physical Layer ]
       (Sensors, Actuators, RFID, Embedded Devices)
```

**Perception Layer:** Interacts directly with the physical world via sensors and actuators. It senses environmental variables (temperature, pressure, motion) and executes physical actions.

**Network Layer:** Handles data transmission between perception and application layers using wired (Ethernet, CAN, Modbus) or wireless (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, 5G) protocols.

**Application Layer:** Hosts the control algorithms, analytics, user interfaces, and decision-making logic. This is where business and operational decisions are made.

### Two Threats per Layer (3 marks)

- **Perception Layer Threats:** (1) Physical tampering of sensors; (2) Side-channel attacks extracting cryptographic keys from embedded devices.
- **Network Layer Threats:** (1) Eavesdropping on unencrypted wireless traffic; (2) Jamming or interference attacks on radio frequency.
- **Application Layer Threats:** (1) Malware injection (e.g., Stuxnet); (2) Insider threats abusing privileged access.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

### Definition of Risk (2 marks)

Risk in CPS security is the **probability and severity of harm** resulting from a threat exploiting a vulnerability. The fundamental risk equation is:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

Where $T$ = Threat probability, $V$ = Vulnerability score, $I$ = Impact value.

### Original Risk Calculation (2 marks)

$$R_{\text{original}} = 0.5 \times 0.8 \times 50{,}00{,}000$$

$$R_{\text{original}} = 0.4 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = ₹20{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

### Residual Risk Calculation (1 mark)

$$R_{\text{residual}} = 0.5 \times 0.2 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = 0.1 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = ₹5{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

### Recommended Countermeasures (2 marks)

1. **Firmware Patching and Hardening (reduces V):** Implement a regular patch management cycle, disable unused services, and use a secure boot process. This is what caused the reduction from $V = 0.8$ to $V = 0.2$.

2. **Network Segmentation and Authentication:** Isolate the infusion pump network from the hospital's general IT network using VLANs and firewalls. Implement strong two-factor authentication for accessing the pump management interface.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing T, V, and I:** Many students mix up these variables. Remember: *T* = how often the attack happens, *V* = how easy it is to succeed, *I* = how bad it is if it succeeds.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** Always state the currency and time period (e.g., ₹ per year).
> 3. **Skipping the Reassessment Step:** Risk management is iterative. Always recompute residual risk after applying controls.
> 4. **Treating CIA as a Checklist:** Examiners award marks for **understanding trade-offs**, not just listing three properties. Always add an "interdependence" or "conclusion" sentence.
> 5. **No Real-World Tie-In:** KTU values applied answers. Mentioning Stuxnet, Colonial Pipeline, or Mirai botnet earns bonus marks.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **CPS Definition:** Integration of cyber (computation/communication) and physical (sensing/actuation) components with real-time feedback loops.
- ✅ **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality (only authorized access), Integrity (data unaltered), Availability (accessible when needed).
- ✅ **Extended Properties:** Authenticity, Non-repudiation, Accountability, Privacy, Safety, Real-time guarantees.
- ✅ **Three-Layer CPS Architecture:** Perception → Network → Application. Each layer has unique threats.
- ✅ **STRIDE Model:** Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- ✅ **STRIDE → CIA Mapping:** Spoofing→Authenticity, Tampering→Integrity, Repudiation→Non-repudiation, Info Disclosure→Confidentiality, DoS→Availability, Elevation→Authorization.
- ✅ **Risk Equation:** $R = T \times V \times I$. Always state units and recompute residual risk.
- ✅ **Availability Formula:** $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$. Five nines = 99.999% ≈ 5.26 min/year.
- ✅ **CPS-Specific Vulnerabilities:** Legacy systems, resource constraints, air-gap myth, insecure legacy protocols (Modbus, DNP3, CAN), real-time requirements conflicting with security.
- ✅ **Risk Treatment:** Mitigation, Transfer, Acceptance, Avoidance.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth:** Multiple independent security layers; no single point of failure.
- ✅ **Security ≠ Safety:** Security is a precondition for safety, but they are distinct concepts.
- ✅ **Real-World Anchors:** Stuxnet (integrity), Colonial Pipeline (availability), Mirai botnet (IoT), Ukraine power grid 2015 (multiple CIA).
- ✅ **Trade-offs:** Confidentiality ↔ Availability, Integrity ↔ Performance, Security ↔ Safety.
- ✅ **Human Factor:** Insider threats and operator errors are often the weakest link.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure Communication in CPS: Secure Communication Protocols, Cryptographic Algorithms and Key Management, Wireless and Network Security Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT823 - SECURITY AND PRIVACY IN CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Secure Communication in CPS: Secure Communication Protocols, Cryptographic Algorithms and Key Management, Wireless and Network Security Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Secure Communication in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

### Formal KTU Definition
**Secure Communication in CPS** refers to the set of protocols, cryptographic mechanisms, and key management procedures that ensure **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, non-repudiation, and freshness** of data exchanged between cyber components (sensors, actuators, controllers, edge/fog/cloud nodes) and physical processes in a Cyber-Physical System. In the KTU 2024 OECPT823 syllabus, it is positioned as the *transport-layer and information-layer defence* that complements hardware-level trust roots and physical-layer isolation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> A CPS is *secure* only when its **Communication Plane**, **Computation Plane**, and **Physical Plane** are simultaneously protected. Secure communication safeguards the *Communication Plane* and indirectly protects the *Physical Plane* (since a hijacked command can detonate a turbine, drain a dam, or mis-dose a patient).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Diplomatic Pouch"
Imagine two embassies (a **field sensor** in a refinery and a **central SCADA controller**) exchanging documents:

| Diplomatic World | CPS Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Sealed tamper-proof bag | **TLS/DTLS tunnel** | Confidentiality + Integrity |
| Wax seal with unique crest | **MAC / Digital Signature** | Integrity + Authentication |
| Courier with verified passport | **X.509 / Pre-shared key handshake** | Entity Authentication |
| One-time codebook per mission | **Session key derived via ECDH** | Forward Secrecy |
| Embassy guard checking bag weight | **Sequence number + freshness check** | Anti-replay protection |
| Trusted notary stamping the document | **PKI / CA hierarchy** | Non-repudiation |

If any of these are missing, an attacker can *eavesdrop, impersonate, replay, modify, or forge* the messages — turning the refinery into a cyber-weapon.

---

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Secure CPS Communication (CIA + F)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test these four properties in 3-mark Part-A questions. Memorize the acronym **AICA-F**: **A**uthentication, **I**ntegrity, **C**onfidentiality, **A**vailability, **F**reshness.

1. **Authentication** — Proving the *identity* of the sender (sensor, controller, actuator, gateway). 
2. **Integrity** — Detecting any *modification* of payload bits during transit (MAC, AEAD, signature).
3. **Confidentiality** — Hiding *semantic content* from eavesdroppers (AES, ChaCha20).
4. **Freshness (Anti-Replay)** — Guaranteeing that an old, captured message **cannot** be re-injected to influence current physical state.
5. (Often added) **Non-Repudiation** — Sender cannot later deny issuing the command.

---

## 1.3 Secure Communication Protocols Used in CPS

A *protocol* is a **stateful, multi-round exchange** of messages — not just an algorithm. KTU 2024 Module 2 emphasises the following families:

| Layer (OSI) | Protocol | CPS Use-Case | Security Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application | **MQTT over TLS 1.3** | IoT-to-Cloud telemetry | X.509 certs, AEAD |
| Application | **CoAP over DTLS 1.3** | Constrained 6LoWPAN nodes | PSK / Raw Public Key |
| Transport | **DTLS 1.3** (Datagram TLS) | UDP-based control loops | Cookie-based DoS defence |
| Network | **IPsec / IKEv2** | Substation-to-control-center | ESP tunnel + AH |
| Network | **RPL + DODAG authentication** | 6LoWPAN mesh (IEEE 802.15.4) | MIC with network key |
| Link | **IEEE 802.1X / MACsec** | Wired fieldbus (EtherCAT) | Port-based NAC |
| Link | **IEEE 802.15.4e / 4g** | WirelessHART, ISA 100.11a | Time-Slotted Channel Hopping + AES-CCM |
| Industrial | **OPC UA over UA TCP** | SCADA ↔ PLC | X.509 + SignedAndEncrypted |
| Automotive | **CANsec / SecOC** (AUTOSAR) | In-vehicle CAN-FD | CMAC + Freshness counter |
| Fieldbus | **PROFINET Security Class 1** | Discrete manufacturing | TLS + integrity CRL |

---

## 1.4 Cryptographic Algorithms — The Building Blocks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU differentiates **Primitives (algorithms)** from **Protocols (exchanges)**. A primitive transforms bits; a protocol exchanges messages.

### 1.4.1 Symmetric-Key Primitives
- **AES-128/256** (block cipher) — used in **GCM, CCM, GCM-SIV** modes.
- **ChaCha20-Poly1305** — software-friendly AEAD for ARM Cortex-M nodes.
- **3DES / SM4** — legacy / Chinese national standard.

### 1.4.2 Asymmetric-Key Primitives
- **RSA-2048/3072** — slow, used only for *signatures* and *key transport* in CPS gateways.
- **ECDH (Curve25519, P-256, secp256k1)** — fast, used for *key agreement*.
- **ECDSA / Ed25519** — compact signatures for firmware updates and signed telemetry.

### 1.4.3 Hash & MAC Functions
- **SHA-256, SHA-3-256, BLAKE2s** — collision-resistant hashing.
- **HMAC-SHA-256** — keyed integrity.
- **CMAC, GMAC, Poly1305** — MACs inside AEAD suites.

### 1.4.4 Key-Derivation Functions
- **HKDF (RFC 5869)**, **SP 800-108**, **KBKDF** — turn raw shared secrets into multiple session keys.

---

## 1.5 Key Management — The Hardest Problem

> [!CAUTION]
> KTU examiners note: *"Cryptography is math; key management is operations. Real CPS attacks (Stuxnet, Industroyer, Pipedream) succeed by stealing keys, not breaking AES."*

**Key Management** encompasses:
1. **Key Generation** — using **TRNG / DRBG** (NIST SP 800-90A) tied to physical entropy (silicon jitter, shot noise).
2. **Key Establishment / Agreement** — pre-shared (PSK), key transport (RSA-OAEP), key agreement (ECDH, DH).
3. **Key Distribution & Storage** — Hardware Security Modules (HSMs), TPM 2.0, Secure Elements (SE), Trusted Execution Environments (TEE), Physically Unclonable Functions (PUF).
4. **Key Rotation & Update** — forward-secure ratchets, key-update protocols.
5. **Key Revocation & Destruction** — OCSP, CRLs, zeroisation on tamper detection.
6. **Key Backup & Escrow** — required for incident recovery, but a liability if compromised.
7. **Symmetric vs Asymmetric Lifecycle** — symmetric keys are short-lived (per-session); asymmetric keys are long-lived (device identity, 5–20 years).

---

## 1.6 Wireless and Network Security Considerations

CPS increasingly relies on **wireless** and **IP-routed** paths. Each adds unique threats:

| Network Type | CPS Deployment | Wireless-Specific Threats | Counter-Measures |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11ax)** | Factory floor AGVs, hospital carts | Rogue AP, KRACK, evil twin | WPA3-Enterprise, 802.1X, PMF |
| **Bluetooth / BLE** | Wearables, robotics | BlueBorne, KNOB attack | LE Secure Connections, numeric comparison |
| **Zigbee / Z-Wave** | Smart buildings, AMI meters | Replay, key extraction from Touchlink | Network-key rotation, install codes |
| **WirelessHART / ISA100** | Process plants | Jamming, DoS | TSCH + channel hopping + AES-CCM |
| **LoRaWAN** | Wide-area CPS sensing | Bit-flipping, replay | AppSKey + DevNonce + frame counter |
| **NB-IoT / LTE-M** | Smart grid, asset tracking | IMSI catching, downgrade | Mutual auth, SUPI concealment |
| **5G URLLC** | V2X, remote surgery | False base-station, slicing leak | SUCI, NEF authentication |
| **Satellite (LEO/MEO)** | Pipeline monitoring, OT | Beam squinting, replay | DTLS + global PKI |
| **Wired (Ethernet, CAN, RS-485)** | In-vehicle, substations | Bus-off attacks, ARP poisoning | MACsec, CANsec, VLAN, NAC |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva**: *"In wireless CPS, the air is the attacker"* — always model **jamming, eavesdropping, injection, replay, and MitM** simultaneously. Use **defence-in-depth**: physical-layer security (beamforming, spread spectrum) + link-layer crypto (AES-CCM) + network-layer (IPsec) + application-layer (MQTT over TLS 1.3).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Threat Model for CPS Communication

A *threat model* explicitly enumerates **who** can do **what** to **which asset** with **what capability**. The standard KTU 2024 treatment is the **Dolev–Yao model** extended with a **physical-attacker** capability:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dolev–Yao Attacker (1983)**
> The attacker controls the network completely — can eavesdrop on, intercept, modify, replay, inject, and re-order any message. End systems are honest. This is the baseline threat model for TLS, DTLS, IPsec, and MQTT security proofs.

For CPS, KTU adds three extra adversarial powers:
1. **Physical / proximity attacker** — within RF range, can jam, replay RF, sniff OTA firmware.
2. **Compromised insider node** — legitimate sensor with valid key behaving maliciously.
3. **Side-channel attacker** — observes power, EM emanations, timing, acoustics of the crypto device.

---

## 2.2 Symmetric Encryption & AEAD — Theory

### 2.2.1 Block-Cipher Modes for CPS
- **ECB (Electronic Code Book)** — *never used in CPS*; equal plaintext blocks produce equal ciphertext blocks, leaking patterns (e.g., actuator OFF vs ON).
- **CTR (Counter Mode)** — turns block cipher into stream cipher; needs unique nonce per (key, message).
- **GCM (Galois/Counter Mode)** — AEAD: provides confidentiality + integrity in one pass; **nonce must never repeat** under same key (catastrophic key-recovery attack).
- **CCM (Counter with CBC-MAC)** — AEAD common in 802.15.4, WirelessHART, BLE.
- **ChaCha20-Poly1305** — ARX-based, constant-time, excellent for ARM Cortex-M0/M3 constrained nodes.

### 2.2.2 Why CPS Prefers AEAD
AEAD gives *one-pass* authenticated encryption, which suits real-time control loops where **latency budget ≤ 10 ms** (e.g., IEC 61850 GOOSE messages) and the device has **< 64 KB RAM**.

### 2.2.3 Authentication Modes
- **MAC-then-Encrypt** — SSH-style; vulnerable to padding-oracle attacks.
- **Encrypt-then-MAC** — IPsec ESP; secure.
- **Encrypt-and-MAC** — TLS 1.2 (legacy); secure but heavy.
- **AEAD single primitive** — TLS 1.3, DTLS 1.3; the modern standard.

---

## 2.3 Asymmetric Cryptography in CPS

### 2.3.1 RSA — Used Sparingly
- **Key generation**: pick two primes $p,q$; compute $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, choose $e$ with $\gcd(e,\phi)=1$, set $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
- **Encryption / decryption**: $c \equiv m^e \pmod n$, $m \equiv c^d \pmod n$.
- **Signature**: $s \equiv m^d \pmod n$, verify $m \equiv s^e \pmod n$.
- **CPS use**: only on gateways, HSMs, or during commissioning (slow: ~1 ms sign, ~30 ms verify on Cortex-A53).

### 2.3.2 ECC — The CPS Favourite
- **ECDH key agreement**: Alice sends $aG$, Bob sends $bG$, shared = $abG$.
- **ECDSA signature**: pair $(r,s)$; compact, ~64 bytes for P-256, ~32 for Ed25519.
- **Ed25519 (EdDSA)**: deterministic, fast, side-channel-resistant; ideal for firmware signing.

### 2.3.3 Post-Quantum (Emerging, KTU 2024 Awareness)
- **NIST PQC standards (FIPS 203/204/205)**: ML-KEM (Kyber), ML-DSA (Dilithium), SLH-DSA (SPHINCS+).
- **CPS migration**: hybrid schemes (e.g., X25519+ML-KEM-768) are recommended in TLS 1.3 drafts for long-lived CPS (smart-grid, V2X).

---

## 2.4 Key Management Theory

### 2.4.1 Key Hierarchy (Industrial Standard)

```
Master Key (MK)         — lives in HSM/TPM, never leaves hardware
  └── Key Encryption Key (KEK)
        ├── Device Key (DK)         — per device, long-term
        │     └── Session Key (SK)  — per session, short-term
        └── Update Key (UK)
```

### 2.4.2 Key Lifecycle States
1. **Pre-activation** — generated but unused
2. **Active** — used in production
3. **Deactivated** — old key retained for verification
4. **Destroyed / Zeroised** — erased, no recovery
5. **Compromised** — leaked; must revoke and rekey

### 2.4.3 Key Establishment Protocols in CPS
- **Pre-Shared Key (PSK)** — symmetric, smallest footprint; used in IEEE 802.15.4, BLE Legacy.
- **Raw Public Key (RPK)** — DTLS 1.3 mode, no cert chain.
- **Public-Key Infrastructure (PKI)** — X.509 certs from a CPS-aware CA.
- **ECDH + Authentication (ECDH-PSK, ECDHE-PSK, ECDHE-RSA, ECDHE-ECDSA)** — TLS 1.3.
- **PAKE (Password-Authenticated Key Exchange)** — SRP, OPAQUE — for human-set passwords on CPS.
- **Group Key Management** — GDOI, GSAKMP, MARC — for WirelessHART, IP multicast commands.

---

## 2.5 Wireless & Network Security — Theory

### 2.5.1 Wireless Attack Taxonomy
- **Passive**: eavesdropping, traffic analysis, RF fingerprinting.
- **Active**: injection, replay, rogue AP, jamming, MitM.
- **Physical-layer**: scrambling, key extraction from side-channels.

### 2.5.2 The CIA-Triad Mapped to Wireless CPS
| Attack Class | Confidentiality Impact | Integrity Impact | Availability Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eavesdropping | High | None | None |
| Bit-flipping | None | High | Medium |
| Replay | Low | High | Medium |
| Jamming | None | None | High |
| Rogue AP / MitM | High | High | High |
| Compromised sensor | High | High | High |

### 2.5.3 Wireless-Specific Crypto Requirements
- **Low power** (µW–mW for battery CPS nodes).
- **Small code size** (≤ 32 KB flash).
- **Fast wake-up** (≤ 1 ms handshake after sleep).
- **Robust to packet loss** (DTLS 1.3 stateless cookie exchange).
- **Resistant to side-channel** (constant-time, masking, TEE).

### 2.5.4 Jamming Defence
- **Spread spectrum** (FHSS, DSSS).
- **Time-Slotted Channel Hopping (TSCH)** — IEEE 802.15.4e.
- **Frequency hopping** — Bluetooth AFH, WirelessHART.
- **Beamforming & MIMO** — Wi-Fi 6E, 5G NR.
- **Powerful error-correcting codes** — LDPC, Polar codes.

### 2.5.5 Network Layer Security in CPS
- **IPsec ESP + AH** — tunnel mode for backhaul, transport mode for edge.
- **MACsec (802.1AE)** — link-layer, ideal for substation Ethernet.
- **RPL security** (RFC 6550 + RFC 9008) — secures 6LoWPAN routing.
- **VLAN + NAC (802.1X)** — segmentation in industrial Ethernet.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat on the table below:** KTU 2024 OECPT823 is a *qualitative + design* paper. Quantitative crypto formulas are limited to *complexity-bound* reasoning (used in viva / 14-mark design questions), not heavy numerical computation. The table is for *concepts and metric recall*.

| # | Concept / Formula | Symbol | Meaning | Typical CPS Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AES security level | $k$ | Key length in bits | **128, 192, 256** | 256 bits = top secret / long-life |
| 2 | Brute-force ops | $2^k$ | Work to break symmetric key | $2^{128}$ | Computationally infeasible |
| 3 | RSA modulus strength | $n = pq$ | Modulus size | **2048–3072 bits** | Equivalent to ~112–128-bit security |
| 4 | ECC equivalent security | EC size | Curve order | **256 bits (P-256, Curve25519)** | Equivalent to RSA-3072 |
| 5 | Hash collision resistance | $2^{h/2}$ | Birthday attack work | SHA-256: $2^{128}$ | NIST post-2010 standard |
| 6 | Pre-image resistance | $2^{h}$ | Work to invert hash | SHA-256: $2^{256}$ | One-way property |
| 7 | HMAC tag length | $\tau$ | MAC bits | **64, 96, 128 bits** | 96 bits standard in TLS |
| 8 | AEAD tag length | $\tau$ | Authentication tag | 128 bits (GCM, Poly1305) | Never truncate below 64 |
| 9 | Nonce length | $n$ | AEAD IV size | **96 bits** for GCM | Must be unique per (key) |
| 10 | Key agreement entropy | $H(K)$ | Bits of shared secret | ≥ **128 bits** | From ECDH output |
| 11 | Key lifecycle duration | $T_K$ | Active key period | session: 1 hr; device: 1–10 yr | Shorter = stronger forward secrecy |
| 12 | PKI chain depth | $d$ | Cert chain | 3–4 levels (Root → Sub → Device) | Deeper = higher overhead |
| 13 | Replay window | $W$ | Anti-replay window | sequence number 64-bit | Wrap-around in years |
| 14 | Latency budget | $L$ | CPS control loop | **1–10 ms** (GOOSE), **100 ms** (HTTP) | Crypto must fit |
| 15 | Energy per auth | $E$ | J/op | 1–10 µJ for AES-CCM | Critical for battery nodes |
| 16 | TLS 1.3 round-trips | 1-RTT | Handshake | **1-RTT (0-RTT resumption)** | Faster than TLS 1.2 (2-RTT) |
| 17 | DTLS cookie | $\kappa$ | DoS defence bits | ≥ 128 bits | Stateless for server |
| 18 | PUF entropy | $H_{PUF}$ | Bits from physical unclonable function | 128–256 bits | Used as root-of-trust |
| 19 | Jamming margin | $J$ | dB above noise | ≥ 10 dB | To overcome interference |
| 20 | Information leakage (side-channel) | $\epsilon$ | Bits per trace | **0** (ideal constant-time) | Violation → key extract |

### 2.6.1 KTU Viva Short-List
- **Dolev–Yao model** — controls network, not endpoints.
- **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** — past sessions stay secret even if long-term key leaks; requires (EC)DHE per session.
- **AEAD security bound** — confidentiality = IND-CCA, integrity = INT-CTXT.
- **Key hierarchy** — MK > KEK > DK > SK; MK *never* leaves HSM.
- **Diffie–Hellman problem** — given $g^a, g^b$, computing $g^{ab}$ is hard (CDH assumption).
- **Side-channel attack classes** — timing, power (DPA/SPA), EM, cache, acoustic, fault injection.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RSA Modulus & Security Relationship

The **equivalence between RSA modulus size and symmetric key size** is a *direct consequence* of the best-known integer factorisation algorithm (General Number Field Sieve, GNFS). KTU examiners like this derivation because it justifies **why ECC is preferred in CPS**.

**Goal**: Show that to achieve $\lambda$-bit security (i.e., attacker needs $2^\lambda$ operations to break), RSA needs $\approx 6.3\lambda$ bits and ECC needs $\approx 2\lambda$ bits.

### Step 1 — Define $\lambda$-bit security
A system has $\lambda$-bit security if breaking it requires $\Theta(2^\lambda)$ elementary operations.

### Step 2 — RSA security
The best attack against RSA is to factor $n = pq$. The GNFS has sub-exponential cost:

$$
L_n\left[\tfrac{1}{3}, c\right] = \exp\left( \left( c + o(1) \right) (\ln n)^{1/3} (\ln \ln n)^{2/3} \right)
$$

For 128-bit security ($\lambda = 128$), the NIST recommendation (SP 800-57) is $n \ge 3072$ bits. So we obtain the empirical ratio:

$$
n \approx 24 \times \lambda \quad \text{(for } 80 \le \lambda \le 128 \text{)}
$$

More precisely (NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{RSA}}(\lambda) &\approx 6.3\,\lambda \;\;\text{bits (approx. linear)} \\
\text{or}\;\; \lambda &= \tfrac{1}{6.3}\,n_{\text{RSA}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\lambda = 128$:

$$
n_{\text{RSA}}(128) \approx 6.3 \times 128 \approx 806 \text{ bits (rough); refined by NIST to 3072 bits for } \ge 128 \text{-bit security due to GNFS constants.}
$$

For **examination purposes**, KTU accepts the *rule of thumb*:

> **RSA-2048 ≈ 112-bit security; RSA-3072 ≈ 128-bit security; RSA-15360 ≈ 256-bit security.**

### Step 3 — ECC security
The best attack against ECC is Pollard's rho for the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP), which is fully exponential:

$$
T_{\text{ECDLP}} \approx \sqrt{q} = 2^{\lambda/2}
$$

To achieve $\lambda$-bit security, the curve order $q$ must satisfy $q \ge 2^{2\lambda}$. Thus for 128-bit security, we need a 256-bit curve. Hence:

$$
n_{\text{EC}}(\lambda) = 2\lambda \quad \text{bits}
$$

### Step 4 — Comparative result

$$
\frac{n_{\text{RSA}}(\lambda)}{n_{\text{EC}}(\lambda)} = \frac{6.3\,\lambda}{2\,\lambda} \approx 3.15
$$

So **ECC gives the same security as RSA with ~3× shorter keys** — saving bandwidth, RAM, and energy in CPS.

### Step 5 — Operational meaning for CPS
- **Bandwidth saving**: a 256-bit ECDH public key vs a 3072-bit RSA public key → 12× smaller messages.
- **Latency saving**: ECDH keygen on Cortex-M4 ≈ 200 ms vs RSA-2048 keygen ≈ 6 s.
- **Energy saving**: ~30× less energy per handshake, critical for battery CPS.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU marker tip**: In a 14-mark design question, justify your choice of ECC over RSA in CPS by *quantitatively* citing the ratio above plus one operational metric (latency, energy, or bandwidth).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — AEAD Nonce-Uniqueness Requirement

**Why must the nonce never repeat under the same key?**

Consider AES-GCM with key $K$ and two distinct messages $M_1, M_2$ encrypted with the **same** 96-bit nonce $N$. Let $H = E_K(0^{128})$ be the hash key. GCM uses $N$ as the initial counter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_0 &= N \,\|\, 0^{31} \,\|\, 1 \quad \text{(96-bit nonce case)} \\
C_1 &= M_1 \oplus E_K(J_0) \\
C_2 &= M_2 \oplus E_K(J_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

An attacker who sees $C_1, C_2$ immediately recovers:

$$
C_1 \oplus C_2 = M_1 \oplus M_2
$$

So confidentiality of the *difference* $M_1 \oplus M_2$ is lost. Worse, if $M_1$ is known, $M_2 = M_1 \oplus C_1 \oplus C_2$ is fully revealed.

For the authentication tag, the polynomial hash key $H$ is the same, so forgery becomes feasible. Joux's 2006 attack: **a single nonce reuse recovers the GHASH key $H$ in $O(1)$ work**, allowing arbitrary forgeries. Hence:

> **AES-GCM is catastrophically broken on nonce reuse — equivalent to publishing the key.**

**Design rule for CPS**: generate nonces from a 128-bit CTR-DRBG *with per-device state* (RFC 5116, RFC 8983) and never from a global counter across reboots.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Forward-Secrecy Bound (PFS)

A protocol offers **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** if compromise of the long-term private key $sk_{LT}$ does *not* reveal past session keys.

Let:
- $sk_{LT}$ = long-term private key (ECDSA or RSA).
- $E = g^{a}$ = ephemeral ECDH public key.
- $SS = g^{ab}$ = shared session secret.

Suppose $sk_{LT}$ is compromised at time $t_c$. To break session at time $t_s < t_c$, the attacker must solve the **Computational Diffie–Hellman (CDH) problem**: given $(g, g^a, g^b)$, compute $g^{ab}$. The best algorithm is Pollard's rho in $O(\sqrt{q})$ steps. For 256-bit curve:

$$
T_{\text{break}}(q) = \sqrt{q} = \sqrt{2^{256}} = 2^{128} \text{ operations.}
$$

Hence past session keys remain **128-bit secure** even after $sk_{LT}$ leakage. This is the formal definition of PFS.

> [!NOTE]
> **TLS 1.3 enforces PFS by mandating (EC)DHE in every handshake.** Static RSA key transport is *removed* from TLS 1.3 — this is the single biggest cryptographic change in the standard, and KTU 2024 explicitly tests it.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — WirelessHART / 802.15.4e Frame Counter for Anti-Replay

In 802.15.4, the **frame counter** is a 4-byte (32-bit) field incremented per packet. Probability that an attacker can replay a frame within the receiver's anti-replay window of size $w$ is:

$$
P_{\text{collision}} = 1 - \prod_{i=0}^{w-1}\left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{32} - i}\right) \approx \frac{w(w-1)}{2 \times 2^{32}}
$$

For $w = 1000$:

$$
P_{\text{collision}} \approx \frac{10^6}{2^{33}} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{-4}
$$

This is *too high* for safety-critical CPS. Hence **WirelessHART enforces a duplicate-detection window = 0** (only the exact counter is accepted), and the counter itself is 32-bit. Wrap-around in $\approx 2^{32} \times T_{\text{pkt}}$ seconds = $2^{32} \times 0.1$ s ≈ **13.6 years** at 10 Hz — acceptable.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Energy Cost of Authenticated Encryption (CPS Battery Budget)

A typical CPS node (e.g., NRF52840 Cortex-M4) consumes:
- AES-128 ECB block: 1 cycle / byte (with hardware accelerator).
- AES-GCM: ~1 cycle / byte + GHASH.

For a 64-byte payload with 16-byte tag, total work = 80 byte-blocks ≈ 80 cycles. At 64 MHz clock and 0.5 mA active, energy per packet:

$$
E_{\text{pkt}} = \frac{80}{64 \times 10^6} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.0 \approx 1.875 \text{ µJ}
$$

For 1 packet per second from a 2400 mAh @ 3 V battery (28.8 kJ):

$$
\text{Battery life} = \frac{28{,}800 \text{ J}}{1.875 \text{ µJ} \times 1 \text{ pkt/s}} \approx 4.85 \times 10^{11} \text{ s} \approx 15{,}400 \text{ years}
$$

But this ignores *radio energy*, which dominates (TX @ 0 dBm ≈ 8 mA for 10 ms ≈ 240 µJ). So:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radio, not crypto, is the energy bottleneck in CPS.** Security *adds* a few % overhead, well worth it.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mapping CPS Security Properties to Protocols

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this matrix**: Tick = property natively provided; Em Dash = absent or application-responsibility. KTU 2024 Part-A questions often ask: *"Which of the following protocols does NOT provide PFS?"* Use this matrix.

| Protocol | Confidentiality | Integrity | Authentication | PFS | Non-Repudiation | Anti-Replay | CPS Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TLS 1.2 (RSA) | ✓ | ✓ (MAC) | ✓ (cert) | ✗ | ✓ | Partial (sequence) | Legacy SCADA |
| **TLS 1.3** | ✓ (AEAD) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (mandatory ECDHE) | ✓ (signature) | ✓ (epoch+seq) | Modern edge |
| DTLS 1.2 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Optional | ✓ | ✓ (epoch) | UDP telemetry |
| **DTLS 1.3** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | OPC UA PubSub |
| IPsec ESP (AES-GCM) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (SA) | ✓ (with PFS) | ✗ | ✓ (ESN/seq) | Substation WAN |
| IPsec AH | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Integrity-only legacy |
| MACsec 802.1AE | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (PAE) | ✓ (LMSAK rotation) | ✗ | ✓ (PN) | Industrial Ethernet |
| WPA3-Enterprise | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (802.1X) | ✓ (SAE) | ✗ | ✓ | Factory Wi-Fi |
| WPA3-Personal (SAE) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (password) | ✓ (forward secrecy is the *goal*) | ✗ | ✓ | Office IoT |
| Zigbee 3.0 | ✓ (AES-CCM) | ✓ | ✓ (APS key) | ✗ (until rekey) | ✗ | ✓ (frame counter) | Smart-home CPS |
| WirelessHART | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (joiner) | ✗ (joiner key) | ✗ | ✓ (32-bit counter) | Process automation |
| LoRaWAN 1.1 | ✓ | ✓ (MIC) | ✓ (DevAddr) | ✗ (NwkSKey lifetime) | ✗ | ✓ (32-bit FCnt) | Smart metering |
| BLE 5.x LE-SC | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (ECDH P-256) | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ (conn. event) | Wearable CPS |
| CANsec (AUTOSAR) | — | ✓ (CMAC) | ✓ | — | — | ✓ (FV) | Automotive |
| MQTT v5 + TLS 1.3 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Cloud telemetry |
| OPC UA (binary) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Industrial control |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — 7-mark design question style

**Problem**: A water-pumping station uses WirelessHART sensors reporting every 200 ms. Each packet is 80 bytes. Design the AEAD scheme and quantify the security strength.

**Solution**:

1. **Choose AEAD suite**: AES-CCM-128 (4-byte MIC, 13-byte nonce) — natively supported by 802.15.4 hardware.
2. **Key length**: 128-bit.
3. **Nonce**: 13 bytes = 104 bits; structure = 8-byte source address + 5-byte frame counter.
4. **Frame counter**: 32 bits; wrap-around at 4.29 × 10⁹ packets → at 5 pkt/s, wrap in **27 years** — exceeds device lifetime.
5. **Confidentiality**: payload encrypted with AES-128-CTR; security = $2^{128}$ operations to break.
6. **Integrity**: 4-byte (32-bit) MIC; forgery probability per packet = $2^{-32}$. With attacker trying 1 million packets per second, expected forgery time = $2^{32} / 10^6 \approx 4300$ s ≈ 72 min — *insufficient* for safety-critical.
7. **Re-design**: bump MIC to 8 bytes (64-bit); forgery time = $2^{64} / 10^6$ s ≈ 585,000 years — acceptable.
8. **Energy cost** (per Section 3.5): radio TX dominates; AEAD overhead is ~1.5 µJ per packet, ≈ 0.2% of TX budget.
9. **Key management**: joiner key (pre-shared at commissioning) → network key + device-specific link key via Join procedure.
10. **PFS limitation**: WirelessHART has *no* PFS at the link layer; mitigated by frequent network-key rotation (e.g., daily).

This is a model 7-mark answer — a KTU examiner awards full marks for *correct suite + nonce analysis + key-management narrative + numerical bound + CPS-specific mitigation*.

```mermaid
    end

    subgraph TR3[Key Management Plane]

    end

    B -.->|telemetry| P1
    P1 -.->|secured by| K1
    P1 -.->|secured by| K2
    P1 -.->|secured by| K3

```

## 4.2 TLS 1.3 Handshake (CPS-relevant, 1-RTT)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Cli as CPS Sensor
    participant Ser as Edge Gateway
    participant HSM as Hardware HSM

    Cli->>Ser: ClientHello (ECDHE key share, signature algs, PSK mode)
    Ser->>HSM: Load long-term cert + private key
    HSM-->>Ser: Return sig
    Ser-->>Cli: ServerHello (selected cipher, server ECDHE share, encrypted_extensions, cert, verify)
    Cli->>Cli: derive session_secret = HKDF(ECDH shared)
    Cli->>Ser: Finished (MAC over handshake)
    Ser->>Ser: verify Finished
    Ser-->>Cli: Finished (now application data encrypted)
    Note over Cli,Ser: 1-RTT done. PFS via ephemeral ECDHE. AEAD = AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305.
    Cli->>Ser: Application Data (telemetry, encrypted + tagged)
    Ser->>Cli: Application Data (control command, encrypted + tagged)
```

---

## 4.3 Key Lifecycle in CPS (NIST SP 800-57 + IEC 62443 mapping)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Pre-activation: key generated in HSM] --> S2[Active: used for AEAD handshake]
    S2 --> S3[Rotation: HKDF rekey; old key deactivated]
    S3 --> S4[Deactivated: kept for verify only]
    S4 --> S5[Destroyed: zeroise; log]
    S2 -.->|compromise detected| S6[Revoked: CRL/OCSP push]
    S6 --> S7[Re-issued: device re-enrolled]
    S7 --> S2
```

---

```mermaid
graph TB
    L7[Application: MQTT/CoAP/OPC UA, signed commands] --> L5
    L5[Transport: TLS 1.3 / DTLS 1.3, AEAD] --> L4
    L4[Network: IPsec ESP, RPL sec] --> L3
    L3[Link: 802.15.4e TSCH, BLE LE-SC, MACsec] --> L2
    L2[Physical: spread spectrum, beamforming, FHSS] --> L1
    L1[Hardware root: PUF, TPM, secure element]
    L7 --> P1
    L5 --> P1
    L4 --> P1
    L3 --> P1
    L2 --> P1
    L1 --> P1
    P1[Cross-layer: monitoring, IDS, side-channel counter-measures]
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1[Eavesdropping] --> C1[AES-128/256 in AEAD mode]
    T2[Replay attack] --> C2[Monotonic frame counter + replay window]
    T3[Rogue AP / MitM] --> C3[802.1X, mutual auth, certificate pinning]

```

## 4.6 Key Establishment in Constrained CPS (DTLS 1.3 PSK / RPK)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as Sensor (8-bit MCU)
    participant EDGE as Edge Gateway
    participant CA as Trust Anchor CA

    Note over Sensor: Pre-loaded PSK or X.509 cert
    Sensor->>EDGE: ClientHello (with PSK identity or RPK)
    EDGE->>CA: Verify RPK / PSK identity
    CA-->>EDGE: OK or revoke
    EDGE-->>Sensor: ServerHello + Finished
    Sensor->>EDGE: Finished (verify handshake MAC)
    Note over Sensor,EDGE: Session keys via HKDF-Expand-Label.
    EDGE-->>Sensor: Application data (AEAD encrypted)
    Sensor-->>EDGE: Application data (AEAD encrypted)
```
