# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between confidentiality, integrity, and authentication as security services in Cyber-Physical Systems. Give one CPS-specific example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Confidentiality (1 mark):** ensures that the message content is hidden from unauthorized parties. *CPS example*: an actuator command "Open Valve 7 to 80% flow" must be hidden from a competitor sniffing the wireless link between SCADA and the field PLC. Achieved by AES-GCM / ChaCha20-Poly1305.
- **Integrity (1 mark):** ensures the receiver can detect any modification of the message in transit. *CPS example*: a sensor reading of 22.4 °C must not be alterable to 122.4 °C (which would trigger an emergency shutdown of a reactor). Achieved by CMAC, GMAC, AEAD tag.
- **Authentication (1 mark):** confirms the identity of the sender. *CPS example*: a master controller must verify that an emergency-stop command originated from a legitimately enrolled safety PLC, not a spoofed device. Achieved by X.509 certificates, ECDSA signatures, or pre-shared symmetric keys with mutual challenge–response.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain why AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) is preferred over separate encryption + MAC in CPS communication protocols.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **(1 mark) Single primitive, two services:** AEAD — e.g., AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 — provides confidentiality and integrity in *one* cryptographic operation, halving code size and execution time on resource-constrained CPS nodes (e.g., 8-bit MCUs).
2. **(1 mark) Provable security:** AEAD schemes are proven IND-CCA + INT-CTXT secure. Combining Encrypt-and-MAC or MAC-then-Encrypt manually is error-prone (e.g., padding-oracle attacks on MAC-then-Encrypt in older IPsec).
3. **(1 mark) Side-channel / misuse resistance:** AEAD APIs minimise the chance of developer error (no separate key handling for MAC, no nonce reuse pitfalls as visible in GCM misuse). TLS 1.3 and DTLS 1.3 mandate AEAD-only ciphersuites, eliminating CBC-mode legacy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module 2 Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students *frequently* confuse **algorithm** (primitive) with **protocol** (exchange). Saying "AES is a protocol" loses 2 marks.
> - Drawing a *TLS handshake* without naming the **1-RTT vs 2-RTT** difference loses 1–2 marks.
> - In wireless-security questions, students *omit* the **physical-layer threats (jamming, RF fingerprinting)**; markers deduct 2 marks for missing the *cross-layer* perspective.
> - Forgetting **PFS** in design questions on TLS 1.3 / DTLS 1.3 is the single most common mark-loss in 2023–2024 OEC papers.
> - Citing outdated algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4) without flagging their deprecation is a 1-mark penalty.
> - In key-management answers, students often fail to mention **root of trust** (TPM/HSM/PUF) — 1 mark deduction.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Secure Communication Protocols

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **TLS 1.3 handshake** for a CPS edge-to-cloud communication. Explain how it provides **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** and **mutual authentication**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **DTLS 1.3** and **TLS 1.3** for use in CPS over UDP. Why is DTLS preferred for real-time control loops? List two CPS-specific challenges DTLS mitigates. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) TLS 1.3 handshake (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 (1.5 marks) — ClientHello:** CPS sensor sends a ClientHello offering AEAD ciphersuites (e.g., TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256), key-share groups (X25519, P-256), and signature algorithms (Ed25519, ECDSA-P256-SHA256). Includes a **pre-shared key extension** if 0-RTT resumption is desired.
- **Step 2 (1.5 marks) — ServerHello:** Edge gateway picks a ciphersuite, sends its **ephemeral ECDHE public key** $E_S$, and immediately encrypts the rest of the handshake. Server transmits `encrypted_extensions`, `certificate` (X.509 with CPS-aware CA), `certificate_verify` (signature over the handshake transcript using long-term key), and `finished` (MAC over the transcript).
- **Step 3 (1 mark) — Client Finished & Key Derivation:** The client derives the **shared secret** $SS = X25519(priv_C, E_S)$, then $HS = HKDF-Extract(SS, "derived")` and `MS = HKDF-Extract(HS, "master")`. The client sends its own `Finished` and verifies server's.
- **Step 4 (1 mark) — Mutual authentication:** The client also sends a `certificate` + `certificate_verify` if the server requested mutual auth (server certificate is *always* verified).
- **PFS (1 mark):** Because the ECDHE keys are *ephemeral* (fresh per session), compromising the long-term private key (used only for the signature) **does not reveal** the session keys derived from $SS$. This is the formal definition of PFS.
- **AEAD application data (1 mark):** All subsequent telemetry and control messages use `AEAD-Encrypt(key=traffic_secret, nonce=seq, plaintext, AAD)` providing confidentiality + integrity in one pass.

Diagram (textual):

```
Sensor  →  ClientHello (key_share = X25519 pub)        →
Edge    ←  ServerHello + EncryptedExtensions + Cert +
            CertVerify + Finished (server_ECDHE_share)  ←
Sensor  →  Finished (now handshake is complete)        →
Sensor  →  Application Data (AEAD encrypted telemetry) →
Edge    →  Application Data (AEAD encrypted command)   →
```

**(b) DTLS 1.3 vs TLS 1.3 (7 marks):**

| Property | TLS 1.3 | DTLS 1.3 |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP (reliable, ordered) | UDP (datagram, best-effort) |
| Replay defence | Sequence number (64-bit) | Epoch + sequence number (64-bit), explicit replay window |
| Handshake | 1-RTT (or 0-RTT resume) | 1-RTT + cookie mechanism for DoS defence |
| Fragmentation | Stream | Explicit record framing with length prefix |
| Reordering | Not needed | Anti-reordering via explicit sequence numbers |
| Use in CPS | Cloud telemetry (MQTT over TCP) | Real-time control (OPC UA PubSub, GOOSE, RPL) |

**Why DTLS is preferred for real-time CPS control loops (2 marks each = 6 marks, 1 mark for challenge list):**

1. **Latency bound:** TCP retransmission can stall a 10 ms control loop; UDP/DTLS is bounded by a single datagram round-trip.
2. **Multicast support:** DTLS 1.3 supports 1-to-N control messages (e.g., substation protection commands). TCP is point-to-point only.
3. **Stateless cookie for DoS:** server issues a cookie in HelloRetryRequest; stateless verification prevents amplification attacks against the resource-constrained edge.
4. **Packet-loss tolerance:** explicit record sequence numbers let receiver reorder/deduplicate without breaking the cryptographic context.

**Two CPS-specific challenges DTLS mitigates (1 mark):**

- **Replay of stale actuator commands** → counter-checked against sequence window.
- **DoS by Hello-flood** → cookie forces attacker to prove IP reachability before consuming server CPU.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Cryptographic Algorithms & Key Management

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 3]**

**(a)** Compare **symmetric** and **asymmetric** cryptographic algorithms for CPS. State two use-cases in CPS where each is preferred, with justification including the *operational cost* (energy, latency, code size). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, describe a **key-management hierarchy** for an industrial CPS with 5,000 field sensors communicating with two edge gateways and one cloud SCADA. Explain how **forward secrecy** is achieved at the link, transport, and application layers. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Symmetric vs Asymmetric in CPS (7 marks):**

**Symmetric primitives — AES-128, ChaCha20-Poly1305, HMAC-SHA-256 (3 marks):**

- **Operation cost:** ~1 cycle/byte on hardware AES, code size 1–3 KB.
- **Latency:** ~10 µs per 64-byte block on Cortex-M4.
- **Energy:** ≈ 1.5 µJ per encrypted packet.
- **Key issue:** same key on both sides → *scalability problem* (n² keys for n devices).
- **CPS use-case 1 (1 mark):** **Authenticated sensor telemetry** — AES-CCM-128 inside IEEE 802.15.4e (WirelessHART, ISA 100.11a) for 100 ms updates from 5,000 sensors, where handshake overhead would dominate.
- **CPS use-case 2 (1 mark):** **Firmware confidentiality on flashless nodes** — ChaCha20-Poly1305 with a per-device symmetric key delivered during manufacturing, decrypting a 256 KB image in ~2 s.

**Asymmetric primitives — ECDH (X25519), Ed25519, RSA-3072 (3 marks):**

- **Operation cost:** ECDH ~5 ms, Ed25519 sign ~10 ms, RSA-3072 keygen ~6 s on Cortex-M4.
- **Latency:** 50–1000× slower than symmetric.
- **Energy:** ~30–500× higher.
- **Key benefit:** enables *authentication without pre-shared secrets* (PKI), *digital signatures* for firmware, and *forward-secret key agreement* (PFS).
- **CPS use-case 1 (1 mark):** **Firmware signature verification at boot** — Ed25519 verify-only in ROM, ensures the device runs trusted code; public key is fixed in silicon.
- **CPS use-case 2 (1 mark):** **Mutual authentication and key agreement during commissioning** — ECDH X25519 in a 1-RTT TLS 1.3 handshake between a brand-new sensor and the gateway, deriving a session key with PFS.

> **Conclusion for Part (a):** *Use symmetric for data plane, asymmetric only for control plane (handshake, signature, key agreement).*

**(b) Key-management hierarchy for 5,000 sensors (7 marks):**

**Hierarchy diagram (1.5 marks):**

```
                  Root CA (offline, HSM in factory)
                          │
              ┌───────────┼───────────┐
        Sub-CA-A        Sub-CA-B      Sub-CA-C
        (Operator)      (Operator)    (Vendor)
              │                │
        Edge Gateway 1   Edge Gateway 2
        (HSM-attached)
              │                │
       ┌──────┴──────┐  ┌──────┴──────┐
    Device Cert  Device Cert  Device Cert ...
    (per-sensor X.509, ECDSA P-256, 5-yr validity)
              │
        Session Key (ECDH X25519, 1-hour rotation)
              │
        Application Key (per-stream, e.g., MQTT topic)
```

**Layer description (3.5 marks):**

- **Root of trust layer (1 mark):** Root CA private key lives *only* in a hardware HSM (FIPS 140-2 Level 3+) in the factory. It signs Sub-CA certs. Air-gapped.
- **Sub-CA layer (0.5 mark):** Operator / vendor Sub-CAs issue device certificates. Sub-CA private keys in HSMs.
- **Device identity layer (0.5 mark):** Each of 5,000 sensors has a unique X.509 certificate with ECDSA P-256 public key, private key stored in Secure Element (e.g., ATECC608B). Validity 5 years.
- **Session-key layer (0.5 mark):** After TLS/DTLS handshake, an ephemeral X25519 key agreement creates a *fresh* 256-bit session key every hour.
- **Application-key layer (0.5 mark):** HKDF-Expand derives per-MQTT-topic or per-CoAP-resource keys for fine-grained access control (e.g., "actuator-on" topic has its own key, separate from "telemetry" topic).
- **Storage (0.5 mark):** Long-term keys in Secure Element; session keys in RAM, zeroised on tamper.

**Forward Secrecy at three layers (2 marks):**

- **Link layer (WirelessHART / 802.15.4e) (0.7 mark):** *Limited* PFS — network key rotation every 24 h. Compromise of today's key reveals only today's traffic.
- **Transport layer (DTLS 1.3) (0.7 mark):** Strong PFS — (EC)DHE per session; ephemeral key erased after handshake; even if Sub-CA private key leaks, past sessions stay secure.
- **Application layer (MQTT topic key) (0.6 mark):** PFS via HKDF-Expand on a high-entropy seed, rotated every 10 min; independent of link/transport secrets.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (read 5 minutes before exam).**

### Key Concepts
- **Secure communication in CPS** = CIA + F (Freshness) + Non-Repudiation.
- **Dolev–Yao attacker** controls the network; protocols must be secure under this model.
- **AEAD** (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, AES-CCM) is the modern default — single primitive, two services.
- **TLS 1.3 and DTLS 1.3** mandate AEAD + (EC)DHE → PFS by design.
- **Nonce reuse in GCM = total break** (Joux 2006).
- **Key hierarchy** Master → KEK → Device → Session, with Master key in HSM/TPM, never in software.
- **ECDH (X25519)** is the CPS-friendly key agreement; **Ed25519** is the CPS-friendly signature.
- **RSA-3072 ≈ 128-bit security; P-256 ≈ 128-bit security; SHA-256 ≈ 128-bit collision resistance.**
- **Wireless threats** = eavesdrop, replay, rogue AP/MitM, jamming, side-channel, compromised nodes.
- **Defence-in-depth** = physical + link + network + transport + application + monitoring.

### Key Numerical Anchors
- AES-128 = $2^{128}$ ops brute force.
- RSA-3072 ≈ AES-128 in security.
- P-256 = AES-128 in security.
- SHA-256 collision = $2^{128}$, pre-image = $2^{256}$.
- 4-byte MIC forgery = $2^{-32}$ per try → too weak for safety.
- 8-byte MIC forgery = $2^{-64}$ per try → standard.
- 32-bit frame counter at 5 Hz wraps in **27 years**.
- TLS 1.3 = 1-RTT; TLS 1.2 (RSA) = 2-RTT (no PFS).
- DTLS cookie = stateless DoS defence, ≥ 128 bits.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Using ECB mode for anything.
- ❌ Reusing a GCM nonce.
- ❌ Storing long-term keys in cleartext flash.
- ❌ Confusing algorithm (AES) with protocol (TLS).
- ❌ Designing CPS security with TCP for real-time loops.
- ❌ Saying "AES is unbreakable" — quantify with $2^{128}$.
- ❌ Skipping the physical-layer threat in wireless questions.
- ❌ Forgetting PFS in TLS 1.3 design questions.
- ❌ Citing deprecated primitives (MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4) without flagging.
- ❌ Forgetting to mention root-of-trust (TPM/HSM/PUF) in key-management answers.

### KTU-Style "One-Liners" for Quick Recall
- **"CIA + F"** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, **Freshness**.
- **"1-RTT + ECDHE = PFS"** = the TLS 1.3 trick.
- **"HSM stores MK, never leaves hardware"** = the key-hierarchy rule.
- **"Radio energy >> crypto energy"** = the CPS battery rule.
- **"AEAD = one primitive, two services"** = the modern crypto rule.
- **"Side-channel = constant-time + masking + TEE"** = the implementation rule.
- **"Cross-layer defence"** = PHY + LINK + NET + TRANS + APP + MON.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrity and Availability in CPS: Techniques for Ensuring Data Integrity, Fault Tolerance and Redundancy, Protection Against Denial of Service Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT823 - SECURITY AND PRIVACY IN CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Integrity and Availability in CPS: Techniques for Ensuring Data Integrity, Fault Tolerance and Redundancy, Protection Against Denial of Service Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Data Integrity in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
*Data Integrity* in CPS refers to the **assurance that data — both in transit across the cyber-physical network and at rest in sensors, actuators, controllers, and databases — has not been altered, corrupted, deleted, or forged in an unauthorized manner**, thereby preserving the trustworthiness of measurements, control commands, and state estimations that drive the physical process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In CPS, integrity violations are not just "data tampering" — they can directly translate to **physical-world consequences**: a corrupted actuator command can over-speed a turbine, a forged sensor reading can cause a dam gate to open, or a falsified GPS coordinate can misroute an autonomous vehicle.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine an air traffic controller (ATC) guiding pilots. The controller receives the **altitude, speed, and heading** of every aircraft. If a hacker injects false altitude data, the controller may instruct two planes to occupy the same altitude, causing a mid-air collision. Data integrity mechanisms in CPS act like a **tamper-evident seal and a verified courier** — every message is sealed (hashed) and signed, and the courier carries a verified identity certificate. If the seal breaks or the signature fails, the message is rejected immediately.

### 1.2 Availability in CPS

**Formal Definition:**
*Availability* in CPS is the **guarantee that the cyber-physical system, its communication channels, computational resources, and physical actuators remain accessible and operational to authorized users and processes with an acceptable Quality of Service (QoS)**, especially under adversarial conditions such as Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks, component failures, or environmental disturbances.

The standard industry availability classes (called "nines of availability") are:

| Availability % | Downtime/Year | Tier |
|---|---|---|
| **99.9%** (three nines) | 8.76 hours | Tier I |
| **99.99%** (four nines) | 52.6 minutes | Tier II |
| **99.999%** (five nines) | 5.26 minutes | Tier III / IV |

> [!NOTE]
> CPS often target **five-nines (99.999%)** availability because physical systems (e.g., pacemakers, nuclear reactors, fly-by-wire aircraft) cannot tolerate even minutes of downtime without catastrophic loss.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of availability like **water supply to a hospital's ICU**. If the water pipe (the network) is clogged by an attacker (DoS), the dialysis machines stop, ventilators fail, and patients die. CPS availability mechanisms are like **multiple independent water pipes, reserve tanks, and pressure pumps** — if one is blocked, the others take over instantly.

### 1.3 Threat Landscape at a Glance

> [!WARNING]
> The **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) applies to CPS, but the priority order is often **I → A → C** (Integrity first, then Availability, then Confidentiality) — unlike IT systems where Confidentiality often leads. Why? A stolen password is recoverable; a sabotaged insulin pump is fatal.

**Key Threat Metrics Used in CPS:**
- **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) — average time a system operates before failing.
- **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) — average time to restore operation.
- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) = MTTF + MTTR.
- **λ (Failure Rate)** — failures per unit time (Poisson-distributed failures assumed).
- **R(t)** (Reliability function) — probability the system survives till time $t$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Data Integrity Techniques

CPS integrity protection operates at **multiple layers**:

#### A. Origin Integrity (Who sent it?)
1. **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)** — symmetric key, e.g., HMAC-SHA-256.
2. **Digital Signatures** — asymmetric (RSA, ECDSA), provides non-repudiation.
3. **Authenticated Encryption (AEAD)** — e.g., AES-GCM combines confidentiality + integrity.

#### B. Content Integrity (Has the payload changed?)
1. **Cryptographic Hash Functions** — SHA-256, SHA-3, BLAKE2.
2. **Checksums & CRCs** — CRC-32 for error detection in noisy industrial channels.
3. **Merkle Trees** — efficient integrity verification of large log streams in SCADA.

#### C. Temporal / Sequential Integrity (Are events in correct order?)
1. **Sequence Numbers & Timestamps** — protected inside MAC.
2. **Blockchain / Tamper-Evident Logs** — append-only ledger with hash chaining.
3. **Authenticated Skip-lists** — efficient time-series integrity in IoT.

#### D. Semantic Integrity (Is the value physically plausible?)
1. **Plausibility Checks** — e.g., a car's wheel speed cannot exceed 300 km/h.
2. **Model-based Anomaly Detection** — Kalman filters, state estimators.
3. **Range & Rate-of-Change Validation** — reject out-of-bound or physically impossible deltas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pure cryptographic integrity (MAC/digital signatures) is **necessary but not sufficient** in CPS. An attacker who replays an *old* valid signed command still violates integrity in the semantic sense. Hence CPS must combine **freshness (nonce/timestamp)** with **semantic plausibility**.

### 2.2 Fault Tolerance & Redundancy — Core Strategies

**Fault Tolerance Principle:** A system is fault-tolerant if it can **continue providing correct service** despite the presence of faults. Achieved through **Redundancy** — the addition of extra resources beyond what is strictly needed in a fault-free scenario.

#### A. Hardware Redundancy
1. **Passive Redundancy — Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR):**
   Three identical modules vote; majority (2-out-of-3) wins. Tolerates **1 faulty module**.
2. **Active Redundancy — Hot/Warm/Cold Standby:**
   - **Hot standby:** Spare runs in lockstep, ready to take over in microseconds.
   - **Warm standby:** Spare is partially powered, takeover in seconds.
   - **Cold standby:** Spare is off; requires boot-up (minutes).
3. **Dual Modular Redundancy (DMR) with self-checking:** Used when 3-way voting is too costly.

#### B. Software Redundancy
1. **N-Version Programming:** N independently developed implementations of the same spec; outputs voted.
2. **Recovery Blocks:** Primary block + acceptance test; on failure, alternate block executes.
3. **Diversity:** Different OS, compiler, or hardware to defeat common-mode bugs.

#### C. Information Redundancy (Error Correcting Codes)
1. **Hamming Codes** — single-bit error correction, common in memory.
2. **Reed-Solomon Codes** — burst error correction, used in storage and wireless.
3. **Convolutional & Turbo Codes** — used in CPS wireless links (e.g., industrial Wi-Fi, 5G URLLC).

#### D. Time Redundancy
- **Transient-fault retry:** Re-execute the same operation; if it succeeds, the fault was transient (e.g., cosmic ray upset).
- **Checkpointing & Rollback Recovery:** Periodic state snapshots; on failure, restore last good checkpoint.

#### E. Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)
- Tolerates **arbitrary, malicious faults** (not just crashes). Critical in CPS where attackers control components.
- Classical result: To tolerate $f$ Byzantine faults, need $\geq 3f + 1$ total nodes.
- Used in distributed CPS consensus (e.g., PLC clusters, drone swarms).

### 2.3 Denial-of-Service (DoS) Protection in CPS

**CPS-Specific DoS Attack Surfaces:**
1. **Jamming** of wireless channels (e.g., ZigBee, LoRa, 5G).
2. **Flooding** of control packets (e.g., Modbus/TCP request flood).
3. **Resource exhaustion** on constrained embedded devices (CPU, memory, battery).
4. **Sensor blinding/saturation** — overpowering physical sensors with noise.
5. **Routing blackhole/sinkhole** in CPS mesh networks.
6. **Logic-level DoS** — forcing PLC into scan-stop mode.

**Countermeasure Layers:**

| Layer | Technique |
|---|---|
| **Physical** | Frequency hopping, spread spectrum, directional antennas, jamming detection |
| **Data Link** | Rate limiting, time-slot reservation, channel access control |
| **Network** | Firewalls, deep packet inspection, filtering (ACLs), SDN-based traffic shaping |
| **Transport** | SYN cookies, connection limits, QoS prioritisation |
| **Application** | ReCAPTCHA-like challenges, request signing, semantic validation |
| **System** | Watchdog timers, resource quotas, process isolation |
| **Architectural** | Replication, diversity, geographic dispersion, micro-segmentation |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table summarises every formula you must memorise for the KTU 2024 ESE on this module. Pay special attention to units and boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability (constant failure rate) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ in failures/hour |
| 2 | Unreliability / CDF | $F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ |
| 3 | MTTF (constant $\lambda$) | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours |
| 4 | Availability (steady-state) | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | Availability in % | $A_\% = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 6 | Series system reliability | $R_\text{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All must work |
| 7 | Parallel (k-out-of-n) system reliability | $R_\text{sys} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^{i}(1-R)^{n-i}$ | $n$ components, $k$ needed |
| 8 | TMR (3 modules, voter) | $R_\text{TMR} = R_\text{voter} \cdot \left[R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)\right]$ | $R_\text{voter}$ usually $\approx 1$ |
| 9 | CRC-32 polynomial | $x^{32} + x^{26} + x^{23} + x^{22} + x^{16} + x^{12} + x^{11} + x^{10} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{5} + x^{4} + x^{2} + x + 1$ | IEEE 802.3 |
| 10 | Hamming distance | Minimum bit-flips to change valid codeword to another | Determines error-detection/correction capability |
| 11 | Byzantine fault bound | Need $n \geq 3f + 1$ nodes to tolerate $f$ Byzantine faults | $f$ = number of traitors |
| 12 | HMAC definition | $\text{HMAC}_K(m) = H\!\left((K \oplus opad) \;\Vert\; H((K \oplus ipad) \;\Vert\; m)\right)$ | $H$ = hash function |
| 13 | Jamming SNR threshold | $\text{SNR} \geq \text{SNR}_\text{min}$ for successful reception | Below threshold → DoS |
| 14 | Availability nines conversion | $\text{Downtime/yr} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ minutes | For planning SLA |
| 15 | Cost of redundancy | $C_\text{total} = n \cdot C_\text{unit} + C_\text{voter} + C_\text{ops}$ | $n$ = redundancy factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX safety check:** All absolute value / divisibility symbols inside table cells use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table renderer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Availability from Renewal Theory

We derive the classic availability formula $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the system life cycle.**
A repairable CPS alternates between two states:
- **UP** (operational), lasting on average $\text{MTTF}$ time units.
- **DOWN** (under repair), lasting on average $\text{MTTR}$ time units.

**Step 2 — One complete renewal cycle.**
A single cycle of "operate → fail → repair → operate" takes:

$$
T_\text{cycle} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = \text{MTBF}
$$

**Step 3 — Fraction of time UP.**
By the renewal-reward theorem, the long-run fraction of time the system is operational is:

$$
A = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\text{Total UP time}}{T} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage.**

$$
A_\% = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**
A CPS sensor has $\text{MTTF} = 9000$ hours and $\text{MTTR} = 1$ hour. Find its availability.

$$
A = \frac{9000}{9000 + 1} = \frac{9000}{9001} \approx 0.999889
$$

$$
A_\% \approx 99.9889\%
$$

$$
\text{Downtime per year} = (1 - 0.999889) \times 525{,}600 \approx 58.4 \text{ minutes}
$$

That is approximately **three-and-a-half nines** availability.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: TMR (Triple Modular Redundancy) Reliability

**Step 1 — Model.**
Three identical modules, each with reliability $R$. Their outputs feed a majority voter with reliability $R_v$. The system is correct if the voter is correct AND at least 2 of 3 modules are correct.

**Step 2 — Enumerate outcomes.**
Let $X$ = number of correct modules out of 3. $X \sim \text{Binomial}(3, R)$.

$$
P(X = 0) = (1-R)^3
$$

$$
P(X = 1) = 3 R (1-R)^2
$$

$$
P(X = 2) = 3 R^2 (1-R)
$$

$$
P(X = 3) = R^3
$$

**Step 3 — TMR passes if $X \geq 2$.**

$$
P(X \geq 2) = P(X=2) + P(X=3) = 3R^2(1-R) + R^3
$$

**Step 4 — Apply AND with voter reliability.**

$$
R_\text{TMR} = R_v \cdot \left[\,R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)\,\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the bracket.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^3 + 3R^2(1-R) &= R^3 + 3R^2 - 3R^3 \\
&= 3R^2 - 2R^3 \\
&= R^2 (3 - 2R)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form.**

$$
R_\text{TMR} = R_v \cdot R^2 (3 - 2R)
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical sanity check.**
If $R = 0.9$ and $R_v = 1$:

$$
R_\text{TMR} = (0.9)^2 (3 - 2 \times 0.9) = 0.81 \times 1.2 = 0.972
$$

Compare to single module $R = 0.9$: TMR improves reliability from **90% → 97.2%**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hash-Based Message Integrity Verification

**Step 1 — Sender side.**
Compute hash of message $m$:

$$
h = H(m)
$$

Append MAC using shared secret $K$:

$$
t = \text{HMAC}_K(h) = H\big((K \oplus opad) \;\Vert\; H((K \oplus ipad) \;\Vert\; m)\big)
$$

Transmit $\langle m, t \rangle$ to receiver.

**Step 2 — Receiver side.**
Receiver recomputes $h' = H(m)$, recomputes $t' = \text{HMAC}_K(h')$, and compares:

$$
\text{Accept} \iff t \overset{?}{=} t'
$$

**Step 3 — Bit-length justification (why SHA-256?).**
SHA-256 produces 256-bit output. By the **birthday paradox**, a brute-force collision is feasible in approximately $2^{128}$ operations, which is computationally infeasible today. Hence the integrity guarantee is **$O(2^{128})$ security** against forgery.

**Step 4 — Why a bare hash $H(m)$ is *not* enough.**
Without HMAC wrapping, an attacker can:
- Substitute $\langle m', H(m') \rangle$ — both match.
- Mount a length-extension attack on SHA-256 (in raw form).

HMAC neutralises both because the secret $K$ is unknown to the adversary.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Technique Selection for CPS Sub-Systems

| CPS Sub-System | Primary Integrity Threat | Recommended Integrity Technique | Recommended Availability Technique | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Smart Grid SCADA** | False control commands to breakers | Digital signatures (ECDSA) + sequence numbers | TMR + geographic diversity + hot standby | High-stakes, latency-tolerant |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Spoofed GPS / LiDAR | Cross-check multi-sensor plausibility + HMAC | Hardware TMR on braking ECU | Real-time, safety-critical |
| **Medical Implant (Pacemaker)** | Forged firmware updates | Digital signature + hash chain | Watchdog timer + battery reserve | Life-critical, low-bandwidth |
| **Industrial IoT Sensor Mesh** | Replay of old sensor readings | Timestamp + nonce + HMAC | Frequency hopping + rate limiting | Resource-constrained |
| **Drone Swarm** | Byzantine malicious drones | BFT consensus (e.g., PBFT) | N-modular redundancy + mesh routing | Multi-node cooperative |
| **Smart Water Distribution** | Tampered flow readings | Merkle-tree log + CRC | Dual-redundant PLCs | Auditability required |
| **Building Automation (HVAC)** | Setpoint manipulation | AES-GCM (AEAD) | Cold standby controller | Lower criticality |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the **integrity technique scales with criticality**: lightweight HMAC for low-stakes telemetry, digital signatures for command & control, BFT for cooperative multi-agent CPS.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Cost-Benefit of Redundancy (Quick Estimation)

Suppose a single component costs $C = \text{₹}50{,}000$, has reliability $R = 0.95$, and a system failure costs $C_f = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000$ (₹ 50 lakh).

**Single component:**
$$
\text{Expected loss} = (1 - R) \cdot C_f = 0.05 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}2{,}50{,}000
$$

**TMR with voter (assume $R_v = 1$):**
$$
R_\text{TMR} = R^2(3 - 2R) = (0.95)^2(3 - 1.9) = 0.9025 \times 1.1 = 0.99275
$$

$$
\text{Expected loss} = (1 - 0.99275) \times 50{,}00{,}000 = 0.00725 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}36{,}250
$$

**Cost of TMR:**
$$
C_\text{TMR} = 3C + C_\text{voter} \approx 3 \times 50{,}000 + 20{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}70{,}000
$$

**Net saving:**
$$
\text{Saving} = 2{,}50{,}000 - 36{,}250 = \text{₹}2{,}13{,}750 \quad \text{vs. cost of ₹1,70,000} \;\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Net benefit} = \text{₹}43{,}750
$$

TMR is economically justified for this CPS.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Layered CPS Integrity & Availability Architecture

This diagram maps the complete defence-in-depth architecture for a CPS, from physical sensors up to application-layer control logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHY["LAYER 1: PHYSICAL SENSORS & ACTUATORS"]
        s1["Temp Sensor"]
        s2["Pressure Sensor"]
        s3["Flow Sensor"]
        s4["Motor Actuator"]
        s5["Solenoid Valve"]
    end

    subgraph SEM["LAYER 2: SEMANTIC VALIDATION (PLAUSIBILITY)"]
        v1["Range Check"]
        v2["Rate of Change Check"]
        v3["Model-based Estimator Kalman Filter"]
    end

    subgraph CRY["LAYER 3: CRYPTOGRAPHIC INTEGRITY"]
        c1["HMAC-SHA256"]
        c2["Digital Signature ECDSA"]
        c3["Merkle Tree Log"]
        c4["Sequence Number Nonce"]
    end

    subgraph NET["LAYER 4: NETWORK DoS PROTECTION"]
        n1["Firewall ACL"]
        n2["Rate Limiter"]
        n3["SDN Traffic Shaper"]
        n4["Jamming Detector"]
    end

    subgraph RED["LAYER 5: REDUNDANCY & FAULT TOLERANCE"]
        r1["TMR Voter"]
        r2["Hot Standby"]
        r3["Cold Standby"]
        r4["Byzantine Fault Tolerance"]
    end

    subgraph REC["LAYER 6: RECOVERY & CHECKPOINTING"]
        rc1["Watchdog Timer"]
        rc2["Checkpoint Snapshot"]
        rc3["Rollback Recovery"]
    end

    PHY --> SEM
    SEM --> CRY
    CRY --> NET
    NET --> RED
    RED --> REC
    REC --> PHY

    s1 --> v1
    s2 --> v2
    s3 --> v3
    v1 --> c1
    v2 --> c2
    v3 --> c3
    c1 --> n1
    c2 --> n2
    c3 --> n3
    c4 --> n3
    n1 --> r1
    n2 --> r2
    n3 --> r3
    n4 --> r4
    r1 --> rc1
    r2 --> rc2
    r3 --> rc3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and letter-prefixed (e.g., `s1`, `v1`, `c1`, `n1`, `r1`, `rc1`) — no reserved keywords used.
> - All node labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only — no `**bold**`, `*italic*`, or HTML tables inside double-quoted labels.

---

### 4.2 TMR (Triple Modular Redundancy) Voting Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["Input Signal X"] --> m1["Module M1"]
    inp --> m2["Module M2"]
    inp --> m3["Module M3"]
    m1 --> v["Majority Voter"]
    m2 --> v
    m3 --> v
    v --> out["Output Y correct"]
    m1 -. faulty .-> v
    m2 -. faulty .-> v
    m3 -. faulty .-> v
```

**Operating principle:** The voter (typically a hardware triplicated comparator) accepts the value produced by **at least two matching modules**. Even if one module is faulty (or malicious — Byzantine), the system output remains correct.

---

### 4.3 DoS Attack Mitigation Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Incoming Packet Arrives"] --> rate{"Rate within SLA"}
    rate -- "No exceed" --> acl{"Source IP in ACL whitelist"}
    rate -- "Exceed limit" --> dropA["Drop and log to IDS"]
    acl -- "No match" --> dropB["Drop reject"]
    acl -- "Match" --> sig{"Signature valid HMAC"}
    sig -- "No invalid" --> dropC["Drop and raise alert"]
    sig -- "Yes valid" --> fresh{"Freshness check Nonce and Timestamp"}
    fresh -- "Replay expired" --> dropD["Drop replay attack"]
    fresh -- "Fresh" --> sem{"Semantic plausibility check"}
    sem -- "Implausible" --> dropE["Quarantine and alert"]
    sem -- "Plausible" --> accept["Forward to controller"]
    dropA --> ids["Update IDS database"]
    dropB --> ids
    dropC --> ids
    dropD --> ids
    dropE --> ids
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU-style block diagram for DoS mitigation in CPS**. It shows the cascading gate sequence: rate → identity → signature → freshness → semantics. Each gate that fails produces a different security log entry, enabling forensic root-cause analysis.

---

### 4.4 Byzantine Fault Tolerance Consensus Flow (PBFT-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client["Client / CPS Node"] --> pre["Pre-Prepare Phase"]
    pre --> p1["Replica 1"]
    pre --> p2["Replica 2"]
    pre --> p3["Replica 3"]
    pre --> p4["Replica 4 f=1"]
    p1 --> prep["Prepare Phase broadcast"]
    p2 --> prep
    p3 --> prep
    p4 --> prep
    prep --> comm["Commit Phase 2f+1 matches"]
    comm --> reply["Reply to Client"]
    reply --> done["Operation Accepted"]
```

**PBFT requirement:** $n \geq 3f + 1$ replicas. For $f = 1$ (one Byzantine traitor), $n = 4$ replicas are needed, matching the diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Differentiate between *data integrity* and *data availability* in a Cyber-Physical System. Why is integrity considered more critical than confidentiality in many CPS deployments? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Data integrity** is the assurance that sensor readings, control commands, and stored state have not been altered in an unauthorised manner (corruption, forgery, replay).
- **Data availability** is the assurance that the CPS, its communication channels, and actuators remain operational and accessible to authorised users with acceptable latency, even under attack or failure.
- Integrity is often prioritised over confidentiality in CPS because **tampered physical data can cause immediate, irreversible harm** (e.g., wrong insulin dose, false turbine overspeed trip), whereas a confidentiality breach typically allows recovery (key rotation, password reset, breach disclosure). In safety-critical CPS the priority order is **Integrity → Availability → Confidentiality**, the reverse of typical IT systems.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q2. List any three techniques used to ensure data integrity in CPS and briefly state the role of each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code):** Provides symmetric-key integrity and authenticity; lightweight enough for resource-constrained IoT/CPS sensors.
2. **Digital Signatures (e.g., ECDSA):** Provide asymmetric integrity with non-repudiation — receiver can prove to a third party that the command originated from a specific authorised controller.
3. **Merkle Tree / Hash-Chained Logs:** Enable efficient, append-only tamper-evident storage of CPS event logs; any retroactive modification breaks the chain, alerting auditors.
*(Alternative acceptable techniques: CRC, AEAD, sequence numbers + nonces, plausibility checks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Each Part B question offers **internal choice**, exactly as per KTU 2024 ESE regulations. We present **Question A** and **Question B** as two independent alternatives; the student answers one.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 2]**

**(a) Explain in detail the various techniques for ensuring data integrity in Cyber-Physical Systems. Differentiate between MAC and digital signatures with respect to their suitability in CPS. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Data integrity in CPS can be ensured through four classes of techniques:

**1. Cryptographic Hash Functions (SHA-256, SHA-3):**
A hash $h = H(m)$ produces a fixed-size digest. Any single-bit change in $m$ causes an avalanche effect, completely changing $h$. Used in HMAC, digital signatures, and Merkle trees.

**2. Message Authentication Codes (HMAC):**
A keyed hash $\text{HMAC}_K(m)$ ensures both integrity and authenticity. Only parties holding the shared key $K$ can produce or verify the tag. **Suitable for CPS** because it is computationally cheap (a few µs on an 8-bit MCU) and fits bandwidth-constrained channels (e.g., WirelessHART, ZigBee).

**3. Digital Signatures (RSA, ECDSA):**
Asymmetric: sender signs with private key $\text{sk}$, receiver verifies with public key $\text{pk}$. Provides **non-repudiation** — the signer cannot later deny signing. **Suitable for CPS command flows** (firmware updates, breaker trip commands) where the receiver may need to prove the command's origin in a forensic or regulatory investigation.

**4. Sequence Numbers, Nonces, Timestamps:**
Defeat **replay attacks**. A validly signed but old command must be rejected. CPS typically uses a 32-bit or 64-bit monotonically increasing counter protected inside the MAC.

**5. Semantic / Plausibility Checks:**
Independent of cryptography, a controller checks that the received value is physically plausible (e.g., reactor temperature cannot rise by 200 °C in 1 ms). Acts as the **last line of defence** if cryptography is broken.

**MAC vs Digital Signatures in CPS:**

| Property | HMAC | Digital Signature (ECDSA) |
|---|---|---|
| Key type | Symmetric (shared $K$) | Asymmetric ($sk$, $pk$) |
| Non-repudiation | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Computation cost | Low (~µs) | Higher (~ms) |
| Suitability for resource-constrained CPS nodes | ✅ Excellent | ⚠️ Acceptable for gateways/PLCs |
| Suitability for control commands requiring audit | ⚠️ Weak | ✅ Strong |
| Replay protection | Needs separate nonce | Needs separate nonce |
| Key distribution in CPS | Harder (shared secret between all pairs) | Easier (only public key broadcast) |

**Conclusion:** HMAC is preferred for **in-cluster sensor-to-controller traffic**; digital signatures are preferred for **inter-cluster command traffic** and **firmware distribution**.

---

**(b) Describe fault tolerance and redundancy techniques in CPS. Derive the reliability expression for Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) and show that TMR improves system reliability when individual module reliability $R > 0.5$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Fault Tolerance Concept:**
A system is fault-tolerant if it continues to provide correct service despite component faults. The cornerstone is **redundancy** — the inclusion of extra resources beyond what is strictly needed.

**Categories of Redundancy:**
1. **Hardware Redundancy:** TMR, dual modular redundancy, hot/warm/cold standby.
2. **Software Redundancy:** N-version programming, recovery blocks.
3. **Information Redundancy:** Error-correcting codes (Hamming, Reed-Solomon).
4. **Time Redundancy:** Retries, checkpoint/rollback.
5. **Functional Redundancy:** Diverse algorithms (e.g., two different pressure-estimation methods).

**Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR):**
Three identical modules perform the same computation; a majority voter selects the output that appears at least twice.

**Derivation of TMR Reliability:**

Let $R$ be the reliability of each module and $R_v$ the voter reliability. The number of correct modules $X$ follows $\text{Binomial}(3, R)$. TMR succeeds when $X \geq 2$:

$$
P(X \geq 2) = \binom{3}{2}R^2(1-R) + \binom{3}{3}R^3
$$

$$
P(X \geq 2) = 3R^2(1-R) + R^3
$$

Apply AND with voter:

$$
R_\text{TMR} = R_v \cdot \big[\,3R^2(1-R) + R^3\,\big]
$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3R^2(1-R) + R^3 &= 3R^2 - 3R^3 + R^3 \\
&= 3R^2 - 2R^3 \\
&= R^2(3 - 2R)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$
R_\text{TMR} = R_v \cdot R^2 (3 - 2R)
$$

**Proof that TMR improves reliability when $R > 0.5$:**

We must show $R_\text{TMR} > R$ for $0.5 < R < 1$, assuming $R_v = 1$:

$$
R^2(3 - 2R) > R
$$

Divide both sides by $R$ (since $R > 0$):

$$
R(3 - 2R) > 1
$$

$$
3R - 2R^2 > 1
$$

$$
2R^2 - 3R + 1 < 0
$$

Factor:

$$
(2R - 1)(R - 1) < 0
$$

For $R \in (0.5, 1)$, the first factor is positive, the second is negative, so the product is **negative** — satisfying the inequality. ∎

**Numerical verification:** For $R = 0.9$, $R_\text{TMR} = 0.9^2 \times (3 - 1.8) = 0.81 \times 1.2 = 0.972 > 0.9$ ✓.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):**
> A common mistake students make is **forgetting to factor in the voter reliability $R_v$**. A real voter is not perfectly reliable; in exam answers, always state assumptions explicitly. Another common error is **failing to verify the boundary condition** $R > 0.5$. The examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if you do not prove the condition.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 2, Alternative Choice]**

**(a) What is a Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack in the context of CPS? Explain with suitable examples the different types of DoS attacks and the layered defence mechanisms used to mitigate them. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
A *Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack* in CPS is a deliberate attempt by an adversary to **render the system, its sensors, its communication channels, or its actuators unavailable** to authorised users or processes. The attack can be launched in the cyber domain (network flooding), the physical domain (jamming), or the logical domain (state machine abuse).

**Types of CPS-Specific DoS Attacks:**

1. **RF / Wireless Jamming:**
   An attacker transmits high-power noise on the frequency used by CPS wireless links (ZigBee 2.4 GHz, LoRa 868/915 MHz, Wi-Fi 5 GHz), raising the noise floor and preventing legitimate packets from being decoded.

2. **Network Flooding:**
   The attacker sends a massive volume of packets to the SCADA gateway (e.g., a Modbus/TCP server), exhausting its socket buffer and CPU. Real control packets get dropped.

3. **Protocol Exploit DoS:**
   Malformed packets (e.g., oversized DNP3 frames, malformed IEC-61850 GOOSE) force the controller into a scan-stop or error state, halting control.

4. **Resource Exhaustion on IoT Endpoints:**
   Mirai-style botnets force CPS sensors to participate in distributed floods, draining their battery and CPU.

5. **Sensor Blinding / Saturation:**
   A physical-layer attack: high-intensity light blinds a LiDAR; ultrasonic noise deafens an acoustic sensor.

6. **Routing-Layer DoS:**
   In CPS mesh networks, a blackhole node drops all packets routed through it, partitioning the network.

7. **Logic / State-Machine DoS:**
   Forcing a PLC into a non-recoverable state (e.g., triggering a SafeStop that requires manual reset).

**Layered Defence Mechanism (defence in depth):**

| Layer | Countermeasure |
|---|---|
| **Physical** | Spread spectrum, frequency hopping, directional antennas, jamming detection, sensor shielding |
| **Data Link (MAC)** | Time-slot TDMA/CSMA-CA, packet retransmission with limits, rate limiting per node |
| **Network** | Firewalls, Access Control Lists (ACLs), Deep Packet Inspection (DPI), SDN-based traffic shaping |
| **Transport** | SYN cookies, per-source connection caps, QoS prioritisation for control traffic |
| **Application** | Authentication of every command, semantic plausibility checks, request throttling |
| **System** | Watchdog timers, process isolation, resource quotas, microkernel architecture |
| **Architectural** | Geographic diversity, multi-path routing, replicated controllers, micro-segmentation |

**Conclusion:**
CPS DoS protection must be **defence-in-depth**, because attackers can choose whichever layer is weakest. No single countermeasure is sufficient.

---

**(b) Define the term *Availability* in CPS. A CPS controller has MTTF = 8000 hours and MTTR = 2 hours. Calculate (i) the steady-state availability, (ii) the percentage availability, (iii) the annual downtime in minutes, and (iv) comment on whether this satisfies a four-nines SLA. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
*Availability* in CPS is the probability that the system is operational and able to perform its intended function at a given instant of time, considering both random failures and active attacks.

**Given:**
- MTTF = 8000 hours
- MTTR = 2 hours

**(i) Steady-State Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 2} = \frac{8000}{8002} \approx 0.999750
$$

**(ii) Percentage Availability:**

$$
A_\% = 0.999750 \times 100 = 99.9750\%
$$

**(iii) Annual Downtime in Minutes:**

One year = 365 × 24 × 60 = 525,600 minutes.

$$
\text{Downtime} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600 = 0.000250 \times 525{,}600 = 131.4 \text{ minutes}
$$

Equivalently, about **2 hours 11 minutes** of downtime per year.

**(iv) Four-Nines SLA Comparison:**

Four-nines = 99.99% → maximum 52.56 minutes/year of downtime.

Our system has 131.4 minutes/year of downtime → **does NOT satisfy** the four-nines SLA. It is only at the **three-and-a-half nines (99.975%)** level.

**To reach four nines, the operator must:**
- Reduce MTTR (faster repair), or
- Increase MTTF (more reliable components), or
- Add redundancy (e.g., hot-standby controller).

**For example,** to achieve $A = 0.9999$ with $\text{MTTR} = 2$ hours:

$$
\frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + 2} = 0.9999
$$

$$
\text{MTTF} = 0.9999 \times \text{MTTF} + 0.9999 \times 2
$$

$$
0.0001 \times \text{MTTF} = 1.9998
$$

$$
\text{MTTF} = 19{,}998 \text{ hours} \approx 2.28 \text{ years}
$$

This represents a 2.5× improvement in component reliability, which is usually achieved through TMR + better components.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> Two common mistakes:
> 1. **Unit confusion** — students write downtime in hours when asked in minutes. Always re-read the question's unit requirement.
> 2. **SLA mis-statement** — students say "three nines" when the value is 99.975% (which is three-and-a-half nines). The examiner expects the precise tier identification, not an approximation.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert years to minutes** — remember $1 \text{ year} = 525{,}600$ minutes, not 365 days × 24 = 8760 (that is hours).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2**

**Core Concepts to Memorise:**
- [ ] **Data Integrity** = "data has not been altered" — must be combined with **freshness** (nonce/timestamp) to defeat replays.
- [ ] **Availability** = $\dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$; for high-criticality CPS aim for **five nines (99.999%)**.
- [ ] **CIA priority in CPS** is typically **Integrity → Availability → Confidentiality** (reverse of IT).
- [ ] **HMAC** for in-cluster CPS traffic; **Digital Signatures** for command & control and firmware updates.
- [ ] **Merkle trees** for tamper-evident CPS log storage.
- [ ] **Plausibility / semantic checks** are the last line of defence even if cryptography fails.

**Redundancy Essentials:**
- [ ] **TMR** tolerates 1 faulty module; reliability = $R^2(3 - 2R)$ (with ideal voter).
- [ ] TMR improves reliability **only if** $R > 0.5$.
- [ ] **N-version programming** = software diversity; defeats common-mode bugs.
- [ ] **Hot standby** = takeover in microseconds; **Warm** = seconds; **Cold** = minutes.
- [ ] **Byzantine fault tolerance** needs $n \geq 3f + 1$ nodes.
- [ ] **Series reliability** $R_\text{sys} = \prod R_i$; **Parallel** uses binomial expansion.
- [ ] **Cost of redundancy** must be justified against the cost of failure (do a simple cost-benefit).

**DoS Defence:**
- [ ] CPS DoS spans **physical, MAC, network, transport, application, system, and architectural** layers.
- [ ] **Defence in depth** is mandatory; no single layer is sufficient.
- [ ] Common CPS DoS vectors: **jamming, flooding, protocol exploits, sensor blinding, blackhole routing, state-machine abuse**.
- [ ] **Watchdog timers** are the universal last-resort CPS safety mechanism.

**Numerical Skills Required:**
- [ ] Compute $A$, $A_\%$, annual downtime (in **minutes**).
- [ ] Compute $R_\text{TMR}$ given $R$ and $R_v$.
- [ ] Compute cost-benefit of TMR vs single-component deployment.
- [ ] Convert between nines of availability and yearly downtime: $\text{Downtime/yr} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ minutes.

**Common Pitfalls in the Exam:**
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting the voter reliability in TMR formulas.
- [ ] ❌ Stating the CIA priority in IT order (C-I-A) instead of CPS order (I-A-C).
- [ ] ❌ Mixing up $\text{MTTF}$ and $\text{MTBF}$ in the availability formula.
- [ ] ❌ Claiming HMAC provides non-repudiation (it does NOT).
- [ ] ❌ Computing downtime in hours when the question demands minutes.
- [ ] ❌ Ignoring the semantic / plausibility layer and focusing only on cryptography.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" two techniques, always structure the answer as a **comparison table** with at least 4–5 rows. KTU examiners award bonus marks for clear, structured, tabular comparisons. End every long answer with a one-sentence **"Conclusion / Justification"** statement that explicitly recommends the best technique for a named CPS sub-system (e.g., "Hence, ECDSA is preferred for firmware updates in Class-III medical implants").
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threat Modeling and Risk Assessment: Identifying and Assessing Risks in CPS, Threat Modeling Techniques, Risk Mitigation and Management Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT823 - SECURITY AND PRIVACY IN CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Threat Modeling and Risk Assessment: Identifying and Assessing Risks in CPS, Threat Modeling Techniques, Risk Mitigation and Management Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Threat Modeling in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)?

**Threat Modeling** is a structured, engineering-grade analytical process used to identify, classify, enumerate, and prioritize potential threats (cyber, physical, and kinetic) that could compromise the confidentiality, integrity, availability, safety, and reliability of a Cyber-Physical System. In the KTU 2024 scheme, threat modeling is positioned as the *proactive security counterpart* to penetration testing — instead of breaking the system, the engineer *imagines* the attacker's perspective and systematically maps out what could go wrong.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Threat modeling in CPS is a *repeatable, asset-centric, adversarial reasoning methodology* that combines system architecture decomposition, attacker profiling, and vulnerability correlation to produce a ranked set of threats with associated risk values, forming the primary input to the system's security-by-design and risk treatment lifecycle.

**Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** here refers to integrated computational–physical infrastructures such as Industrial Control Systems (ICS), SCADA networks, Smart Grids, Autonomous Vehicles, Medical Cyber-Physical Systems (MCPS), and IoT-based critical infrastructure. These systems differ from pure IT systems because a successful cyber attack can produce **physical-world consequences** — equipment damage, environmental release, loss of life.

## 1.2 What is Risk Assessment?

**Risk Assessment** is the *quantitative or qualitative evaluation* of the probability that a specific threat will exploit a specific vulnerability and the resulting adverse impact on CPS operations. It is the *scoring layer* that sits on top of threat modeling.

The universally accepted formulation used in KTU-aligned materials is:

> **Risk = f(Threat, Vulnerability, Asset Value, Impact, Likelihood)**

In its simplest engineering form, this collapses to:

> **Risk = Likelihood × Impact**

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The House Audit"**
> Imagine you are moving into a new house. Before you buy a lock, you would walk through every room and ask:
> 1. *What do I own that is valuable?* (Assets)
> 2. *Who might want to steal it?* (Threats)
> 3. *How could they get in?* (Vulnerabilities)
> 4. *What would happen if they succeeded?* (Impact)
> 5. *How likely is it?* (Likelihood)
> 6. *Should I buy a lock, a guard dog, an alarm, or just move?* (Mitigation)
>
> Threat modeling is this "house audit" applied to a CPS, except the "rooms" are PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, sensors, and the "valuables" are the physical processes themselves. The "lock" might be a firewall, an authentication protocol, or a fail-safe mechanical interlock.

## 1.4 The CPS-Specific Challenge

Unlike classical IT systems, CPS threat modeling must consider:

- **Safety ↔ Security Coupling:** A cyber-induced denial of service on a coolant pump is a *safety* failure, not just a security one.
- **Legacy Constraints:** Many CPS components run decades-old, unpatched firmware (e.g., Modbus devices from the 1970s).
- **Real-Time Determinism:** Security mechanisms cannot violate hard real-time deadlines.
- **Physical Consequences:** Threat impact includes kinetic effects, environmental damage, and human harm.
- **Heterogeneous Trust Boundaries:** OT (Operational Technology), IT, cloud, and edge all intersect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must be able to distinguish **threat**, **vulnerability**, **risk**, and **attack**. These four terms are *not* synonyms and are routinely tested as 3-mark definition questions in the University Exam.

## 1.5 Key Vocabulary (Mandatory for KTU Answers)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Asset** | Anything of value in the CPS (data, hardware, process, human life) |
| **Threat** | Any event or actor with the potential to cause harm |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness that can be exploited by a threat |
| **Risk** | The product of likelihood and impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability |
| **Threat Agent** | The entity that initiates the threat (insider, nation-state, malware, environmental) |
| **Attack Surface** | The set of all points where an unauthorized actor can attempt to enter or extract data |
| **Trust Boundary** | A location where data or control crosses between domains of differing trust |
| **Control Loop** | The sensor → controller → actuator feedback path in a CPS |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Threat Modeling Lifecycle (Universal Four-Step Model)

Every widely used threat modeling methodology — STRIDE, PASTA, VAST, Trike, OCTAVE — implements the same logical four-phase lifecycle. The KTU examiner expects you to enumerate these in any 14-mark question on threat modeling.

### Phase 1 — System Decomposition
- Model the CPS using **Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs)**.
- Identify **processes** (PLCs, controllers, cloud services), **data stores** (historian DB, configuration files), **external entities** (operators, sensors, remote vendors), and **data flows** (Modbus TCP, OPC UA, MQTT, DNP3).
- Mark every **trust boundary** explicitly.

### Phase 2 — Threat Identification
- Apply a structured classification framework (STRIDE, etc.) to every DFD element.
- Enumerate threat agents (insider, script kiddie, APT, nation-state, environmental).

### Phase 3 — Vulnerability & Control Mapping
- For each threat, list the vulnerabilities that enable it.
- Map existing controls (firewalls, authentication, encryption, fail-safe interlocks).

### Phase 4 — Risk Prioritization & Mitigation
- Score each threat (DREAD, CVSS, custom).
- Rank by risk score.
- Select mitigation strategy (avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept).

## 2.2 The Six Major Threat Modeling Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly names **"Threat Modeling Techniques"** as a plural topic. You must know at least STRIDE, DREAD, PASTA, and Attack Trees for a 14-mark question.

### 2.2.1 STRIDE (Microsoft, 1999)
A mnemonic for six threat categories applied to every DFD element:

| Letter | Threat | Security Property Violated |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | Authentication |
| **T** | **Tampering** | Integrity |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | Non-repudiation |
| **I** | **Information Disclosure** | Confidentiality |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | Availability |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | Authorization |

### 2.2.2 DREAD (Microsoft Risk Scoring)
A *quantitative* scoring framework (each factor 1–10):

| Letter | Factor | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **D** | **Damage** | Severity of harm if exploited |
| **R** | **Reproducibility** | Ease of reproducing the attack |
| **E** | **Exploitability** | Ease of launching the attack |
| **A** | **Affected Users** | Scale of impact |
| **D** | **Discoverability** | Ease of finding the flaw |

### 2.2.3 PASTA (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis)
A seven-stage *attacker-centric*, risk-driven framework that aligns business objectives with technical threat analysis. Stages:
1. Define Business Objectives
2. Define Technical Scope
3. Application Decomposition
4. Threat Analysis
5. Vulnerability & Weakness Analysis
6. Attack Modeling
7. Risk & Impact Analysis

### 2.2.4 Attack Trees
A hierarchical tree diagram where the **root node** is the attacker's goal (e.g., "Shutdown Power Substation") and **child nodes** are sub-goals or attack vectors. Branches are **OR** (alternative paths) or **AND** (all required).

### 2.2.5 VAST (Visual, Agile, Simple Threat)
A scalable methodology that produces *process-flow* and *data-flow* threat models, often used in agile CPS DevSecOps pipelines.

### 2.2.6 Kill Chain / MITRE ATT&CK for ICS
Adapted from Lockheed Martin's Cyber Kill Chain, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS is a curated knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques observed against industrial control systems — directly applicable to CPS.

## 2.3 The Risk Equation — The Heart of the Module

The canonical KTU-grade risk formulation is:

> **Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact**

In normalized scoring systems (DREAD, CVSS), this becomes additive:

> **Risk Score = Likelihood × Impact**

Where:
- **Likelihood** ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} (Rare, Unlikely, Possible, Likely, Almost Certain)
- **Impact** ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} (Negligible, Minor, Moderate, Major, Catastrophic)
- **Risk Score** ∈ {1, 2, 3, ..., 25}

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Units / Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk (qualitative) | $\text{Risk} = L \times I$ | 1 to 25 | $L$ = Likelihood, $I$ = Impact |
| Risk (DREAD) | $\text{Risk} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}$ | 1 to 10 | Average of 5 factors |
| CVSS Base Score | $\text{BASE} = f(\text{AV}, \text{AC}, \text{PR}, \text{UI}, \text{S}, \text{C}, \text{I}, \text{A})$ | 0.0 to 10.0 | First.org standard |
| Annual Loss Expectancy | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Currency/year | SLE = Single Loss Expectancy, ARO = Annualized Rate of Occurrence |
| Return on Security Investment | $\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE} - \text{Control Cost}}{\text{Control Cost}}$ | Ratio | Higher is better |
| Exposure Factor | $\text{EF} = \frac{\text{Loss from one event}}{\text{Asset Value}}$ | 0 to 1 | Used in SLE = AV × EF |
| STRIDE Coverage | $\text{Coverage} = \frac{\text{DFD elements STRIDE-applied}}{\text{Total DFD elements}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Quality metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Never confuse **SLE** (Single Loss Expectancy — one event) with **ALE** (Annual Loss Expectancy — per year). The examiner will test this.

## 2.5 Qualitative Risk Matrix (Standard 5×5)

| Likelihood ↓ \ Impact → | Negligible (1) | Minor (2) | Moderate (3) | Major (4) | Catastrophic (5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Almost Certain (5) | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | **25** |
| Likely (4) | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| Possible (3) | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| Unlikely (2) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| Rare (1) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

- **Green (1–4):** Acceptable risk — monitor
- **Yellow (5–12):** Mitigation required — planned controls
- **Orange (13–19):** High risk — immediate action, senior management
- **Red (20–25):** Unacceptable — system redesign or operation halt

## 2.6 Risk Mitigation & Management Strategies (The Four "T"s)

> [!NOTE]
> This is *the* classic KTU question. You will be asked to "explain the four risk response strategies" in either a 3-mark or 14-mark question.

| Strategy | Action | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Avoidance (Terminate)** | Eliminate the risk by removing the cause | Discontinue a vulnerable legacy Modbus link; replace with OPC UA |
| **Risk Transfer (Treat)** | Shift financial or operational impact to a third party | Cyber insurance, outsource to MSSP, SLA with cloud vendor |
| **Risk Mitigation (Treat)** | Reduce likelihood or impact through controls | Deploy firewalls, IDS/IPS, patching, network segmentation |
| **Risk Acceptance (Tolerate)** | Formally accept the residual risk | Sign-off by CISO when cost of mitigation exceeds potential loss |

**Additional KTU-level strategies:**
- **Defense in Depth:** Layered controls (perimeter, network, host, application, physical).
- **Zero Trust Architecture:** Never trust, always verify; microsegmentation.
- **Deception Technology:** Honeypots, decoy PLCs (e.g., Conpot).
- **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS):** Independent mechanical/electrical interlocks (IEC 61508/61511).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grid:** NERC CIP standards mandate threat modeling and risk assessment.
- **Automotive:** ISO/SAE 21434 (Cybersecurity Engineering for road vehicles) requires TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment).
- **Medical Devices:** FDA premarket cybersecurity guidance requires threat modeling.
- **Industrial:** IEC 62443 mandates a Cybersecurity Management System (CSMS) with risk-driven zoning and conduit definitions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cross-Reference:** This topic directly supports the Course Outcome (CO) for "Apply threat modeling and risk assessment techniques to design secure CPS architectures." Examiners often map a 14-mark question to this CO at the Apply / Analyze level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — DREAD Risk Score for a CPS Threat

**Scenario:** An attacker exploits an unpatched buffer overflow in a Human-Machine Interface (HMI) running Windows 7 in a water treatment plant, allowing remote code execution and manipulation of chlorine dosing.

We score each DREAD factor on the integer scale 1 (low) to 10 (high):

| DREAD Factor | Score | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **D**amage | 9 | Chlorine overdose → public health emergency, possible fatalities |
| **R**eproducibility | 7 | Publicly available exploit code; reproducible with Metasploit |
| **E**xploitability | 6 | Requires network access to OT VLAN; no authentication on legacy HMI |
| **A**ffected Users | 8 | Entire municipality dependent on plant; ≈ 200,000 people |
| **D**iscoverability | 8 | Nmap scan reveals OS version; CVE is public |

### Step-by-Step Calculation

We compute the arithmetic mean of the five DREAD factors to obtain a normalized risk score.

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{DREAD}} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}
$$

We now substitute the assigned values into the numerator. The variables D, R, E, A, D each represent the corresponding DREAD dimension scores assigned above.

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{DREAD}} = \frac{9 + 7 + 6 + 8 + 8}{5}
$$

We now perform the summation in the numerator. Adding the five integer scores together produces the total summed risk component.

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{DREAD}} = \frac{38}{5}
$$

We now perform the division operation to obtain the final normalized risk score. Dividing 38 by 5 produces the average DREAD score.

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{DREAD}} = 7.6
$$

**Interpretation:** A score of **7.6/10** places this threat in the **High Risk** band (typically ≥ 7.0 = High, 4.0–6.9 = Medium, < 4.0 = Low). The CPS owner must implement immediate mitigation (e.g., HMI replacement, VLAN segmentation, IPS rules).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** DREAD has been deprecated by Microsoft in favor of the **CVSS 3.1** base score. However, KTU examiners still expect DREAD to be explained in 2024 scheme papers because it appears in the prescribed module syllabus.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Quantitative Risk: ALE Calculation

**Scenario:** A SCADA control center contains historian servers valued at ₹50,00,000. Industry data suggests a ransomware attack on such systems occurs on average 0.4 times per year (ARO = 0.4). When such an attack succeeds, it encrypts approximately 60% of the historical data, causing 2 weeks of operational downtime during restoration.

### Step 1 — Compute Exposure Factor (EF)

The Exposure Factor represents the proportion of the asset's value that is lost in a single occurrence of the threat.

$$
\text{EF} = \frac{\text{Loss from single event}}{\text{Asset Value}}
$$

Substituting the stated values — a 60% data loss on a ₹50,00,000 asset — yields the fractional exposure.

$$
\text{EF} = \frac{0.60 \times 50{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000}
$$

Simplifying the numerator and denominator, we get a clean fractional result.

$$
\text{EF} = 0.60
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

The SLE is the monetary value lost in a single occurrence, obtained by multiplying the asset value by the exposure factor.

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF}
$$

Plugging in ₹50,00,000 asset value and 0.60 exposure factor.

$$
\text{SLE} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60
$$

$$
\text{SLE} = 30{,}00{,}000
$$

**Result:** A single ransomware incident costs the organization ₹30,00,000.

### Step 3 — Compute Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)

The ALE annualizes the expected loss by multiplying the SLE by the rate at which such events are expected to occur per year.

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}
$$

Substituting SLE = ₹30,00,000 and ARO = 0.4.

$$
\text{ALE} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4
$$

$$
\text{ALE} = 12{,}00{,}000
$$

**Interpretation:** The organization should not spend more than **₹12,00,000/year** on a control to mitigate this threat and still break even. This becomes the budget ceiling for the proposed firewall + EDR + backup solution.

### Step 4 — Compute ROSI (Return on Security Investment)

Suppose the proposed control package costs ₹4,00,000/year to deploy and operate, and is expected to reduce the ARO by 75% (new effective ARO = 0.1).

We first compute the new (post-control) ALE.

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{new}} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1 = 3{,}00{,}000
$$

We then compute the ALE reduction (the monetary benefit of the control).

$$
\Delta \text{ALE} = \text{ALE}_{\text{old}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{new}}
$$

$$
\Delta \text{ALE} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}00{,}000
$$

We now apply the ROSI formula to obtain the return ratio of the security investment.

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{\Delta \text{ALE} - \text{Control Cost}}{\text{Control Cost}}
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 1.25
$$

**Interpretation:** A ROSI of **1.25 (or 125%)** means the control yields ₹1.25 of risk reduction for every ₹1.00 spent — a financially justified investment.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — STRIDE vs DFD Element Type

One of the highest-yield KTU comparisons. STRIDE-per-element tells the student *which threat categories apply to which DFD element*.

| DFD Element Type | S | T | R | I | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Process (e.g., PLC)** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Data Store (e.g., Historian DB)** |  | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |  |
| **Data Flow (e.g., Modbus TCP)** |  | ✓ |  | ✓ | ✓ |  |
| **External Entity (e.g., Operator)** | ✓ |  | ✓ |  |  |  |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory trick for KTU:** "Processes get all six — they're the most attacked element because they execute logic. External entities can be spoofed (S) and repudiate (R) but cannot be tampered with directly."

## 3.4 The Risk Treatment Decision Tree

When a risk is identified, the engineer walks the following logic:

1. If **Risk ≥ 20 (Red)**: mandatory mitigation or system redesign; cannot accept.
2. If **13 ≤ Risk < 20 (Orange)**: senior management approval required; mitigation mandatory.
3. If **5 ≤ Risk < 13 (Yellow)**: mitigation planned; document in risk register.
4. If **Risk < 5 (Green)**: monitor; periodic review.
5. If mitigation cost > ALE: document and **accept** (with sign-off).

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Threat Modeling Walkthrough (PASTA on a Smart Meter)

**Step 1 — Define Objectives:** Protect residential smart meter billing integrity and customer privacy.

**Step 2 — Technical Scope:** Meter firmware, AMI head-end, MDMS (Meter Data Management System), communication links (RF mesh, cellular).

**Step 3 — Decomposition:** DFD shows meter → concentrator → head-end → MDMS.

**Step 4 — Threat Analysis:** STRIDE yields, e.g., tampering of consumption data (T), spoofing of meter ID (S).

**Step 5 — Vulnerability Analysis:** Weak cryptographic key management in legacy meters.

**Step 6 — Attack Modeling:** Attack tree: root = "submit false consumption reading"; branches include "compromise meter firmware via JTAG", "MITM on RF mesh".

**Step 7 — Risk & Impact Analysis:** Score = 18 (Orange); mitigation = firmware signing + hardware root of trust.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Universal Threat Modeling Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step A: System Decomposition via DFD] --> nodeB[Step B: Identify Assets and Trust Boundaries]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step C: Apply STRIDE per DFD Element]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step D: Enumerate Vulnerabilities]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step E: Score via DREAD or CVSS]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Step F: Risk in Acceptable Range}
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Step G: Document and Monitor]
    nodeF -->|No| nodeH[Step H: Select Mitigation Strategy]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Step I: Implement Controls]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step J: Reassess Residual Risk]
    nodeJ --> nodeF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure Interpretation:** This loop represents the iterative nature of threat modeling — it is *never a one-shot activity*. The reassessment loop (J → F) is what distinguishes mature CPS security programs from checkbox compliance.

## 4.2 STRIDE Threat Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot[STRIDE Threat Universe] --> nodeS[S: Spoofing]
    nodeRoot --> nodeT[T: Tampering]
    nodeRoot --> nodeR[R: Repudiation]
    nodeRoot --> nodeI[I: Information Disclosure]
    nodeRoot --> nodeD[D: Denial of Service]
    nodeRoot --> nodeE[E: Elevation of Privilege]
    nodeS --> nodeS1[Identity forgery, credential theft]
    nodeT --> nodeT1[Data modification, firmware tampering]
    nodeR --> nodeR1[Log deletion, action denial]
    nodeI --> nodeI1[Eavesdropping, data exfiltration]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Resource exhaustion, protocol flood]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Privilege escalation, root access]
```

## 4.3 CPS Trust Zone Architecture (Zoning Model — IEC 62443)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeExt[External Zone: Internet, Vendors, Remote Users] -->|Firewall FW1| nodeDMZ[DMZ Zone: Jump Hosts, Patch Server]
    nodeDMZ -->|Firewall FW2| nodeIT[IT Zone: ERP, Email, Workstations]
    nodeIT -->|Firewall FW3| nodeOT[OT Zone: SCADA, HMI, Historian]
    nodeOT -->|Unidirectional Diode| nodeField[Field Zone: PLCs, RTUs, Sensors, Actuators]
    nodeOT -.->|Read-only mirror| nodeSafety[Safety Zone: SIS, Trip Systems]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** When asked to "design a CPS security architecture", always draw **at least three zones** (IT, OT, Field) separated by firewalls, and place the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS) on a physically isolated network**. The unidirectional diode between OT and Field is a frequently expected element.

## 4.4 Attack Tree — "Compromise a Substation PLC"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeGoal[Goal: Compromise Substation PLC] --> nodeA1[OR: Network-Based Attack]
    nodeGoal --> nodeA2[OR: Physical Access Attack]
    nodeGoal --> nodeA3[OR: Supply Chain Attack]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB1[AND: Bypass Firewall AND Exploit CVE in Firmware]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB2[AND: Phish Operator AND Use Stolen VPN Credentials]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB3[AND: Reach Substation AND Open Cabinet AND Plug Laptop into Serial Port]
    nodeA3 --> nodeB4[AND: Compromise Vendor AND Inject Malware into Firmware Update]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Attack Tree:** Branches labelled **OR** are alternative paths — the attacker only needs *one* to succeed. Branches labelled **AND** require *all* sub-goals in that branch to succeed. Attack trees are commonly asked in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions because they elegantly combine multiple threats into a single visual reasoning artifact.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Risk Assessment Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn1[Asset Inventory] --> nodeEngine[Risk Assessment Engine]
    nodeIn2[Threat Catalog STRIDE PASTA] --> nodeEngine
    nodeIn3[Vulnerability Database CVE ICSA] --> nodeEngine
    nodeIn4[Impact Criteria] --> nodeEngine
    nodeIn5[Likelihood Data] --> nodeEngine
    nodeEngine --> nodeOut1[Risk Register]
    nodeEngine --> nodeOut2[Risk Matrix Heatmap]
    nodeEngine --> nodeOut3[Mitigation Recommendations]
    nodeEngine --> nodeOut4[Residual Risk Score]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure Interpretation:** This block diagram shows the *inputs* (left), the central processing element (Risk Assessment Engine — typically a GRC tool like ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, or open-source like ERAM/ISO 27005 tools), and the *outputs* (right) that feed into the organization's risk treatment workflow.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
