## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define threat modeling. List any two threat modeling techniques used in Cyber-Physical Systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Threat modeling is a systematic and structured engineering process used to identify, classify, and prioritize potential threats to a Cyber-Physical System by analyzing its architecture, assets, data flows, and trust boundaries from an adversary's perspective. It produces a ranked set of threats that informs the design of security controls.

**Two techniques (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **STRIDE** — Microsoft mnemonic for Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege.
2. **PASTA** — Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis, a seven-stage attacker-centric risk-driven methodology.

*(Acceptable alternates: DREAD, Attack Trees, VAST, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between threat, vulnerability, and risk with a CPS example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each term with example):**

| Term | Definition | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Threat** | Any event or actor with the potential to cause harm | A nation-state APT group targeting a power grid |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness that can be exploited by a threat | Unpatched CVE-2021-XXXX in a Schneider Electric PLC |
| **Risk** | The product of likelihood and impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability | High risk — the unpatched PLC in a substation has a 70% chance of being compromised in 12 months, with potential grid-wide blackout |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A:**
> Students frequently write "threat = vulnerability = risk" in 3-mark definition questions. The examiner *will* award **zero marks** for this. You must show the *causal chain*: **Threat × Vulnerability → Risk**. Use distinct words for each concept. A well-drawn mini-table earns full marks faster than paragraphs.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question 3. (A) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the **STRIDE** threat modeling framework in detail. Apply STRIDE to identify the threats for a **SCADA-based water treatment plant** with at least four DFD elements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **Attack Tree** methodology. Construct an attack tree for the goal *"Manipulate the chlorine dosing level in a water treatment SCADA system"*. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question 3(A)

#### Part (a) — STRIDE Framework Applied to a Water Treatment Plant (7 marks)

**STRIDE Framework Explanation (3 marks):**
STRIDE is a mnemonic developed by Microsoft that classifies all cyber threats into six categories, each violating a specific security property. Spoofing violates authentication, Tampering violates integrity, Repudiation violates non-repudiation, Information Disclosure violates confidentiality, Denial of Service violates availability, and Elevation of Privilege violates authorization. STRIDE is applied to every element of a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) — namely, processes, data stores, data flows, and external entities.

**System Description (1 mark):**
The water treatment plant SCADA system consists of:
- **External Entity (E1):** Plant Operator
- **Process (P1):** SCADA Server (HMI software)
- **Process (P2):** PLC controlling chlorine dosing
- **Data Store (D1):** Historian Database storing sensor logs
- **Data Flow (F1):** Modbus TCP link from SCADA server to PLC
- **Data Flow (F2):** OPC UA link from SCADA to Historian

**STRIDE Application Table (3 marks):**

| DFD Element | S | T | R | I | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E1: Operator** | Credential theft via phishing | — | Operator denies issuing valve command | — | — | — |
| **P1: SCADA Server** | Forged admin login | Malware injection via USB | Log tampering | Credential dump exposure | CPU/memory exhaustion | Local privilege escalation to root |
| **P2: Chlorine PLC** | Spoofed Modbus identity | Tamper with setpoint register | — | Eavesdrop sensor values | Stop chlorine pump | Upload malicious ladder logic |
| **D1: Historian** | — | Backdated log entry | Deletion of audit records | Exfiltration of consumption data | Ransomware encryption | — |
| **F1: Modbus TCP** | — | MITM setpoint modification | — | Passive traffic capture | Modbus flood | — |
| **F2: OPC UA** | Stolen X.509 cert | Payload injection | — | TLS strip attack | Connection flood | — |

---

#### Part (b) — Attack Tree for Chlorine Dosing Manipulation (7 marks)

**Attack Tree Methodology Explanation (2 marks):**
An attack tree is a hierarchical, goal-oriented diagram in which the root represents the attacker's primary objective, and child nodes represent sub-goals, attack vectors, or conditions. Branches use **OR** (alternative paths — any one suffices) or **AND** (all conditions required). Leaves are atomic attack steps. The tree is evaluated using Boolean logic and assigned cost/probability/skill values at each leaf.

**Attack Tree Construction (5 marks):**

```
ROOT GOAL: Manipulate Chlorine Dosing Level (overdose or underdose)
│
├── OR: Network-Based Attack Path
│   ├── AND: Compromise SCADA Server
│   │       ├── 1. Phish operator credentials
│   │       └── 2. Exploit unpatched HMI software (e.g., CVE-2022-XXXX)
│   ├── AND: Modbus MITM Attack
│   │       ├── 1. Gain access to OT VLAN via misconfigured switch
│   │       └── 2. Use Modbus MITM proxy to inject setpoint register value
│   └── AND: Direct PLC Exploit
│           ├── 1. Discover PLC on Shodan (no firewall)
│           └── 2. Upload malicious ladder logic via vendor tool
│
├── OR: Insider Attack Path
│   ├── AND: Disgruntled Operator
│   │       ├── 1. Authenticate to HMI with own credentials
│   │       └── 2. Manually override chlorine setpoint
│   └── AND: Vendor Backdoor
│           ├── 1. Use default maintenance account
│           └── 2. Remotely access vendor support tunnel
│
└── OR: Physical Attack Path
    ├── AND: Cabinet Breach
    │       ├── 1. Physically access PLC cabinet (lock pick or key)
    │       └── 2. Plug laptop into programming port (USB/serial)
    └── AND: Sensor Spoofing
            ├── 1. Replace chlorine sensor with fake one
            └── 2. PLC reads safe level, dosing controller over-corrects
```

**Mitigation Countermeasures (built into examiner's marking scheme):**
- Network segmentation (OT VLAN isolation)
- Strong authentication (MFA for operators)
- Removal of default vendor accounts
- Physical locks and tamper-evident seals on cabinets
- Cryptographic integrity on Modbus setpoint commands (upgrade to Modbus/TLS or OPC UA)

---

### Question 3. (B) [Internal Choice — KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the **DREAD** risk scoring methodology. Compute the DREAD score for a threat where D = 8, R = 7, E = 6, A = 9, D = 5, and classify the resulting risk. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **four risk mitigation strategies** (Avoidance, Transfer, Mitigation, Acceptance) with one CPS example for each. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question 3(B)

#### Part (a) — DREAD Risk Scoring (7 marks)

**DREAD Methodology (4 marks):**
DREAD is a Microsoft-developed quantitative risk scoring framework that assigns a score from 1 to 10 to each of five dimensions of a threat. The final risk score is typically the arithmetic mean of these five values. The five dimensions are:
- **D**amage: severity of harm if the threat is realized (data loss, physical damage, financial loss)
- **R**eproducibility: how easily the attack can be reproduced
- **E**xploitability: how much effort, skill, and access is needed to launch the attack
- **A**ffected Users: scale of impact in terms of people, systems, or processes affected
- **D**iscoverability: how easily the underlying vulnerability can be found by an attacker

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{DREAD}} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}
$$

Substituting the values D = 8, R = 7, E = 6, A = 9, D = 5.

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{DREAD}} = \frac{8 + 7 + 6 + 9 + 5}{5}
$$

Summing the numerator.

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{DREAD}} = \frac{35}{5}
$$

Dividing.

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{DREAD}} = 7.0
$$

**Risk Classification (1 mark):**
A DREAD score of **7.0/10** falls in the **High Risk** band (typically: 0–3 = Low, 4–6 = Medium, 7–10 = High). This threat requires **immediate mitigation**, escalation to the CISO, and inclusion in the next patch cycle.

---

#### Part (b) — Four Risk Mitigation Strategies (7 marks — 1.75 marks each)

**1. Risk Avoidance (Terminate):** Eliminate the risk entirely by removing the risky activity, asset, or technology. The CPS owner *refuses to accept* the exposure.
*CPS Example:* Discontinue the use of legacy Modbus TCP (cleartext, no authentication) and replace it with **OPC UA over TLS**, which provides authentication, integrity, and confidentiality by design.

**2. Risk Transfer (Treat):** Shift the financial or operational impact of a realized risk to a third party. The risk itself is not reduced, but its consequences are absorbed elsewhere.
*CPS Example:* Purchase a **cyber insurance policy** with a ₹10 crore coverage limit specifically for OT incidents, or sign a **Managed Security Service Provider (MSSP)** SLA in which the vendor assumes liability for 24×7 SOC monitoring of the SCADA network.

**3. Risk Mitigation (Treat):** Reduce either the likelihood or the impact (or both) of the risk through the deployment of security controls. This is the most common strategy.
*CPS Example:* Implement **defense-in-depth** controls: (i) network segmentation with firewalls between IT and OT zones, (ii) multi-factor authentication for HMI operators, (iii) Intrusion Detection System (IDS) with Modbus-aware signatures, (iv) regular patching cycles, and (v) Safety Instrumented System (SIS) as a last-resort mechanical interlock.

**4. Risk Acceptance (Tolerate):** Formally acknowledge the residual risk and decide to operate with it, typically when the cost of mitigation exceeds the expected loss. Acceptance must be **signed off** by an accountable authority (CISO, Plant Manager).
*CPS Example:* A non-critical building management PLC running outdated firmware is not patched because the cost of downtime during patching (₹8,00,000) exceeds the annual expected loss from compromise (₹3,00,000). The CISO signs a **Risk Acceptance Memo** and the decision is recorded in the risk register with quarterly review.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (14 marks):**
> 1. **DREAD Question Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly use the geometric mean or weighted average. KTU's expected answer is the **simple arithmetic mean**. Using any other formula will lose 1 mark unless you explicitly justify it.
> 2. **Mitigation Strategy Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **Transfer** with **Mitigation**. Remember: *Transfer* shifts the impact; *Mitigation* reduces the impact or likelihood. Insurance is **Transfer**; a firewall is **Mitigation**.
> 3. **Missing Examples:** For 14-mark questions, every concept must be backed by a **CPS-specific example** (SCADA, PLC, smart grid, autonomous vehicle, medical device). Generic IT examples (web servers, databases) earn only partial credit.
> 4. **Diagram Quality:** For attack tree questions, the diagram must show **OR and AND branches clearly** and label leaves. A hand-drawn tree with 5 leaves and no labels loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Risk Score Boundaries:** Always state the **risk band** after a numerical calculation. A score of 7.0 is meaningless without saying "High Risk — immediate action required".

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Threat Modeling and Risk Assessment)**

### Definitions (must know verbatim)
- **Threat:** potential cause of harm (actor + capability + intent + opportunity)
- **Vulnerability:** weakness that enables a threat
- **Risk:** probability × impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability
- **Asset:** anything of value
- **Threat Agent:** entity initiating the threat

### Frameworks (must be able to draw/explain)
- **STRIDE** → 6 threat categories (S, T, R, I, D, E) applied per DFD element
- **DREAD** → 5 risk factors scored 1–10, mean = risk score
- **PASTA** → 7-stage attacker-centric methodology
- **Attack Tree** → OR/AND tree, root = attacker goal
- **CVSS** → 0.0–10.0 base score
- **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** → adversary tactic database

### Formulas (must memorize)
- $\text{Risk} = L \times I$ (Likelihood × Impact)
- $\text{DREAD} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}$
- $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$
- $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$
- $\text{ROSI} = \frac{\Delta \text{ALE} - \text{Control Cost}}{\text{Control Cost}}$

### Risk Treatment Strategies (the Four T's)
1. **Avoidance** — eliminate the risk
2. **Transfer** — shift impact (insurance, MSSP)
3. **Mitigation** — reduce likelihood/impact (controls)
4. **Acceptance** — formally tolerate residual risk (sign-off required)

### CPS-Specific Differentiators (high-yield for KTU)
- CPS threats cause **physical-world** consequences
- **Safety** and **security** are coupled (cyber → kinetic harm)
- **IEC 62443** zoning: IT → DMZ → OT → Field
- **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** are the *last line of defense*
- Legacy protocols (Modbus, DNP3) are **inherently insecure**
- Real-time constraints limit security overhead

### KTU 2024 Examiner Watchlist
- Do not confuse **SLE** vs **ALE**
- Always state the **risk band** after a numerical risk score
- Use **CPS examples**, not generic IT examples, in 14-mark answers
- For attack trees, label **OR/AND branches** and **leaf actions**
- In STRIDE questions, create a **per-DFD-element table** for full marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety and Security Integration: Safety-Critical Systems and Their Security Implications, Techniques for Integrating Safety and Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT823 - SECURITY AND PRIVACY IN CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety and Security Integration: Safety-Critical Systems and Their Security Implications, Techniques for Integrating Safety and Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Safety-Critical Systems (SCS) — Formal Definition

A **Safety-Critical System (SCS)** is a system whose incorrect operation, failure, or compromise can directly result in catastrophic consequences including loss of human life, significant injuries, severe environmental damage, or major financial/societal losses. In the KTU 2024 CPS security framework, an SCS is any cyber-physical system (CPS) where the physical process under control presents an inherent *hazard* (a source of potential harm) whose realization may be triggered by either **random/accidental faults** (safety) or **intentional malicious actions** (security).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A Safety-Critical System is a system whose failure or malfunction has the potential to directly cause unacceptable risk of harm to people, the environment, or the physical asset under its control. The *safety* property is a **non-functional, emergent property** that depends on correct interaction of hardware, software, sensors, actuators, communications, and the controlled physical process.

### 1.1.1 Standard SCS Classification

| Class | Domain | Governing Standard | Integrity Metric |
|-------|--------|--------------------|------------------|
| Avionics | Aircraft flight control | DO-178C / DO-254 | **DAL A–E** |
| Automotive | Vehicle dynamics | ISO 26262 | **ASIL A–D** |
| Industrial Process | Plants, refineries | IEC 61511 | **SIL 1–4** |
| General Electrical/Electronic | Cross-industry | IEC 61508 | **SIL 1–4** |
| Medical Devices | Patient care | IEC 62304 | **Class A–C** |
| Railway | Signaling, train control | EN 50128 / EN 50129 | **SIL 0–4** |
| Nuclear | Reactor control | IEC 61513 / IEEE 603 | **Category A–C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO3 — Analyze safety and security risks in CPS** and **CO4 — Design integrated protection mechanisms for CPS**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — "The Hospital ICU Analogy"

Imagine a modern **Neonatal ICU** in a hospital. The system consists of:
- **Physical process:** A premature newborn requiring precise oxygen, thermal, and drug regulation.
- **Cyber layer:** Ventilators, infusion pumps, heart-rate monitors, central servers, Wi-Fi connectivity, electronic health records.
- **Operators:** Doctors and nurses making critical decisions under time pressure.

Now consider three types of failure:

1. **Random Fault (Pure Safety):** A sensor wire corrodes and gives a false low-oxygen reading. The ventilator pushes too much O₂, blinding the infant (retinopathy of prematurity). Nobody attacked the system — the *world* broke it.

2. **Intentional Malicious Action (Pure Security):** A ransomware actor encrypts the hospital servers, locking out the central monitoring station. Doctors cannot see patient vitals. The ventilator itself is fine, but the *information layer* is broken.

3. **Safety-Security Coupling (The New Reality):** A hacker exploits a Wi-Fi vulnerability, **silently falsifies the oxygen sensor reading** to show normal levels while the infant is actually being over-ventilated. Here, a *security* breach caused a *safety* catastrophe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Insight:** In modern CPS, **safety and security are not independent**. A security compromise of a sensor, actuator, controller, or communication channel is functionally indistinguishable from a random hardware fault *at the safety layer*, but it is far more dangerous because it is *targeted, repeatable, and scalable*.

---

## 1.3 Why Traditional Safety Engineering is No Longer Sufficient

Historically, safety-critical systems relied on **isolation and redundancy** (the *air-gap* myth). The design assumption was:

> *"If the safety-critical system is physically isolated from public networks, and uses only well-vetted hardware/software, then external attacks cannot reach it."*

This assumption is **broken** in 2024 because:

1. **IIoT/Industry 4.0 integration** — plants are now networked for efficiency.
2. **Remote diagnostics and OTA updates** — vendors need bidirectional access.
3. **Supply-chain attacks** — hardware/firmware trojans in components.
4. **Insider threats** — disgruntled employees with system access.
5. **Cascading dependencies** — IT compromise → OT compromise.

> [!WARNING]
> **Historical Evidence — KTU Expectation:**
> - **Stuxnet (2010):** Compromised Siemens S7-300 PLCs controlling Iranian uranium centrifuges; altered motor frequencies while reporting normal values. Classic *sensor falsification* attack.
> - **TRITON/TRISIS (2017):** Targeted Schneider Electric Triconex Safety Instrumented System (SIS); if successful, would have disabled the *last line of safety defense* in a petrochemical plant.
> - **Jeep Cherokee Hack (2015):** Remote exploit of the UConnect head unit → control of steering, brakes, and engine over the cellular network.
> - **Oldsmar Water Treatment (2021):** Attacker remotely increased sodium hydroxide (NaOH) concentration 100× via TeamViewer.

---

## 1.4 Safety vs. Security — Side-by-Side Conceptual Map

| Dimension | **Safety** | **Security** |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| **Origin of threat** | Random / accidental | Intentional / malicious |
| **Adversary model** | Acts of nature, wear-out, human error | Motivated attacker with resources |
| **Adversary intelligence** | None (stochastic) | Strategic, adaptive, learning |
| **Primary goal** | Prevent harm to people, environment, asset | Preserve CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) |
| **Priority during failure** | **Availability + Integrity** | **Confidentiality + Integrity** |
| **Methodology** | FMEA, FTA, HAZOP, STPA | Threat modeling, attack trees, STRIDE |
| **Standardization** | IEC 61508, ISO 26262, DO-178C | IEC 62443, ISO 21434, NIST CSF |
| **Verification** | Probabilistic risk assessment | Penetration testing, red teaming |
| **Failure direction** | Component fails → hazard triggered | Attacker induces *apparent* failure |
| **Time scale** | Long MTTF planning (years) | Threat landscape evolves monthly |
| **Metric** | PFD, PFH, **SIL/ASIL** | Attack probability, **SL (1–4)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conflict:** Safety systems are designed to **fail-safe** (e.g., a railway signal goes to red on power loss). A security attack may *deliberately* trigger the safe state, which becomes a **denial-of-service (DoS)** on operations, or may *hide* the failure to delay operator response.

---

## 1.5 The Integrated Triad for CPS

In modern CPS, we must engineer for a **Safety + Security + Privacy (SSP)** triad. The operational goals are:

- **Safety** → no unacceptable harm during the operational lifetime.
- **Security** → no unauthorized state transitions, even under active adversarial pressure.
- **Privacy** → no unauthorized disclosure of process data, identity, or behavioral patterns.

This is formalized in the **STAMP (Systems-Theoretic Accident Modeling and Processes)** paradigm by Prof. Nancy Leveson (MIT), which treats safety and security as **control problems** on the same hierarchical state model of the system.

---

## 1.6 Module 3 Learning Outcomes — KTU Mapping

| CO ID | Outcome Statement | Bloom Level |
|-------|-------------------|-------------|
| **CO3** | Identify and analyze the unique safety-security interdependencies in CPS | L3 — Apply |
| **CO4** | Apply and evaluate integration techniques (defense-in-depth, STPA-Sec, co-engineering) | L4 — Analyze |
| **CO5** | Design a layered protection strategy for a representative CPS | L5 — Evaluate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Safety-Critical Systems

### 2.1.1 The Dependability Tree (Avizienis Taxonomy)

A safety-critical system is fundamentally a **dependable system**. According to the Avizienis dependability model (the KTU-recommended formal framework), dependability has the following attributes and threats:

**Attributes (the "good" properties we want):**
- **Reliability** — continuity of correct service.
- **Availability** — readiness for correct service.
- **Safety** — absence of catastrophic consequences.
- **Integrity** — absence of improper system state alterations.
- **Maintainability** — ability to undergo modifications and repairs.
- **Confidentiality** — absence of unauthorized disclosure.

**Threats (the "bad" events we defend against):**
- **Fault** — adjudged or hypothesized cause of an error (random, systematic, intentional).
- **Error** — system state that may lead to a failure.
- **Failure** — event where the system deviates from its specified service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fault → Error → Failure Chain:** A *fault* (e.g., bit-flip, malware injection) creates an *error* (corrupted memory state), which propagates to a *failure* (system delivers wrong output). In safety, we trace this **bottom-up**. In security, we trace it **top-down** (from adversary goal → attack vector → fault). Integration requires both views.

### 2.1.2 The Bathtub Curve and Reliability Theory

The reliability of a hardware component follows the **bathtub curve** as a function of operating time $t$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

where:
- $R(t)$ = reliability (probability of survival up to time $t$), dimensionless.
- $\lambda$ = constant failure rate, in failures per hour (fph) or **FIT** (failures in time, $1 \text{ FIT} = 10^{-9}$ failures/hour).
- $t$ = operating time, in hours.

The **hazard rate** $h(t)$ varies across the three life-cycle regions:

1. **Infant mortality** ($\lambda$ decreasing): manufacturing defects, burn-in.
2. **Useful life** ($\lambda$ constant): random failures dominate.
3. **Wear-out** ($\lambda$ increasing): aging, fatigue.

The **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** for a constant $\lambda$ is:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Quantitative SIL (Safety Integrity Level) and ASIL assignments in IEC 61508 and ISO 26262 are computed from **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** and **Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)** — both derived from $\lambda$ and the diagnostic coverage $DC$ of the safety function.

---

## 2.2 The Safety Integrity Level (SIL) and ASIL Taxonomy

### 2.2.1 SIL (IEC 61508 / IEC 61511)

| SIL | PFD (low-demand mode) | PFH (high-demand / continuous mode) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|-----|------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ | $10^{-6} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-5}$ | 10 – 100 |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ | $10^{-7} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-6}$ | 100 – 1000 |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ | $10^{-8} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-7}$ | 1000 – 10000 |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ | $10^{-9} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-8}$ | 10000 – 100000 |

The relationship between PFD and the **risk reduction** is:

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}}$$

The **average probability of failure on demand** for a 1oo2 (one-out-of-two) redundant system is approximately:

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot \lambda_D^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2 \cdot \frac{1}{3}$$

where $\lambda_D$ is the dangerous failure rate and $t_{CE}$ is the channel-equivalent mean downtime (proof test interval divided by 2).

### 2.2.2 ASIL (ISO 26262 — Automotive)

ASIL is determined by a **risk graph** combining three parameters:
- **S** — Severity (S0 none, S1 light/moderate, S2 severe, S3 life-threatening).
- **E** — Exposure (E0–E5: how often the operational situation occurs).
- **C** — Controllability (C0–C3: can the driver avoid harm?).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners often test the **risk graph** conceptually and the **PFD/PFH ranges** numerically. Memorize the SIL table — it appears in nearly every Module 3 question.

---

## 2.3 Security Levels (SL) — IEC 62443

The industrial security standard IEC 62443 defines **Security Level (SL)** as a *measure of confidence* that the system is free from threats, with five target levels:

- **SL 0** — No specific security requirement.
- **SL 1** — Casual / coincidental adversary.
- **SL 2** — Intentional adversary with simple means, low resources.
- **SL 3** — Intentional adversary with sophisticated means, moderate resources.
- **SL 4** — Intentional adversary with sophisticated means, extensive resources (state-level).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Mapping:** The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that **SL 3 ≈ SIL 3 in difficulty**, but they are *different axes* of risk. A SIL-3 system can be SL-0 (no security) and still be compliant with IEC 61508, but it is then *vulnerable to malicious fault injection* — this is the integration gap.

---

## 2.4 Risk Integration Mathematics

In a fully integrated safety-security analysis, the **total risk** is composed of two contributions:

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{safety}} + R_{\text{security}} - R_{\text{overlap}}$$

where:
- $R_{\text{safety}} = P_{\text{random}} \times C_{\text{hazard}}$
- $R_{\text{security}} = P_{\text{attack}} \times C_{\text{hazard}} \times \text{(Attacker Capability)}$
- $R_{\text{overlap}}$ accounts for events that have been double-counted (e.g., a security event that is also partially captured as a random fault).

In probabilistic form, for an *event tree* with both fault and attack paths, the **combined failure probability** of the safety function under both stressors is:

$$P_{\text{combined}} = 1 - (1 - P_{\text{safety-fail}}) \times (1 - P_{\text{security-breach}} \times P_{\text{compromise-safety}} )$$

If the security breach *causes* a safety failure (the dangerous case):

$$P_{\text{combined}} = P_{\text{safety-fail}} + (1 - P_{\text{safety-fail}}) \times P_{\text{security-breach}} \times P_{\text{compromise-safety}}$$

This is the standard **OR-gate fault tree** logic extended with an attack node.

---

## 2.5 Defense-in-Depth (DiD) — The Foundational Integration Strategy

**Defense-in-Depth** is the application of multiple, independent, layered security and safety controls such that no single failure (fault or attack) defeats the system.

The classical **5-layer DiD** for safety-critical CPS (adapted from IEC 62443 and NIST SP 800-82):

| Layer | Function | Examples |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **L1 — Physical** | Physical access control, tamper detection | Locked cabinets, seals, vibration sensors |
| **L2 — Network** | Segmentation, firewalls, intrusion detection | DMZ, VLANs, unidirectional gateways |
| **L3 — Host/Compute** | OS hardening, application whitelisting, code signing | CIS benchmarks, SELinux, TPM-based boot |
| **L4 — Application** | Authenticated commands, input validation, CRCs | Signed firmware, replay protection, watchdog timers |
| **L5 — Policy/Process** | Procedures, training, incident response | Patching policy, change management, audits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of DiD:** Each layer should be **independent and diverse** (different technology vendors, different teams, different physical media) so that a common-cause failure (e.g., shared compiler bug, shared supply-chain trojan) does not bypass all layers simultaneously.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU Module 3 high-yield. Reproduce the table in your exam-prep notebook.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Where Used |
|---|---------|---------|-------|------------|
| 1 | Reliability function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Hardware/Software reliability |
| 2 | Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | hours | Component-level assessment |
| 3 | MTTF for series of $n$ components | $\text{MTTF}_{\text{series}} = 1 \big/ \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i$ | hours | Reliability block diagrams |
| 4 | MTTF for 1oo2 parallel (active) | $\text{MTTF}_{1oo2} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$ | hours | Redundant systems |
| 5 | Probability of Failure on Demand (1oo1) | $\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \lambda_D \cdot t_{CE}$ | dimensionless | SIL assessment, low-demand |
| 6 | Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{RRF} = 1 / \text{PFD}$ | dimensionless | SIL ↔ risk map |
| 7 | Combined safety-security failure | $P_{\text{combined}} = 1 - (1-P_S)(1-P_A \cdot P_{C \mid A})$ | dimensionless | Integrated risk |
| 8 | IEC 62443 Security Level Target | $\text{SL-T} \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}$ | ordinal | Industrial CPS |
| 9 | Diagnostic coverage | $DC = \lambda_{DD}/(\lambda_{DD}+\lambda_{DU})$ | dimensionless | IEC 61508 architecture |
| 10 | Availability | $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ | dimensionless | Operational safety |
| 11 | IEC 61508 SIL ↔ PFH | See SIL table above | per hour | High-demand mode |
| 12 | Architectural constraint (Type B, SIL 3) | Hardware Fault Tolerance $\ge 1$, SFF $\ge 90\%$ | % | Route 1H architecture |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Style Tip:** When writing formulas in your answer script, **always state the units** and define each variable. Examiners award partial credit for correct variable definitions even if the numerical substitution is wrong.

---

## 2.7 Techniques for Integrating Safety and Security — The Master Catalogue

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **seven canonical integration techniques**. They are presented here in increasing order of organizational maturity.

### 2.7.1 Technique 1 — Unified Risk Assessment (CHASA / STPA-Sec)

**Combined Hazard and Security Analysis (CHASA)** unifies traditional HAZOP, FMEA, and attack-tree analyses into a single workshop where safety engineers and security engineers share the same system model.

The two flagship methods are:

- **STPA-Sec** (Systems-Theoretic Process Analysis with Security) — extends Leveson's STAMP/STPA by adding a *deliberate-loss* causal factor to the safety control structure.
- **CHASSIS** (Combined Harm Assessment of Safety and Security for Information Systems) — ISO/IEC TR 5469-aligned.

### 2.7.2 Technique 2 — Co-Engineering and Co-Development

Safety and security requirements are elicited **jointly**, not in series. The V-Model is extended so that on the left-descending side, both safety and security analyses are performed in parallel; on the right-ascending side, both safety and security verification are performed in parallel.

### 2.7.3 Technique 3 — Defense-in-Depth (DiD)

As covered in §2.5. Layered controls with diversity and independence.

### 2.7.4 Technique 4 — Common Criteria + Safety Augmentation

Use **ISO/IEC 15408 (Common Criteria)** for security evaluation, but **augment the security target** with safety-specific assurance components (e.g., EAL 5+ with *flaw remediation*, *vulnerability analysis*, and *safety-specific AVA_VAN* components).

### 2.7.5 Technique 5 — Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)

Use a **single system model** (SysML, AADL, EAST-ADL) to capture both safety and security annotations. The model supports automated consistency checking and is the basis for **digital twins** used in safety-security simulation.

### 2.7.6 Technique 6 — Standards-Aligned Co-Compliance

Map requirements from **IEC 61508 (safety)** and **IEC 62443 (security)** into a **single requirement matrix**; demonstrate that the most stringent requirement governs. This is the **state-of-practice** in industry today.

| Standard | Domain | Year | Role |
|----------|--------|------|------|
| IEC 61508 | Cross-industry E/E/PE | 2010 | Functional safety baseline |
| IEC 61511 | Process industry SIS | 2016 | Sector-specific functional safety |
| IEC 62443 | Industrial automation security | 2009–present | Security baseline |
| ISO 26262 | Automotive | 2018 | Functional safety for road vehicles |
| ISO/SAE 21434 | Automotive cybersecurity | 2021 | Security for road vehicles |
| IEC 62304 | Medical device software | 2015 | Medical software lifecycle |
| UL 2900 | Medical / industrial security | 2017 | Security evaluation |
| DO-178C | Avionics software | 2011 | Software for airborne systems |
| NIST SP 800-82 | Industrial control systems | Rev 3, 2023 | Security guide for OT |
| NIST CSF 2.0 | Critical infrastructure | 2024 | Cybersecurity framework |

### 2.7.7 Technique 7 — Resilient Design and Intrusion Tolerance

Design the system so that, *even if* a security breach occurs, the safety function is preserved. Key mechanisms:

- **Fail-secure / fail-safe convergence** — security breach triggers the same safe state as a random fault.
- **Graceful degradation** — reduce functionality (e.g., engine power limit) rather than a hard shutdown.
- **Self-healing / reconfiguration** — isolate the compromised subsystem, switch to a redundant path.
- **Moving target defense (MTD)** — randomize addresses, instruction sets, or communication schedules to raise attacker cost.
- **Intrusion-tolerant architectures** — Byzantine fault-tolerant controllers that reach consensus even when one node is maliciously compromised.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | SCS Example | Safety Standard | Security Standard | Integration Approach |
|--------|-------------|------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Aircraft** | Flight Control Computer | DO-178C DAL A | DO-326A / ED-202A | Formal methods + secure boot |
| **Automotive** | ADAS / Autonomous Stack | ISO 26262 ASIL D | ISO/SAE 21434 CAL 4 | Co-engineering with TARA + HARA |
| **Power Grid** | Substation IED | IEC 61850 + IEC 61508 SIL 4 | IEC 62351 + NERC CIP | Defense-in-depth + unidirectional gateways |
| **Medical Implant** | Pacemaker | IEC 62304 Class C | FDA MDR + UL 2900-1 | Signed firmware + radiated EM hardening |
| **Industrial Robot** | Welding arm | ISO 10218-1 Cat 3 | IEC 62443 SL 3 | Safe-rated networks + ZT segmentation |
| **Railway** | ETCS onboard computer | EN 50128 SIL 4 | IEC 62443 SL 3 | STPA-Sec + tamper-evident hardware |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the exhaustive mathematical derivations and analytical steps required for KTU Module 3 problem-solving. **Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability of a 1oo2 Redundant Channel

**Problem Context (typical KTU Part B):** A sensor channel has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. Two such channels are operated in 1oo2 (one-out-of-two voted) parallel active redundancy. Compute the reliability at $t = 10000$ hours.

### Step 1 — Identify the redundancy structure
A 1oo2 system delivers correct service **as long as at least one** of the two channels is working. The unreliability of each channel is:

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

### Step 2 — Compute unreliability of the 1oo2 system
The 1oo2 system fails only when **both** channels fail. Since the channels fail independently:

$$F_{1oo2}(t) = F_1(t) \times F_2(t) = \left(1 - e^{-\lambda t}\right)^2$$

### Step 3 — Compute the reliability of the 1oo2 system
$$R_{1oo2}(t) = 1 - F_{1oo2}(t) = 1 - \left(1 - e^{-\lambda t}\right)^2$$

Expand the square:
$$R_{1oo2}(t) = 1 - \left(1 - 2e^{-\lambda t} + e^{-2\lambda t}\right)$$

$$R_{1oo2}(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values
Given $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and $t = 10000$ hours:

$$\lambda t = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10000 = 0.05$$

$$e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.05} \approx 0.951229$$

$$e^{-2\lambda t} = e^{-0.10} \approx 0.904837$$

Therefore:
$$R_{1oo2}(10000) = 2 \times 0.951229 - 0.904837$$

$$R_{1oo2}(10000) = 1.902458 - 0.904837 = 0.997621$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
$$R_{1oo2}(10000) \approx 99.76\%$$

Compared with a single channel:
$$R_{1oo1}(10000) = 0.951229 \approx 95.12\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The 1oo2 redundancy improves reliability by 4.64 percentage points** at $t = 10000$ h, but at the cost of doubling the hardware and introducing a voter. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if you forget to state the *redundancy gain* in percentage terms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PFD of a 1oo2 Architecture (Low-Demand Mode)

**Problem Context (typical KTU Part B):** A safety instrumented function (SIF) is implemented in 1oo2 architecture. Each channel has a dangerous-failure rate $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof test interval is $T = 8760$ hours (1 year). Compute the PFD and identify the SIL achieved.

### Step 1 — State the IEC 61508 simplified formula for 1oo2 PFD
The simplified PFD formula for a 1oo2 architecture, assuming the dangerous failures are undetected until proof test and a common cause factor $\beta = 0.05$, is:

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_D^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + \beta \cdot \lambda_D \cdot t_{CE}$$

The first term captures *independent* dual failures; the second term captures *common-cause* failures (CCF) that defeat redundancy.

### Step 2 — Compute the channel-equivalent mean downtime
The channel-equivalent mean downtime is half the proof test interval (because on average, an undetected failure has occurred halfway through the interval):

$$t_{CE} = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{8760}{2} = 4380 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

Independent-failure term:
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{indep}} = 2 \times (1 - 0.05) \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (4380)^2 \times \frac{1}{3}$$

$$= 2 \times 0.95 \times 4 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.9184 \times 10^7 \times \frac{1}{3}$$

$$= 1.9 \times 4 \times 10^{-12} \times 6.3948 \times 10^6$$

$$= 1.9 \times 2.5579 \times 10^{-5} = 4.8600 \times 10^{-5}$$

Common-cause-failure term:
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}} = 0.05 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380$$

$$= 0.05 \times 8.76 \times 10^{-3} = 4.380 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 4 — Sum the terms
$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = 4.8600 \times 10^{-5} + 4.380 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = 4.8660 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to scientific notation and compare with SIL table
$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} \approx 4.87 \times 10^{-4}$$

From the SIL table in §2.2.1, the PFD range for **SIL 3** is $10^{-4} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The 1oo2 architecture with the stated parameters achieves **SIL 3**. To reach SIL 4, one must either reduce $T$, reduce $\lambda_D$ (better components), use 2oo3 voting, or add a third independent channel.

### Step 6 — Cross-check with Risk Reduction Factor
$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{4.87 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2053$$

This RRF of ~2053 falls within the SIL 3 range of $10^3$ to $10^4$, confirming the classification.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Safety-Security Risk Computation

**Problem Context (typical KTU Part B):** A CPS has a safety function that, if failed, would cause loss of life with a cost-of-consequence $C = 10^6$ units. Random hardware failure probability is $P_S = 10^{-4}$ per year. The system is exposed to a security attack that succeeds with probability $P_A = 10^{-2}$ per year. If the attack succeeds, the safety function is defeated with probability $P_{C \mid A} = 0.8$. Compute the total annual risk reduction required.

### Step 1 — Compute the safety-only risk
$$R_{\text{safety}} = P_S \times C = 10^{-4} \times 10^6 = 100 \text{ units/year}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the security-induced safety risk
The probability that an attack succeeds *and* defeats the safety function:
$$P_{\text{attack-induced}} = P_A \times P_{C \mid A} = 10^{-2} \times 0.8 = 8 \times 10^{-3}$$

The risk contribution:
$$R_{\text{security}} = P_{\text{attack-induced}} \times C = 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6 = 8000 \text{ units/year}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the combined failure probability of the safety function
$$P_{\text{combined}} = 1 - (1 - P_S)(1 - P_A \cdot P_{C \mid A})$$

$$= 1 - (1 - 10^{-4})(1 - 8 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$= 1 - (0.9999 \times 0.992)$$

$$= 1 - 0.99191 = 8.09 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 4 — Compute combined risk
$$R_{\text{combined}} = 8.09 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6 \approx 8090 \text{ units/year}$$

### Step 5 — Compare and interpret
| Risk Component | Value (units/year) | Percentage of Total |
|----------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Random safety failures | 100 | 1.24 % |
| Security-induced failures | 7990 | 98.76 % |
| **Total combined risk** | **8090** | **100 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The security-induced risk **dominates** the random safety risk by a factor of **80×**. The classical safety-only analysis would have required a PFD of $10^{-4}$ (SIL 3), but the integrated analysis shows the *effective* failure probability is $8.09 \times 10^{-3}$, which is actually a **SIL 1** risk reduction in the combined domain. The system needs to upgrade its security controls more urgently than its safety controls.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bathtub Curve Hazard Integration

**Problem Context:** For a 1oo1 system with constant failure rate $\lambda = 10^{-5}$ per hour, compute:
(a) MTTF
(b) Reliability at 8760 hours
(c) Availability assuming MTTR = 8 hours
(d) The number of failures expected in 10 years (87600 hours)

### Step (a) — MTTF
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{10^{-5}} = 10^5 \text{ hours} = 100000 \text{ hours}$$

### Step (b) — Reliability at 8760 hours
$$R(8760) = e^{-10^{-5} \times 8760} = e^{-0.0876} \approx 0.9161$$

So **91.61 %** probability of no failure in 1 year.

### Step (c) — Availability
$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 100000 + 8 = 100008 \text{ hours}$$

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{100008}{100008 + 8} = \frac{100008}{100016} \approx 0.99992$$

Availability is **99.992 %** (the famous *"four nines"* benchmark).

### Step (d) — Expected failures in 10 years
$$N_{\text{failures}} = \frac{87600}{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{87600}{100000} = 0.876$$

**Less than one failure** in 10 years. Such components are considered suitable for high-reliability SCS.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing an Integration Technique

The following decision matrix maps CPS characteristics to recommended integration techniques. This is a **high-yield KTU Part-B (14-mark) answer framework**.

| CPS Characteristic | Recommended Integration Techniques | Reasoning |
|--------------------|-------------------------------------|-----------|
| Long lifecycle (10–30 yr), fixed-function (e.g., nuclear, railway) | Defense-in-Depth + Standards-Aligned Co-Compliance (IEC 61508 + IEC 62443) | Stability, regulatory acceptance |
| Short lifecycle, high connectivity (e.g., consumer IoT, medical wearable) | Resilient Design + Common Criteria | Patching agility over depth |
| Mixed-criticality (e.g., automotive ADAS + infotainment) | Co-Engineering + MBSE | Hardware isolation, ISO 26262 + 21434 |
| Adversarial threat model (e.g., SCADA, military) | Unified Risk Assessment (STPA-Sec) + Defense-in-Depth | Threat-centric, formal |
| Resource-constrained edge (e.g., IIoT sensor) | Defense-in-Depth (lightweight) + Common Criteria (low EAL) | Memory/compute ceiling |
| AI/ML-enabled control (e.g., autonomous vehicles) | Resilient Design + STPA-Sec | Non-deterministic, novel attack surface |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, do NOT simply list techniques. The KTU examiner awards marks for **(a) justification, (b) cross-reference to standard, (c) trade-off analysis, and (d) architectural choice**. Use the matrix above as a *structure* for your answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section contains professional-grade **Mermaid block diagrams** illustrating the safety-security integration framework.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Master Integration Framework

The following diagram captures the **complete integration workflow** from a CPS's natural-language concept of operation (CONOPS) through analysis, design, and verification.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ConOps and Mission Profile] --> B[Unified Stakeholder Needs]
    B --> C[Hazard Analysis HAZOP FMEA]
    B --> D[Threat Modeling STRIDE Attack Trees]
    C --> E[Unified Risk Register CHASA]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Risk Classification Matrix]
    F --> G[SIL Target Selection IEC 61508]
    F --> H[SL Target Selection IEC 62443]
    G --> I[Integrated Architecture Design]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Defense in Depth 5 Layers]
    I --> K[STPA Sec Control Structure]
    I --> L[Resilience Mechanisms MTD BFT]
    J --> M[Implementation and V Model]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Safety Verification FMEA FTA]
    M --> O[Security Verification Pen Test Fuzzing]
    N --> P[Integrated Certification Package]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Continuous Monitoring and Patching]
    Q --> B
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Defense-in-Depth Layered Architecture

A 5-layer **Defense-in-Depth** stack for a safety-critical CPS such as a substation IED (Intelligent Electronic Device).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 Physical Boundary]
        L1a[Tamper Switches]
        L1b[Conductive Seals]
        L1c[Physical Locks]
    end
    subgraph L2[Layer 2 Network Boundary]
        L2a[Unidirectional Gateway]
        L2b[Industrial DMZ]
        L2c[Network IDS]
    end
    subgraph L3[Layer 3 Host Platform]
        L3a[OS Hardening CIS]
        L3b[Secure Boot TPM]
        L3c[Application Whitelisting]
    end
    subgraph L4[Layer 4 Application Logic]
        L4a[Signed Firmware]
        L4b[Authenticated Commands]
        L4c[Input Validation]
    end
    subgraph L5[Layer 5 Process and Policy]
        L5a[Change Management]
        L5b[Incident Response]
        L5c[Security Training]
    end
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — V-Model with Safety and Security Overlay

The classical V-Model is extended so that safety and security are performed in **parallel** on both the descending (specification) and ascending (verification) sides.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DESC[Descending Side Decomposition]
        A1[Concept of Operations] --> A2[System Requirements]
        A2 --> A3[Subsystem Requirements]
        A3 --> A4[Design Specification]
        A4 --> A5[Implementation]
    end
    subgraph ASC[Ascending Side Verification]
        A5 --> V5[Unit Testing]
        V5 --> V4[Integration Testing]
        V4 --> V3[Subsystem Verification]
        V3 --> V2[System Verification]
        V2 --> V1[Acceptance Testing]
    end
    A1 -.Safety Hazard Analysis.-> V1
    A1 -.Security Threat Analysis.-> V1
    A2 -.Safety Requirements.-> V2
    A2 -.Security Requirements.-> V2
    A3 -.Safety Design.-> V3
    A3 -.Security Design.-> V3
    A4 -.Safety Code Review.-> V4
    A4 -.Security Code Review.-> V4
    A5 -.Safety Unit Tests.-> V5
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Combined Fault + Attack Event Tree

The following diagram is the **extended event tree** where a top event (loss of safety function) is reached by either random hardware failure *or* malicious attack.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[Loss of Safety Function Catastrophic Harm] --> OR1
    OR1{OR Gateway}
    OR1 --> P1[Random Hardware Fault]
    OR1 --> P2[Malicious Cyber Attack]
    P1 --> P1a[Sensor Failure]
    P1 --> P1b[Actuator Failure]
    P1 --> P1c[Controller Failure]
    P2 --> P2a[Authentication Bypass]
    P2 --> P2b[Firmware Tampering]
    P2 --> P2c[Replay Attack on Commands]
    P2a --> P2a1[Forged Identity Accepted]
    P2b --> P2b1[Malicious Code in SIS]
    P2c --> P2c1[Old Valid Command Replayed]
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — STPA-Sec Causal Factor Extension

In **STAMP/STPA**, the original analysis identifies *unsafe control actions (UCAs)* and their causal factors. **STPA-Sec** adds a *deliberate-loss* causal factor that is associated with a malicious actor.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CTRL[Controller Output] --> ACT[Actuator]
    ACT --> PHYS[Physical Process State]
    PHYS --> MEA[Measured Variable]
    MEA --> FB[Feedback Path]
    FB --> CTRL
    CTRL -.UCA Not Provided.-> H1[Hazard 1]
    CTRL -.UCA Provided Too Late.-> H2[Hazard 2]
    CTRL -.UCA Provided Too Early.-> H3[Hazard 3]
    CTRL -.UCA Stopped Too Soon.-> H4[Hazard 4]
    H1 --> CF1[Random Sensor Fault]
    H1 --> CF2[Component Wear Out]
    H1 --> CF3[Deliberate Loss via Attack]
    H2 --> CF1
    H2 --> CF3
    H3 --> CF3
    H4 --> CF2
    H4 --> CF3
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Risk Reduction Loop (Continuous Integration)

The *continuous risk reduction loop* is the operational governance model that ensures safety and security are not "designed-in once" but continuously re-validated against a changing threat and fault landscape.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Assets and Processes] --> B[Analyze Hazards and Threats]
    B --> C[Assess Combined Risk]
    C --> D[Apply Controls Defense in Depth]
    D --> E[Monitor and Detect Anomalies]
    E --> F[Respond and Recover]
    F --> G[Feedback to Hazard and Threat DB]
    G --> A
```
